column3: The contrary opinion, however, as it is more common, so also is it more true: namely, that the ark, the tabernacle, and the altar of incense were not found together with the sacred fire under Nehemiah and Darius, but are to be revealed at the end of the world, when God through Elijah and Enoch will convert His people, that is, the Jews, to Christ, and will gather them together with the Gentiles into one Church. For then Elijah, in order to confirm them in the faith of Christ, will show them the ark promised by Jeremiah for that age. This is proved first, because if these things had been found along with the fire, the author here would certainly have narrated that same fact here, just as he narrated concerning the fire; for this would have pertained plainly to the present matter. But now he narrates nothing of the sort here. Second, because under Nehemiah no complete

Many Jews indeed remained in Babylon; others were dispersed into various regions. Thirdly, if the ark had been found, at the time of the spoliation of the second temple which was carried out by Titus, it would have been placed among the spoils. But it is not placed [among them] according to Jose[phus].

Josephus relates what Pompey saw when he entered the Holy of Holies, enumerating that there was absolutely nothing of the sacred things in the Holy of Holies. When Pompey entered into that same Holy of Holies, Josephus recounts what he saw in the temple.
Judas Maccabaeus, with about six thousand men, called upon the Lord to look upon the people who were trodden down by all, and to have pity on the temple that had been profaned by ungodly men.

The ark of the covenant was hidden by Jeremiah, as noted above; it was never restored to the second temple. This is confirmed four ways: first, from 2 Maccabees 2; second, from Josephus; third, because on the triumphal arch of Titus in Rome we see depicted the golden candlestick and the table of showbread, but no ark is depicted; fourth, because this is the common opinion of the Hebrews, who constantly assert that these vessels were absent from the second temple. The same is asserted by Latin and Greek writers, as St. Epiphanius and Dorotheus, already cited in the life of Jeremiah, St. Thomas, Lyranus, and others.

Some add that the Hebrews, when the ark, the tabernacle, and the altar of incense made by Moses were lacking, fabricated similar ones and placed them in the second temple (for the temple required this adornment as proper to itself); and this is sufficiently gathered from 1 Macc. 1:23, where it is said that Antiochus Epiphanes took from the temple the golden altar and the lampstand of light, and the table of the showbread.

Nebr., Chronol. anno mundi 3730, Bellarmine de verbo Dei c. 15, Abulensis on Exod. c. 15, q. 1, assert that these vessels were hidden in the same place where the body of Moses was laid, and that they will appear when the body of Moses rises again and appears, which will be at the end of the world. So also Dorotheus in the Life of Jeremiah says that he hid the ark in the tomb of Moses and Aaron. Cosmas Magallanes, preface to Joshua, section 6, holds the same view.

The argument of the second book was the same as that of the first, namely the deeds of Judas Maccabaeus and his brothers: for these things, written briefly in the first book, are narrated more fully here; and many things are also added which were omitted in the first book.

[Antiochus the Noble] that is, Antiochus Epiphanes: for ἐπιφανής in Greek means the same as illustrious, noble; he was so called by the Gentiles on account of his illustrious deeds, although more truly he was surnamed ἐπιμανής by Polybius and others, that is, mad and insane, as I said in book 1.

[AND OF THE ILLUMINATIONS WHICH WERE MADE FROM HEAVEN FOR THOSE WHO FOUGHT BRAVELY FOR THE JEWS.] By illuminations from heaven he means the signs sent from heaven on behalf of Judas and his companions, who, aided by the help of Angels and fighting bravely for the Jews, overthrew the great armies of Antiochus. Hence Angels appeared to Judas, and went before his camp as leaders of war, as we shall hear in what follows.

[THE LORD HAVING BEEN MADE PROPITIOUS TO THEM WITH ALL TRANQUILITY,] as if to say: the Lord calming and settling the disturbances, tumults and battles launched against the Jews by Antiochus, and restoring to them their former peace. For the tranquil God tranquilizes all things, and to look upon tranquilly is to rest, as one of the Saints says.

[And likewise what was contained by Jason of Cyrene in five books, we have attempted to abridge into one volume,] that is, to reduce into this brief volume. Calvin and the Innovators object that this Jason was a Cyrenean, and therefore a Gentile, and accordingly this book, which is an abridgment of Jason, is not sacred and canonical. The reply is that this Jason was a Jew, not a Gentile: for Jews were scattered throughout the whole world, and therefore dwelt also in Cyrene, as is clear from Acts 2 and 6. For Gentiles were enemies of the Jews, and mocked, belittled, and distorted their law, rites, and deeds. But this Jason extols and celebrates all these things, as is clear from what follows. Therefore he was himself a true worshipper of God, an upright and pious man, and a zealot for the worship of God.

But even granting that Jason had been a Gentile, Judas nonetheless, who selected, arranged, ordered, composed, and made his own the writings of Jason, and reduced them into this book, with the Holy Spirit always assisting him lest he err anywhere, made this book canonical. Wherefore he accomplished the same effect as if whatever was false, impious, or obscene in the books of Jason, Judas would omit or correct. Note here the canon: When a canonical author cites and approves the sayings of some profane author, by this very act he makes them canonical. Thus St. Paul in Titus 1:12 cites that saying of Epimenides: "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons"; and adding: "This testimony is true," by this very act he attributes to it canonical and infallible authority. Similarly that saying of Job 5:13: "He catches the wise in their craftiness," as it was said by Epiphar the Gentile, does not have canonical authority; yet it acquired it when cited and praised by Paul in 1 Cor. 3:19. In like manner Judas, this canonical author, by citing and praising the words of Jason, made them canonical. So again Paul in Acts 17:28, citing and approving those words of Aratus: "For we are indeed his offspring," attributes to them canonical authority. So Sanchez, Serarius, Bellarmine and others. # GEM

[We have undertaken a task full of watchfulness and toil.] For it is a work of great labor and industry to read very many things, and to compress and comprise all of them methodically, elegantly, clearly, and fully in few words. Thus labored he who transcribed the Iliad of Homer in such minute letters that he enclosed the whole of it within the shell of a nut, as Pliny testifies in book 7, chapter 21; and Archimedes, who, as Cicero says in the Tusculans, bound the motions of the moon, sun, and five wandering stars into a sphere, and that with divine genius. And because few know how to do this, hence compilations are often deficient. To embrace many things in few words is therefore the mark of a great man and of a profound intellect, which perceives and grasps the point of manifold truth in a single glance of the mind. This Laconism, that is, brevity of speech, is the sign of a silent, meditative, and wise mind, and therefore it was used by the seven sages of Greece, whose sayings are these: "Know thyself," "Nothing in excess," "Be surety, harm is at hand," etc. Hear Plutarch in his treatise On Garrulity: "The praises of silence and taciturnity must always be heard, and their majesty, sanctity, and likeness to sacred mysteries, and how those who are more admired, more beloved, and accounted wiser, are those who are restrained and brief in speech, and in whose brief utterance there is great meaning. For Plato also praises these men, teaching that they are like skilled javelin-throwers, when they release words that are crisp, dense, and intertwined with one another. And Lycurgus, restraining his citizens from boyhood in silence, trained them in this skill of speaking concisely and compactly. For just as the Celtiberians strengthen and solidify iron by burying it in the earth, purging away the thick and earthy parts, so Laconic speech was stripped of its bark, and sharpened into effectiveness by the removal of superfluities: for that skillful brevity of speaking, and versatile sharpness in replies, was the fruit of much silence."

[CONCEDING THE TRUTH INDEED TO THE INDIVIDUAL AUTHORS (in Greek: to the author, namely Jason of Cyrene), BUT OURSELVES ACCORDING TO THE GIVEN (already stated) FORM STRIVING FOR BREVITY,] as if to say: I, Judas, the writer of this book, did only this: to abridge the writings of Jason; wherefore I did not wish to scrutinize carefully the truth of individual matters, even the most minute, as written by him, but I attribute the praise of this truthfulness to Jason, as a writer who is upright, truthful, and exact, who first diligently investigated all these things and wrote down only what was certainly established. Thus he thinks modestly of himself, and

SICUT ENIM NOVAE DOMUS ARCHITECTO.] From the same modesty, Judas here compares Jason to the architect of a great building, upon whom falls the arrangement and construction of the entire structure; but himself to the painter who adorns an already completed building with his painting, as if to say: it belongs to the historian to construct the framework of history; but it belongs to the abbreviator to adorn it with his own brief and elegant style.
ETENDI INTELLECTUM COLLIGERE.] He calls 'intellectum' the argument, or rather the subject matter which the historian gathers together and sets before the reader to be read and understood.

EXSECUTIONES RERUM VITARE HI BREVIANT CONCEDENDUM EST.] In Greek: to omit curiosity of detail must be conceded to one making an abbreviation. As if to say: it belongs to the historical author to investigate and narrate each individual detail with care; but it belongs to the abbreviator to cut away many of these things and to encompass much in few words. For in Greek ἐξεργασίαν signifies work done with great diligence and care, that is, what is treated with curiosity, exactness, fullness and completeness. And this our author calls 'exsecutiones': nor should you consider this word un-Latin. For 'exsequi' means to complete and perfect something, and therefore to do it carefully; whence that saying of Tully in the Orator: 'He follows through all things divinely.' And that of the same in De Universitate: 'There is therefore no doubt that he preferred to follow through eternity, since nothing is more beautiful than the world, nor more excellent than its builder.' And that of the same in De Senectute: 'To follow through all the duties and functions of a king.' Again, and more to the point at hand, 'exsequi' means to expound a matter in minute detail and at length, and to pursue it in narration. Thus Livy, book 5: 'He then followed through what the customary derivation was.' And Cicero in Marius, book 1.27: 'I have,' he says, 'many more obscure matters which I can scarcely follow through in words.' And Pliny, book 35, chapter 8: 'Now I shall run through the celebrated matters with the greatest brevity; for such a following-through, that is, a more extended and fuller narration, is not in keeping with the plan of the work.' Thus here 'exsecutiones' are opposed to the abbreviation which our Author here proposed to himself to follow.

[Chapter 3 Synopsis] The prosperous state of Jerusalem is reviewed under the high priest Onias, which the contentiousness and treachery of Simon the temple prefect disturbed: for he himself betrayed the temple treasures to King Seleucus, who sent Heliodorus to plunder them; but he was sharply beaten by angels, and restored to health by the prayers of Onias, and began to celebrate everywhere the power of God and the sanctity of the temple.

IGITUR CUM SANCTA CIVITAS HABITARETUR IN OMNI PACE.] Therefore when the holy city was inhabited in complete peace, and the laws were still being kept in the best manner, on account of the piety of the high priest Onias, and minds having hatred of evil.

FIEBAT UT ET IPSI REGES ET PRINCIPES LOCUM SUMMO HONORE DIGNUM DUCERENT.] It came about that even the kings and princes themselves considered the place worthy of the highest honor, and adorned the temple with the greatest gifts.

ITA UT SELEUCUS ASIAE REX DE REDDITIBUS SUIS PRAESTARET OMNES SUMPTUS AD MINISTERIUM SACRIFICIORUM PERTINENTES.] So that Seleucus king of Asia provided from his own revenues all expenses pertaining to the ministry of sacrifices.

SIMON AUTEM DE TRIBU BENJAMIN PRAEPOSITUS TEMPLI CONSTITUTUS CONTENDEBAT.] Simon of the tribe of Benjamin, appointed prefect of the temple, was striving, with the high priest opposing him, to bring about something wicked in the city.
SED CUM VINCERE ONIAM NON POSSET VENIT AD APOLLONIUM.] But when he could not overcome Onias, he went to Apollonius the son of Tharseas, who at that time was commander of Coelesyria and Phoenicia.

ET NUNTIAVIT EI PECUNIIS INNUMERABILIBUS PLENUM ESSE SERARIUM JEROSOLYMIS.] And he reported to him that the treasury at Jerusalem was full of countless sums of money, and that the common stores were immense, which did not pertain to the account of the sacrifices; and that it was possible for all of it to fall under the power of the king.

CUMQUE RETULISSET AD REGEM APOLLONIUS DE PECUNIIS.] And when Apollonius had reported to the king concerning the money that had been revealed, the king summoned Heliodorus, who was over his affairs, and sent him with instructions to transport the aforesaid money.

STATIMQUE HELIODORUS ITER EST AGGRESSUS.] And immediately Heliodorus set out on his journey, under the pretense indeed of touring the cities of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, but in reality to carry out the king's purpose.

SED CUM VENISSET JEROSOLYMAM ET BENIGNE A SUMMO SACERDOTE IN CIVITATE ESSET EXCEPTUS.] But when he had come to Jerusalem and had been kindly received by the high priest in the city, he reported concerning the information given about the money, and revealed for what reason he was present; and he asked whether these things were really so.
TUNC SUMMUS SACERDOS OSTENDIT DEPOSITA ESSE HAEC ET VICTUALIA VIDUARUM ET PUPILLORUM.] Then the high priest showed that these were deposits, and provisions for widows and orphans.

QUAEDAM VERO ESSE HIRCANI TOBIAE VIRI VALDE EMINENTIS.] But that some belonged to Hyrcanus son of Tobias, a man of very great distinction, in what the impious Simon had reported; and that the whole amounted to four hundred talents of silver and two hundred of gold.

DECIPI VERO EOS QUI CREDIDISSENT LOCO ET TEMPLO.] But that those who had trusted in the place and the temple, which is honored throughout the whole world, would be deceived, since on account of its reverence and sanctity it was altogether impossible.

AT ILLE PRO HIS QUAE HABEBAT IN MANDATIS A REGE DICEBAT OMNI GENERE REGI EA ESSE DEFERENDA.] But he, on account of those things which he had in instructions from the king, said that by every means these things were to be brought to the king.

CONSTITUTA AUTEM DIE INTRABAT DE HIS HELIODORUS ORDINATURUS.] And when the appointed day had come, Heliodorus entered to make his arrangements concerning these matters. And there was no small fear throughout the whole city.

SACERDOTES AUTEM ANTE ALTARE CUM STOLIS SACERDOTALIBUS JACTAVERUNT SE.] But the priests cast themselves before the altar in their priestly vestments, and called upon from heaven Him who had made the law concerning deposits, that He would keep them safe for those who had deposited them. # GEM

JAM VERO QUI VIDEBAT SUMMI SACERDOTIS VULTUM MENTE VULNERABATUR.] Now indeed whoever looked upon the face of the high priest was wounded in his mind: for his changed face and color declared the inner grief of his soul.

CIRCUMFUSA ENIM ERAT MOESTITIA QUAEDAM VIRO ET HORROR CORPORIS.] For a certain sadness was spread over the man, and a trembling of the body, through which the grief of his heart was made manifest to those who looked upon him.
ALII ETIAM GREGATIM DE DOMIBUS CONFLUEBANT.] Others also came together in crowds from their houses, making public supplication, because the place was about to come into contempt.

ACCINCTAEQUE MULIERES CILICIIS PECTUS PER PLATEAS CONFLUEBANT.] And women girded with sackcloth on their breasts came together through the streets; and even virgins who had been shut in ran forward, some to Onias, others to the walls, and some looked out through the windows.
UNIVERSAE AUTEM PROTENDENTES MANUS IN COELUM DEPRECABANTUR.] And all stretching forth their hands to heaven made supplication.
ERAT ENIM MISERA COMMISTAE MULTITUDINIS ET MAGNI SACERDOTIS IN AGONE CONSTITUTI EXSPECTATIO.] For pitiable was the expectation of the mingled multitude and of the high priest placed in agony.

ET HI QUIDEM INVOCABANT OMNIPOTENTEM DEUM UT CREDITA SIBI HIS QUI CREDIDERANT CUM OMNI INTEGRITATE CONSERVARENTUR.] And these indeed called upon almighty God, that the things entrusted to Him might be preserved with complete integrity for those who had entrusted them.

HELIODORUS AUTEM QUOD DECREVERAT PERFICIEBAT EODEM LOCO IPSE CUM SATELLITIBUS CIRCA SERARIUM PRAESENS.] But Heliodorus was carrying out what he had decreed, himself present in that same place with his attendants around the treasury.

SED SPIRITUS OMNIPOTENTIS DEI MAGNAM FECIT SUAE OSTENSIONIS EVIDENTIAM.] But the spirit of almighty God made great and evident his manifestation, so that all who had dared to obey him, falling by the power of God, were turned to dissolution and terror.
APPARUIT ENIM ILLIS QUIDAM EQUUS.] For there appeared to them a certain horse.

Apparuit enim illis quidam equus — This was an Angel who assumed the form of a horse and rider, so as to oppose Heliodorus as a horseman and, as it were, prostrate him in single combat. "Smote Heliodorus with his forefeet" (Greek: concussit in Heliodorum priores ungulas): for horses fight by kicking. The Greek reads: "rushing forward with great impetus he struck Heliodorus with his front hooves," which serve as armed hands. Thus the Angel's horse struck Heliodorus, cast him down, afflicted him, and nearly killed him. In like manner angels at war appeared to Zechariah (ch. 1) in the form of horses and horsemen, and to St. John (Apoc. 5:2). Livy (Decade 1) writes similarly of Castor and Pollux fighting on white horses for the Romans — things much embellished by the Gentiles.

Moreover this was not an imaginary vision appearing to Heliodorus alone, but a real action — the prostration and scourging of Heliodorus before the entire people. For the text says "appeared" (Greek: ὤφθη, i.e., "was seen") to them — namely to all who were present. Hence what follows:

Alii etiam apparuerunt duo juvenes — Two other Angels appeared, bearing the beauty of virtue and heavenly character. They stood around Heliodorus and scourged him from both sides without ceasing, with many blows (holding whips or scourges in their hands, as Gorionides says). The first angelic horseman was as a praetor; these two Angels were as his apparitors and executors of sentence, who therefore scourged the sacrilegious Heliodorus most severely. # GEM Hence it is clear that God punishes sins and sinners not always through demons but also sometimes through good Angels.

Josephus son of Gorion (bk. 3, ch. 1) adds: "Heliodorus entered with all his satellites, walking through the midst of the sanctuary and entering into the Holy of Holies; the Lord caused him and those with him to hear voices of uproar and most violent commotion that broke mountains and split rocks, so that all his satellites began to flee and hide wherever they could, and Heliodorus was left alone; when he lifted his eyes he saw a man of most terrible aspect clothed in a golden garment from which precious gems flashed, advancing and blocking him, leaping and running against Heliodorus," etc.

If God thus punished Heliodorus for merely attempting to remove deposits from the Temple, how will He punish those who plunder the goods of temples, monasteries and holy places and convert them to profane and secular uses? Let them consider how God punished Antiochus Epiphanes for despoiling the Temple; let them read his horrible death recounted in ch. 9. Let them also remember the vengeance inflicted by God on the sacrilegious Alcimus (bk. 1, ch. 9, v. 55). Indeed all histories are full of such examples.

Furthermore this scourging by Angels — who are most powerful and avenge injuries to God and His Temple most keenly — was far more severe and harsh than any scourging by men; especially because they wished to make of Heliodorus an example of sharp divine vengeance upon the sacrilegious, to deter others from similar plundering of the Temple. Hence when asked by King Seleucus, he said: "If you have an enemy, send him there, and you will receive him back scourged."
Obtulit pro salute viri hostiam salutarem — That is, a peace-offering (victima pacifica), which was offered by law for the peace and health of any person, according to the law of Leviticus 3.

Et omni gratias agens — For this the Angels themselves had commanded, v. 33. Gorionides adds that at the Angels' prompting Heliodorus fell at the feet of Onias and thus gave thanks in supplication. For he was moved to this by the health received and the life given through Onias — indeed Heliodorus owed his very life to Onias.
Testabatur autem omnibus — From this it appears that Heliodorus acknowledged the true God of Israel and gave testimony to all.
Simon, the aforesaid informer against the money and his country, spoke ill of Onias, as though he himself had incited Heliodorus to these things, and had himself been the instigator of evils.
And he dared to call a provisor of the city, and a defender of his nation, and a zealot of the law of God, a plotter against the kingdom.
But when the enmities had proceeded so far that even homicides were committed by certain associates of Simon,
Onias, considering the danger of the contention, and that Apollonius was raging, as being governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, to augment the malice of Simon, betook himself to the king,
not as an accuser of the citizens, but considering in himself the common utility of all the multitude.
For he saw that without the king's providence it was impossible that peace should be given to affairs, nor that Simon could cease from his folly.
But after the decease of Seleucus, when Antiochus, who was called Nobilis, had taken the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias ambitiously sought the high priesthood.
Having approached the king, he promised him three hundred and sixty talents of silver, and out of other revenues eighty talents.

Besides this, he promised also a hundred and fifty more, if power were granted him to set up a place of exercise and a school for youth, and to enrol those who were at Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch.
Which when the king had granted, and he had obtained the chief rule, he immediately began to turn his countrymen to the manner of the Gentiles.

And abolishing those things which had been established by the kings for the benefit of the Jews, through John the father of Eupolemus, who performed a lawful embassy to the Romans for friendship and alliance, he annulled the lawful privileges of the citizens and introduced practices contrary to the law.
For he ventured to establish a place of exercise under the very citadel, and to put the most vigorous of the youths in brothels.
Now this was not the beginning, but a certain increase and progress of heathen and foreign manner of life, because of the wickedness of the impious and not high

Simon, who had informed against the temple treasury and the people, made false accusations against Onias, as if he had been the one who stirred up trouble and incited the plot against Heliodorus, and as the contriver of evils.
And he dared to call the benefactor of the city, the protector of his countrymen, and the zealot for the laws, a traitor against the state.
But when the enmity had reached such a point that murders were being committed through one of Simon's trusted associates,
Onias recognized the danger of the contention and that Apollonius, son of Menestheus, the governor of Coele-Syria and Phoenicia, was intensifying the malice of Simon,
he betook himself to the king, not to be an accuser of his fellow citizens, but looking to the good of all the people, both public and private.
For he saw that without royal providence it was impossible for the state to be settled in peace, and that Simon would not cease from his folly.
But after Seleucus died and Antiochus, called Epiphanes, succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means,
promising the king in a petition three hundred and sixty talents of silver and from another revenue eighty talents.
Beyond this he promised to pay one hundred and fifty more if it were granted him authority to establish a gymnasium and a youth corps for it, and to register the people of Jerusalem as Antiochenes.
When the king had assented and Jason had come to power, he immediately shifted his countrymen over to the Greek way of life.

And setting aside the royal concessions to the Jews, secured through John the father of Eupolemus, who went on the mission to establish friendship and alliance with the Romans, he abolished the lawful ways of living and introduced customs contrary to the law.
For he readily established a gymnasium right under the citadel itself, and he led the noblest of the young men under subjection to the broad-brimmed hat.
There was such an extreme of Hellenization and increase in the adoption of foreign ways because of the surpassing wickedness of Jason, who was ungodly and no true high priest,

that the priests were no longer intent upon their services at the altar. Despising the sanctuary and neglecting the sacrifices, they hastened to take part in the unlawful proceedings in the wrestling arena after the call to the discus,
disdaining the honors prized by their fathers and putting the highest value upon Greek forms of prestige.
For this reason heavy disaster overtook them, and those whose ways of living they admired and wished to imitate completely became their enemies and punished them.

For it is no light thing to show irreverence to the divine laws — a fact that later events will make plain. # GEM Here Lapide notes that divine justice, though it may seem to delay, never fails to exact condign punishment for contempt of sacred law; this is a universal axiom vindicated throughout sacred history.
When the quinquennial games were being held at Tyre and the king was present,

the vile Jason sent envoys, chosen as being Antiochene citizens from Jerusalem, to carry three hundred silver drachmas for the sacrifice to Hercules. Those who carried it, however, thought it not proper to use it for sacrificial purposes, since that was not fitting, but to use it for some other expenditure.
So this money was intended by the sender for the sacrifice to Hercules, but on account of those who carried it, it was applied to the construction of triremes.

When Apollonius son of Menestheus was sent to Egypt for the coronation of Philometor as king, Antiochus learned that Philometor had become hostile to his government, so he took measures for his own security. He went to Joppa and then proceeded to Jerusalem.
He was welcomed magnificently by Jason and the city, and was ushered in with a blaze of torches and with shouts. Then he marched his army into Phoenicia.
After a period of three years Jason sent Menelaus, the brother of the aforementioned Simon, to bring money to the king and to carry through some essential business.
But he, when presented to the king, extolled him with an air of authority, and secured the high priesthood for himself, outbidding Jason by three hundred talents of silver.
After receiving the king's orders he returned, possessing no qualification for the high priesthood, but having the hot temper of a cruel tyrant and the rage of a savage beast.
So Jason, who after supplanting his own brother was supplanted by another man, was driven as a fugitive into the land of Ammon.
Although Menelaus continued to hold the office, he did not pay regularly any of the money promised to the king.
When Sostratus the captain of the citadel kept requesting payment — for the collection of the revenue was his responsibility — the two of them were summoned by the king on account of this matter.
Menelaus left his brother Lysimachus as his deputy in the high priesthood, while Sostratus left Crates, the commander of the Cyprians.
While such was the state of affairs, it happened that the people of Tarsus and of Mallus revolted because their cities had been given as a present to Antiochis, the king's concubine.
So the king went in haste to settle the trouble, leaving Andronicus, a man of high rank, to act as his deputy.

But Menelaus, thinking he had obtained a suitable opportunity, stole some of the gold vessels of the temple and gave them to Andronicus; other vessels he had already sold to Tyre and to the neighboring cities.
When Onias became fully aware of these acts, he publicly exposed them, having withdrawn to a place of sanctuary at Daphne near Antioch.

Therefore Menelaus, taking Andronicus aside, urged him to kill Onias. Andronicus came to Onias, and resorting to treachery offered him sworn pledges and gave him his right hand; he persuaded him, though still suspicious, to come out from the place of sanctuary; then, with no regard for justice, he immediately put him out of the way. # GEM Lapide observes here that Andronicus violated the most sacred law of asylum and the sanctity of an oath simultaneously — a double sacrilege that cried to heaven for vengeance, as the sequel demonstrates.
For this reason not only Jews, but many also of other nations, were grieved and displeased at the unjust murder of the man.
When the king returned from the region of Cilicia, the Jews in the city appealed to him with regard to the unreasonable murder of Onias, and the Greeks shared their hatred of the crime.
Therefore Antiochus was grieved at heart and filled with pity, and wept because of the sober and modest life of the deceased.

And being inflamed with anger, he immediately stripped off the purple robe from Andronicus, tore off his garments, and led him through the whole city to that very place where he had committed the outrage against Onias, and there he dispatched the bloodthirsty fellow. The Lord thus repaid him with the punishment he deserved.

When many acts of sacrilege had been committed in the city by Lysimachus with the connivance of Menelaus, and when report of them had spread abroad, the populace gathered against Lysimachus, because many of the gold vessels had already been stolen.

And since the crowds were becoming aroused and filled with anger, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men and launched an unjust attack, under the leadership of a certain Auranus, a man advanced in years and no less advanced in folly.

But when the Jews became aware of Lysimachus's attack, some picked up stones, some blocks of wood, and others took handfuls of the ashes that were lying about, and threw them in wild confusion at Lysimachus and his men.
As a result, they wounded many of them, and killed some, and put all the rest to flight; the temple robber himself they killed close by the treasury.
Charges were brought against Menelaus about this incident.
When the king came to Tyre, three men sent by the senate pleaded the cause before him.
But Menelaus, already as good as beaten, promised a substantial bribe to Ptolemy son of Dorymenes to win over the king.
Therefore Ptolemy, taking the king aside into a colonnade as if to get a breath of fresh air, induced him to change his mind.

Menelaus, the cause of all the evil, he acquitted of the charges against him, while he sentenced to death those unfortunate men, who would have been freed uncondemned even if they had pleaded their case even before Scythians.

Simon (who had informed Heliodorus about the treasury) accused Onias before the king, as if Onias himself had instigated Heliodorus to commit those outrages. For thus the impious ascribe the miracles of God to magic and the invocation of demons.
Onias went to King Seleucus to refute Simon's slanders and to obtain through the king that Simon be silenced.

After the death of Seleucus (slain through the treachery of Heliodorus), when Antiochus called Epiphanes (brother of the slain Seleucus) assumed the kingdom, Jason, brother of Onias—who had borne the Hebrew name Jesus but adopted the Greek form Jason—sought the high priesthood by approaching the king.
Jason promised the king 360 talents of silver. For the magnitude of this sum, calculate from what was said in the preceding chapter at verse 11.

Beyond this he promised another 150 talents if permission were granted him to establish a gymnasium and an ephebeia in Jerusalem. Concerning what these were, see Book 1, chapter 1, verse 15. Peter Faber (Agonisticon, Book 3, chapters 27–28) notes that Lyra most aptly explained the ephebeia as a brothel of beautiful youths; and Athenaeus (Deipnosophistae, Book 14) says ephebi are prostituted boys, called in Hebrew Kadeshim, that is, the filthy and wicked. Our translator renders them 'effeminate men'; Lyra, 'male brothel-keepers.' They are also called teleiazomenoi, that is, initiated and consecrated, namely to Astarte or Venus or Priapus, for Astarte or Venus was the goddess of the Syrians. These same persons wore a broad hat called the petasus (from petannymi, meaning 'to spread'). Or the petasus was a curtain drawn before a brothel to conceal its filthiness. Furthermore, Faber understands by ephebeia taverns and cookshops for feasting and drinking, since they wished to imitate their king Antiochus Epiphanes, who was accustomed to revel and drink with common people he found in shops, and to play and dance with young men; hence some called him Epimanes (madman) instead of Epiphanes, as Athenaeus attests (Deipnosophistae, Book 5, p. 57). Thus brothels were joined to taverns, for a belly saturated with food and wine overflows into lust, and as St. Jerome says, it foams over. Faber adds the reason: In Syria, because of the region's fertility, its inhabitants were freed from many labors necessary elsewhere, and so they continually held banquets with bathing, athletic competitions, and most lavish anointings with precious oil; so that even in their public dining halls (which they called by a more honorable name to veil the shameful reality) they spent the greater part of the day stuffing their bellies with food and wine, not without the pleasure of music, taking portions of the dishes home afterward; as Athenaeus taught me (Deipnosophistae, Book 5, p. 66).

'And to enroll the inhabitants of Jerusalem as Antiochenes.' Antioch was the capital of the Syrian kingdom; hence Antiochenes enjoyed ample privileges from their kings, as likewise did Alexandrians and Romans. Hence when St. Paul said 'I am a Roman citizen' (Acts 22:25), he thereby exempted himself from the punishment then being threatened.

When those institutions had been abolished—namely the laws, customs, and rites of the Jews established for their benefit by the Persian and Greek kings (even though those kings were pagan idolaters), who had permitted the Jews to live by their own laws and had forbidden them to be forced into Gentile religion and customs—the impious apostate Jason introduced Gentile rites, and did so through John the father of Eupolemus (who later in the time of Judas Maccabaeus served as ambassador to Rome to establish friendship and alliance with the Romans, as in 1 Maccabees 8:17), thus abolishing and destroying the civic rights of the citizens and establishing the wicked institutions of the Gentiles.

For he dared to establish a gymnasium at the very citadel of Sion itself, where youths could practice gymnastics—wrestling, running, jumping, horsemanship, sword-fighting, etc.—and to place the most handsome of the ephebi (that is, adolescents) in brothels for abominable and unnatural lust. The Greek word for ephebi refers to pubescent young men: ephebos means the puberty or adolescence which in males occurs at age fourteen; hebe means youth; whence Hebe, wife of Hercules and goddess of puberty and youth, born of Jupiter and Juno, as Hesiod says in the Theogony. The Greek text reads: 'placing the best of the ephebi under the petasus.' The petasus was a broad hat that was the distinctive mark of catamites, or rather the petasus was a veil and curtain covering a brothel, beneath which they shamelessly practiced their unspeakable and wicked lusts.

So that even the priests hastened to be partakers of the wrestling school, that they might watch the athletes competing in wrestling, running, with swords and spears, and indeed that they themselves might compete in these contests and win the prize set for the victor. Hence he adds by way of explanation: 'from his unjust provision,' that is, the prizes customarily given to winners, such as gold, silver, crowns, garments, arms, etc., which he calls unjust (iniqua), meaning unbecoming to priests, being lay, youthful, shameless, and wanton.

'And in the exercises of the discus.' What these were I explained in Book 1, chapter 1, verse 15. The Greek reads: 'after the provocation of the discus,' for it was customary for the discus to be carried around the wrestling ground and the prize for the winner to be placed upon the discus, so that by this means more contestants were invited and challenged to the discus contest, as Peter Faber teaches (Agonisticon, Book 2, chapter 22).

'The glories of the Greeks.' By this he means the Greek games—the Olympic and Isthmian games celebrated at Corinth—and other similar Gentile customs, of which the Greeks (that is, the Gentiles) boasted, especially those who had won the palm and prize in them.

'On whose account a perilous rivalry possessed them,' that is, they competed dangerously in emulating the games and customs of the Greeks and Gentiles, and each strove to surpass the other in them and boasted of doing so. Note 'perilous': there was danger of quarrels and fights lest they attack and destroy one another; danger also lest, as quasi-Greeks, they be entirely subjugated by the Greeks, namely the Antiochus kings of Asia; but greatest of all was the danger from God, lest He severely punish their apostasy from Him and from Judaism to Gentilism and demons.

'Whom they had as enemies and destroyers'—both because the Greeks had formerly been enemies and persecutors of the Jews, and because now by their example they were destroying the souls of the Jews who imitated them. # GEM

[On the events preceding ch.4] Simon the informer, who had betrayed the temple treasury, now falsely accused Onias to Apollonius of plotting against the state, seeking to turn the king against the holy high priest.
Onias is called "a plotter against the government" by Simon, though in truth he was a guardian of his people and zealous for the laws — a man of genuine piety slandered by one of corrupt ambition.
The rivalry between Simon and Onias grew so fierce that murders were committed by Simon's faction, so grave was the disorder arising from this man's malice.
Onias therefore went to the king — to Seleucus — to obtain relief, judging that without royal intervention the affair could not be settled and Simon would not cease his malice.

When Antiochus Epiphanes succeeded to the kingdom after Seleucus, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by promising the king 360 talents of silver and 80 talents from other revenues.

Jason promised Antiochus 360 talents and from another source 80 talents more, and undertook to pay 150 more if authority were given him to establish a gymnasium and body of youth and to enrol the men of Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch.
Jason thus purchased the high priesthood by simony, bribing Antiochus to depose his own brother Onias, a holy and innocent man, and to appoint him in his place — a grave sacrilege and injustice.

When Jason obtained the high priesthood, he immediately shifted his countrymen over to the Greek way of life, introducing Gentile customs and abandoning the holy laws established by God through Moses.
He set aside the royal concessions granted to the Jews through the embassy of John the father of Eupolemus, and destroyed the lawful institutions, introducing new customs contrary to the Law.

Jason erected a gymnasium beneath the very citadel itself and induced the noblest of the young men to wear the Greek hat — a mark of pagan culture — thus drawing Israel's finest youth away from their ancestral religion.

Such was the extreme of Greek fashions and increase of heathen customs through the exceeding profaneness of Jason, that wicked and no true high priest, that the priests had no more any zeal for the services of the altar.

The priests were no longer diligent at the altar but despised the temple and neglected the sacrifices, hastening to take part in the unlawful exercises in the wrestling court after the signal for the discus-throwing — impious subversion of their sacred office. # GEM

They set no store by the honors of their fathers but thought the Greek glories the finest. For the sake of these they endured great hardship, coveting Greek fashions in everything — clear sign of how ambition and vanity corrupt religious men.

But this very emulation of Greek customs and eager imitation of them proved their ruin; for the very men whose ways they longed to follow and whom they sought to imitate became their enemies and punished them.

It is no light thing to sin against the laws of God; the following period itself will make this clear. When Antiochus turned upon them, these apostates found no mercy from him whom they had flattered.

When the quinquennial games were held at Tyre in the presence of the king, the vile Jason sent envoys as representing the Antiochenes of Jerusalem, carrying 300 drachmas of silver for the sacrifice to Hercules.

[On the 300 drachmas sent for the sacrifice of Hercules] Jason sent 300 didrachmas of silver — that is, 300 Roman silver coins, with which victim animals such as sheep and goats could be bought for sacrifice to Hercules. Some however, thinking this sum too mean and paltry for a High Priest seeking to win Antiochus's favor, hold these didrachmas were of gold, yet called "of silver" meaning simply "of money" — so that Jason sent 300 golden didrachmas making 600 Roman gold pieces. Thus Sanchez; for a gold drachma equals one aureus, and a didrachma two aurei, so 300 golden didrachmas equal 600 aurei.

[On Hercules at Tyre] The sacrifice was for Hercules, who was venerated in Syria and Tyre as a kind of god of strength, wars and contests, being the especial god of the Tyrians, hence called Hercules Tyrius. See Giraldus on the gods of the nations under Hercules. Hence many believe Hercules was none other than Samson, who near Tyre in Palestine gave extraordinary examples of his strength, as I noted in the book of Judges. There a quinquennial contest was celebrated in Hercules's honor, wherein athletes professed to imitate Hercules, and statues and shrines of Hercules were set up in the gymnasia there, as Serarius shows from Galen, Pausanias, Athenaeus and others.

[On the envoys' scruple] Jason sent the 300 didrachmas for Hercules's sacrifice; but his envoys, though impious, were less wicked than Jason: being Jews who worshipped the true God and detested idols, they who carried the money requested that it not be spent on sacrifice to Hercules or other pagan gods but applied to other uses. Therefore Antiochus Epiphanes spent the money on the construction of triremes which he was fitting out for his first expedition against Egypt, which follows.

[On Antiochus's visit to Jerusalem] Antiochus Epiphanes came to Jerusalem because certain chief men of Egypt had defected from king Ptolemy Philometor and wished to make his younger brother Ptolemy Euergetes, also called Physcon, their king. Seeing the quarrel between the two brothers Philometor and Physcon over the kingdom of Egypt, Antiochus, who was as it were the uncle of both (for Cleopatra, sister of Epiphanes, was the mother of both), pretended he wished to be the arbitrator and mediator of the dispute and to settle the matter between the brothers; but in truth he intended to seize the kingdom from both nephews and arrogate it to himself. The chief men and guardians of the king, suspecting this, refused to allow him to meddle in Egyptian affairs. And this is what is said here: when Antiochus learned he had been excluded from Egyptian affairs, consulting his own interests in Syria, he departed from Tyre and came to Joppa, and thence to Jerusalem, where Jason received him as a king, indeed as a kind of earthly god — for such Antiochus wished to be regarded and adored — with torchlight processions and acclamations, as follows.

After three years Jason sent Menelaus, brother of the aforementioned Simon the traitor and informer concerning the temple funds, carrying money to king Antiochus Epiphanes as a kind of tribute in token of subjection.

Menelaus, being commended by Jason to the king, magnified the face of his power — that is, with grand words extolled the royal power, majesty and splendor of Antiochus, arguing that through it he could accomplish anything in Syria not only in secular and political matters but also in ecclesiastical and sacred ones, and could therefore appoint whatever High Priest he wished in Jerusalem and so transfer the pontificate from Jason to himself. By this art and fraud Menelaus twisted the high priesthood from Jason back upon himself, outbidding Jason by 300 talents of silver — that is, offering Antiochus 300 talents more than Jason had offered. Just as Jason by offering 360 talents to Antiochus Epiphanes had bought the pontificate and thrust out his own brother Onias the Holy, so likewise Menelaus, sent by Jason, treacherously supplanted Jason himself and thrust him out, promising the king another 300 talents beyond the 360 already promised by Jason. Hence the Greek reads: "surpassing Jason by 300 talents" — Jason had promised 360 talents for the pontificate, but Menelaus increased this sum and nearly doubled it; as when a thing is sold at auction each bidder raises the price and the one who offers the highest price obtains the thing, so here Menelaus, adding 300 and promising the king in all 660 talents, obtained the pontificate and cunningly excluded his own master Jason.

Menelaus, having received the king's mandate that the Jews should receive him as High Priest in place of Jason, came having nothing worthy of the priesthood — both because Menelaus was of the tribe of Benjamin which was ineligible for the priesthood, whereas Jason was of the tribe of Levi and therefore eligible; and because he was impious and obtained the pontificate by simony. He had the temper of a cruel tyrant, hence in v.34 he had the true and holy Pontiff Onias cruelly murdered through Andronicus.

Jason, who had taken his own brother Onias the High Priest captive, was himself deceived by his servant Menelaus and driven out as a fugitive into the land of the Ammonites. This was a just judgment of God and a just penalty of retaliation: he who had supplanted his brother was supplanted by his servant; he who had bought the pontificate was expelled by a higher bidder; he who had driven Onias the Holy Pontiff into exile lived miserably as an exile among the Ammonites. Here observe how true is that word of Sirach 27:28: "He who throws a stone on high throws it on his own head, and a treacherous blow opens up wounds." And: "He who digs a pit will fall into it, and he who sets a stone as a trap for his neighbor will stumble over it, and he who lays a snare for another will perish in it."

Menelaus was removed from the pontificate by Antiochus Epiphanes because he did not deliver the 360 talents he had promised. Epiphanes therefore created in place of Menelaus as pontiff his brother Lysimachus, who apparently promised the king the same sum. So the impious Menelaus had expelled the impious Jason, and was likewise expelled by his own impious brother Lysimachus. When honor and ambition are at stake, brother does not acknowledge brother but hates and supplants him, for each desires dignity for himself rather than for his brother; indeed not rarely he snatches it from his brother. So great is the force of ambition and cupidity in man corrupted by sin.

It came to pass that the people of Tarsus and Mallus rose in revolt because they had been given as a gift to Antiochis the king's concubine by Epiphanes, so that from their revenues she might procure for herself costly garments, necklaces, maidservants, servants and carriages. Hear Cicero, Actio 5 against Verres: "They say that barbarian kings of the Persians and Syrians customarily have several wives..."
Simon, who had informed against the treasury and his country, made false accusations against Onias, saying that it was he who had incited Heliodorus and had been the originator of these evils.
He dared to call the benefactor of the city, the protector of his fellow countrymen, and a zealot for the laws, an enemy of the state.
When his hatred progressed to such a degree that even murders were being committed by one of Simon's approved agents,
Onias recognized the danger of the contention, and that Apollonius, son of Menestheus and governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, was intensifying the malice of Simon,
he betook himself to the king, not to be an accuser of his fellow citizens, but with a view to the common and private good of all the people.
For he saw that without royal oversight, it was impossible for the state to have peace any longer, and that Simon would not cease from his madness.
But after the death of Seleucus, when Antiochus, who was called Epiphanes, took the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means,
promising the king in his petition three hundred and sixty talents of silver, and out of another revenue eighty talents.
He further promised to pay a hundred and fifty more, if it were allowed to establish by his authority a gymnasium and youth-center, and to enroll the people of Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch.
When the king had granted this, and Jason had seized the high priesthood, he immediately shifted his countrymen over to the Greek way of life.

He set aside the royal concessions to the Jews, secured through John the father of Eupolemus, who had gone on the mission to establish friendship and alliance with the Romans; and he annulled the lawful ways of living and introduced new customs contrary to the law.
For with alacrity he founded a gymnasium right under the citadel, and led the noblest of the young men to wear the Greek hat.
There was such an extreme of Hellenization and increase in the adoption of foreign ways because of the surpassing wickedness of Jason, who was ungodly and no true high priest,

that the priests were no longer earnest about the services of the altar, but despising the sanctuary and neglecting the sacrifices, they hurried to take part in the unlawful proceedings in the wrestling court after the signal for the throwing of the discus,
counting as nothing the honors prized by their ancestors and thinking that Greek forms of prestige were the finest thing of all.
For this reason, grave trouble overtook them, and the very people whose ways of living they eagerly emulated and whom they wished to imitate in everything became their enemies and punished them.

For it is no light thing to show irreverence to the divine laws, as subsequent events will make clear. # GEM Lapide notes: the punishment of Hellenizers by the very Greeks they imitated is divine justice, showing that contempt of God's law inevitably draws down retribution from the very source of the temptation.
When the quinquennial games were being held at Tyre and the king was present,

the vile Jason sent envoys, chosen as being Antiochian citizens from Jerusalem, to carry three hundred silver drachmas for the sacrifice to Hercules. Those who carried this money judged it inappropriate to use it for sacrifice, since that was forbidden, and determined it should be spent on other expenditures.
So this money was intended by the sender for the sacrifice to Hercules, but on account of those who carried it, it was devoted to the construction of triremes.

When Apollonius the son of Menestheus was sent to Egypt for the enthronement of Ptolemy Philometor as king, Antiochus learned that Ptolemy was hostile to his government, and he took measures for his own security. Accordingly he went to Joppa and then on to Jerusalem.
He was welcomed magnificently by Jason and the city, and was escorted in with a blaze of torches and with shouts. From there he marched his army into Phoenicia.
After a period of three years Jason sent Menelaus, the brother of the previously mentioned Simon, to carry the money to the king and to deliver reports on important matters.
But he, when presented to the king, flattered him with an air of authority, and diverted the high priesthood to himself, outbidding Jason by three hundred talents of silver.
After receiving the king's orders he returned, possessing no qualification for the high priesthood, but having the hot temper of a cruel tyrant and the rage of a savage wild beast.
So Jason, who after supplanting his own brother was supplanted by another man, was driven as a fugitive into the country of the Ammonites.
Although Menelaus continued to hold the office, he paid nothing of the money he had promised to the king,
despite the demand of Sostratus the commandant of the citadel, whose duty it was to collect the revenues. For this reason both were summoned before the king.
Menelaus left his brother Lysimachus as his deputy in the high priesthood, while Sostratus left Crates, the commander of the Cyprian troops, as his deputy.

While such was the state of affairs, it happened that the people of Tarsus and of Mallus revolted because their cities had been given as a present to Antiochis, the king's concubine. Lapide: Tarsus and Mallus were noble cities of Cilicia, not Sicily, and they revolted because they were indignant at being handed over to a royal concubine who called herself Antiochis after her lover Antiochus.
So the king went hastily to settle the trouble, leaving Andronicus, a man of high rank, to act as his deputy.

Then Menelaus, thinking he had obtained a suitable opportunity, stole some of the golden vessels of the temple and gave them to Andronicus; and the other vessels he sold to Tyre and the neighboring cities.

When Onias became fully aware of this, he rebuked Menelaus for the sacrilege. Lapide: Although Onias had been unjustly stripped of the high priesthood and was living in exile at Antioch, yet because he was truly and legitimately the high priest, he rebuked the sacrilegious act out of priestly zeal, even though he likely foresaw that this would bring about his own death—knowing thereby he would obtain the crown of martyrdom he desired. # GEM

On this account Menelaus, taking Andronicus aside, urged him to kill Onias. Andronicus came to Onias, and resorting to treachery offered him sworn pledges and persuaded him to come out from the place of sanctuary; then, with no regard for justice, he immediately put him out of the way.
For this reason not only Jews, but many also of other nations, were indignant and displeased at the unjust murder of the man.
When the king returned from the region of Cilicia, the Jews in the city, and certain of the Greeks who also abhorred the injustice of the deed, appealed to him about Onias's murder.

Antiochus was genuinely grieved and was moved to pity, and wept because of the moderation and good conduct of the deceased. Lapide: It is remarkable that even the impious king Epiphanes shed tears over the death of Onias, a testimony to the sanctity of this holy high priest who governed the Jewish people for thirty-six years with great fortitude.

Inflamed with anger, he immediately stripped Andronicus of his purple robe, tore off his other garments, and led him through the whole city to that very place where he had committed the outrage against Onias, and there he put the murderer out of the way, the Lord repaying him with the punishment he deserved.

When many acts of sacrilege had been committed in the city by Lysimachus with the connivance of Menelaus, and when report of them had spread abroad, the populace gathered against Lysimachus, because many of the golden vessels had already been stolen.

Since the crowds were becoming aroused and filled with anger, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men and launched an unjust attack, under the leadership of a certain Auranus, a man advanced in years and no less advanced in folly.

When the Jews became aware of Lysimachus's attack, some picked up stones, some blocks of wood, and others took handfuls of the ashes that were lying there and threw them in wild confusion at Lysimachus and his men.
As a result, they wounded many of them, and killed some, and put all the rest to flight; the sacrilegious fellow himself they killed close by the treasury.

Charges were brought against Menelaus about this incident. Lapide: The Jews accused Menelaus before Antiochus Epiphanes as the author of all these evils, for it was he who had counseled his brother Lysimachus the pseudo-pontiff to commit so many crimes and acts of sacrilege.
When the king came to Tyre, three men sent by the senate of Jerusalem brought the case before him.

But Menelaus, already as good as beaten, promised a substantial sum of money to Ptolemy son of Dorymenes to win over the king. Lapide: Ptolemy son of Dorymenes held great authority and favor with Antiochus Epiphanes, and was bribed by Menelaus to persuade the king to acquit him as if innocent, and to condemn the Jewish accusers—who were entirely innocent—as slanderers deserving death by the law of retaliation.
Therefore Ptolemy, taking the king aside into a colonnade as if for refreshment, induced him to change his mind.

Menelaus, the cause of all the evil, he acquitted of the charges against him, while those unfortunate men, who would have been freed even if they had pleaded their case before Scythians, he condemned to death.
Those who had spoken for the city, for the villages, and for the holy vessels quickly suffered the unjust penalty.
Therefore even some Tyrians, vexed at the crime, provided magnificently for their burial.

And so, through the greed of those who held power, Menelaus remained in office, growing in wickedness, having become the chief plotter against his fellow citizens. Lapide: Menelaus, after his brother Lysimachus the pseudo-pontiff had been killed by the people, thrust himself again into the pontificate through courtiers corrupted by money—though he was not of the tribe of Levi but of Benjamin. He held this illegitimate pontificate for four years, from the year 140 of the Greeks when he contrived the killing of the holy Onias, until the year 145 when Mattathias rose up against him and the king, and was appointed leader and high priest by the people.
He commanded his soldiers to kill, and to spare none that met them, and to slay those who went up into the houses.
So there were slaughters of young men and old, destruction of women and children, and killing of virgins and infants.
In the space of three days, eighty thousand were slain, forty thousand were taken prisoner, and no fewer were sold.
But not content with this, he presumed to enter the temple, the most holy place in all the earth, with Menelaus for his guide, who had been a traitor to the laws and to his country.
And with his wicked hands he took the sacred vessels, which had been placed there by other kings and cities for the adornment and glory of the place, handling them unworthily and defiling them.

Thus Antiochus, being alienated in mind, did not consider that God was for a little while angry with the city because of the sins of the inhabitants; and therefore this contempt had come upon the place.

For had it not happened that they were involved in many sins, this man also, as soon as he came, would have been scourged and driven back from his presumption, as Heliodorus was, who was sent by King Seleucus to plunder the treasury. # GEM
But God did not choose the people for the sake of the place, but the place for the sake of the people.

And therefore the place itself, having shared in the misfortunes of the people, afterward shared in their benefits; and what was forsaken in the wrath of the Almighty was restored again in all its glory, when the great Lord was reconciled.

So Antiochus, having carried away from the temple one thousand eight hundred talents, departed in haste to Antioch, thinking in his arrogance that he could make the land navigable and the sea passable on foot, so great was the pride of his heart.
He left governors to afflict the people: at Jerusalem, Philip, a Phrygian by birth, and in character more barbarous than the man who appointed him;
and at Gerizim, Andronicus and Menelaus, who pressed more heavily upon the citizens than the rest.
And being set against the Jews, he sent Apollonius, a hateful commander, with an army of twenty-two thousand, commanding him to slay all men of mature age and to sell the women and young people.
When he arrived at Jerusalem, he feigned peace and waited until the holy sabbath day; then finding the Jews keeping the day of rest, he commanded his men to take up arms.
He killed all who came out to watch, and running through the city with armed men, he slew a great multitude.

But Judas Maccabaeus, who was the tenth, had withdrawn into the wilderness, and there he and his companions lived in the mountains after the manner of wild beasts, feeding on herbs, that they might not be partakers of the defilement.

EODEM TEMPORE Antiochus (Epiphanes) SECUNDAM PROFECTIONEM PARAVIT IN AEGYPTUM: that he might subjugate it by arms; but being prevented by the Romans, returning into neighboring Judea, he poured out his indignation. Gabriel had foretold this same thing to Daniel more than three hundred years before, at ch. 11, v. 23 and following. See what was said there.

CONTIGIT AUTEM PER UNIVERSAM JEROSOLYMORUM CIVITATEM VIDERI DIEBUS XL PER AERA EQUITES DISCURRENTES: among themselves with drawn swords, and hurling missiles at one another, as the author amplifies with the most vivid words that follow; all of which were portents of the immense calamity threatening Judea, as follows.

Moreover these portents were wrought by Angels at God's command in the air, through a certain disposition, motion, and concurrence of vapors and exhalations, which bore the appearance of colliding battle lines, so that God might thereby foretoken to the Jews the slaughter soon to be inflicted upon them by Antiochus Epiphanes, and the battles which Judas Maccabaeus was to wage against him.

Thus before Titus and the Romans besieged and captured Jerusalem on the 21st day of May, Josephus says in Bk. 7 of the Jewish War, ch. 12: After the feast days, before sunset, chariots were seen carried through the void across all the regions, and armed battle lines crossing through the clouds and surrounding the city.

The same occurred in the time of St. Gregory, when the Lombards invaded Italy. Hear him, Homily 1 on the Gospels: Before Italy was delivered to be smitten by the Gentile sword, we saw fiery battle lines in the sky, flashing with that very blood of the human race which was afterward shed. # GEM

Julius Obsequens, in his book On Prodigies to Augustus Caesar, ch. 6 and 7, reports that under the consuls L. Opimius and Q. Posthumius, weapons were seen flying in the sky; and under the consuls C. Marius and Horns, a dog spoke at Ariminum, and celestial weapons were seen fighting from east and west, with those from the west vanquished.

Under the consuls Lucius Scipio and Caius Norbanus, in the time of Sulla, between Capua and the Volturnus, a tremendous sound of standards and a dreadful clash of arms was heard, so that two battle lines seemed to be engaging for several days. Likewise under the consuls Pansa and Hirtius, when battle was joined against Antony, shapes of weapons and missiles were seen rising from the earth with a crash toward the sky.
Eutropius, Bk. 6 of his History, when Caesar was fighting against Pompey, reports there was such a crash of arms in the air that at Antioch men rushed to the walls as if at the approach of enemies.

Hear also Pliny, Bk. 2, ch. 57: We have learned that the clashing of arms and the sound of trumpets were heard from the sky during the Cimbrian wars, and frequently both before and after. In the third consulship of Marius, celestial weapons were reported by the people of Ameria and Tuder, rushing together from east and west, those from the west prevailing.

Similar signs preceded the assassination of Caesar, of which Appian writes in Bk. 4 of the Civil Wars: Monstrous human voices, the clashing of arms, the rushing of horses were heard with no one in sight. Ovid, in Book Fifteen of the Metamorphoses, writes of the same period: Yet they give no uncertain signs of coming grief; weapons clashing amid dark clouds, and terrible trumpets, and horns heard in the sky, forewarned of the abomination.

Similar portents are reviewed by Seneca in the Natural Questions, by Aug. Niphus in the Meteorologica, by Corn. Gemma Frisius in the Cosmocritica, and by Conr. Lycosthenes (heterodox in faith though he be) in his book On Prodigies, pp. 189, 210, and 228.

I recall that in Belgium, when I was a boy, similar portents in the sky were shown at the beginning of the Belgian wars, which wise men even then foretold from these signs were imminent upon Belgium, and the event proved and still proves that the prediction was most true.

Concerning the spectral visions in the air: some philosophers, as Averroes and others, hold these occur naturally by the power of the stars, and especially by the power of the star Perseus; so also Augustus Niphus, book 1 of the Meteorologica, fol. 31, p. 14; but it is more truly held that these things are accomplished by the special providence of God through Angels, who nonetheless use stars, winds, and vapors to produce these spectral appearances, so that they may foreshadow the figures of impending disaster. See Christoph. à Castro, book 1 of the Prolegomena on the Minor Prophets, ch. 17.

WHEREFORE ALL PRAYED THAT THE PORTENTS (in Greek ἐπιφάνειαν, that is, appearances, visions, displays of armies clashing in the air, which foreshowed that fierce conflicts would occur in Judaea) MIGHT TURN TO GOOD: that is, that the Jews in these conflicts might be victorious, and not be conquered but conquer, as in fact Antiochus Epiphanes was conquered by Judas and his Maccabean brothers.

BUT WHEN A FALSE RUMOR HAD GONE FORTH AS THOUGH ANTIOCHUS (Epiphanes) HAD DEPARTED THIS LIFE, JASON, TAKING NOT FEWER THAN A THOUSAND MEN, SUDDENLY ASSAULTED THE CITY: namely Jerusalem. This Jason, brother of the holy Pontiff Onias, had purchased the High Priesthood from Epiphanes with money, and had thereby thrust his brother Onias from the pontificate; but when Menelaus offered more money, Epiphanes transferred the pontificate to him, excluding Jason. Jason therefore, hearing the rumor of Epiphanes' death, and no longer fearing his displeasure and power, returned from Ammanitis where he was in exile, armed with a thousand men, and invaded and seized Jerusalem in order to recover the pontificate. Wherefore Menelaus, who had displaced Jason, fled from the city into the CITADEL of Mount Sion.

THE PRINCIPALITY (the sacred one, namely the Pontificate, and the political principality annexed to it which Jason most coveted; for in those times the High Priest was simultaneously commander and prince of the people) HE DID NOT OBTAIN: because by the gathering crowd of citizens he himself was driven from the city as an enemy and tyrant along with his men. For what could his thousand men and his schemes avail against hundreds of thousands of citizens? Wherefore as a fugitive he departed again into Ammanitis, and wandering and roaming there like Cain, he fled into Arabia; but when he was suspected there by King Aretas of treachery or of coveting the kingdom, he was seized while fleeing and went to Egypt; because Ptolemy king of Egypt was an enemy of Epiphanes, but when peace was concluded between the two, Jason, lest Epiphanes demand him from Ptolemy for execution, fled from Egypt to the Spartans, as ancient friends and kinsmen of the Jews; and there he miserably ended his life in exile, and being dead was denied proper human burial, and was buried with the burial of an ass in a dunghill.
As a fugitive from the laws - Greek has "transgressor and apostate of the laws," inasmuch as he had apostatized from Judaism to Gentilism and compelled others to apostatize likewise.

The king (Epiphanes) suspected the Jews would abandon his society (their alliance with him). The reasons for suspicion were these: First, because he knew them to be hostile to him, in that he had taken from them Onias the holy High Priest and substituted impious and sacrilegious pseudo-pontiffs, Jason, Menelaus, and Lysimachus, who strove to lead the Jews from Judaism into Gentilism. Second, because Antiochus, having been expelled from Egypt with ignominy by the Romans, supposed he would now be held in contempt by the Jews, and that they, emboldened by the aid of the neighboring Egyptians and their Roman allies, would shake off his yoke, as the Egyptians had done. Third, because he himself knew he was hated by the Jews on account of his avarice and cruelty. Fourth, because the Jews had stoned Lysimachus, whom he had appointed as High Priest. Fifth, because they had spread a false rumor of his death, v. 5. Gorionides adds that they had even rejoiced at it: "There came," he says, "wicked men of our nation to the king and said: Behold, certain portents have been seen in Jerusalem, and the citizens said: Antiochus is dead, and they rejoiced at the destruction of our lord the king." Sixth, because the inhabitants of Jerusalem, upon Antiochus's return from Egypt, were preparing to resist him, and accordingly barred the gates against him as he approached. All these things Epiphanes learned through his spies and friends, and especially through Menelaus, to whom, after the stoning of Lysimachus his father, he had restored the pontificate. Menelaus betrayed to him all the counsels, actions, and words of the Jews against Epiphanes. For these reasons Epiphanes, breaking open by force the gates of the city that had been shut against him, raged against the Jews with such cruelty and barbarity that within three days he slew 80,000, sold 40,000, and bound as many captives, as follows.

Thus Antiochus, alienated in mind (Greek: elated in spirit), supposed he had so thoroughly supplanted the Jews that he could use them as slaves and sacrilegiously violate and plunder their temple, carrying away from it 1,800 talents, v. 21.

But not on account of the place was the nation chosen, but on account of the nation was the place chosen by God. That is to say: God did not choose the Jewish nation on account of the land of Judea, but on account of the nation itself, once faithful and holy under David and Solomon, He chose the land of Judea as the place where He would establish a house for Himself — namely, the Temple — in which, as the most holy among holy citizens, He might dwell among His servants and sons. But now, on account of the great sins of the Jews, God has forsaken the Temple and given it over as prey to Antiochus; yet, as we hope, He will return to it again when the penitent Jews shall have turned back to their God and served Him piously and holily, as they did before. # GEM

He also left overseers to afflict the nation. That is: Antiochus, returning to Antioch as to the royal seat of his kingdom, left behind in Judea savage and brutal men who afflicted the Jews even more than he himself had — namely Philip, Andronicus, and Menelaus the pseudo-pontiff, the suggester and instigator of all his wickedness.

And when he was set against the Jews (some read "opposed"; hence the Greek has the sense of "holding hostile intent against the Jews"): that is, when Antiochus had set his mind, resolved, and firmly determined against the Jews to destroy and annihilate them if he could, he sent Apollonius, the prince hated by the Jews, with an army of 22,000, commanding him to kill all men of full age. This is the same Apollonius who, when Antiochus the Great was fighting against the Romans, commanded his right wing while Hannibal commanded the left, as Livy attests, bk. 37. The same man, sent by Epiphanes against Judas Maccabeus a few years later, was defeated and slain by him, 1 Macc. 3:11.
And all who had gone forth to witness (to observe the sacrifices and sacred offices customarily celebrated by the Jews on the Sabbath feast) he massacred.

But Judas Maccabeus, who was the tenth — that is, he was to be the tenth High Priest, reckoning from Gaddo who had been High Priest in the time of Alexander the Great. So Sa, Mariana, Lyranus, and Dionysius. Others more simply hold that Judas is called the tenth because at that time he held tenth place in the order of priests. Or rather, Judas had been the tenth, that is, a decemvir, one of the ten chief and leading priests who governed the people. From the Greek one may simply translate: Judas...

King Antiochus Epiphanes sent a certain Antiochene senator (for the senate was a council of elders, and wisdom and prudence belong to the aged). In Greek the word is Ἀθηναῖον, that is, an Athenian, perhaps because he was of Athenian origin but lived as a citizen at Antioch; or because his proper name sounded like "Athenian." Our Mariana thinks he was Bacchides, whom Mattathias killed out of zeal when he was compelling the Jews to sacrifice to idols, as was said in book 1, chapter 2, verse 25.

He commanded that the temple be contaminated and renamed after Jupiter Olympius. Thus Antiochus ordered that the idol of Jupiter be placed in the temple and worshiped, and the temple be solemnly dedicated and thenceforth called the temple of Jupiter. Behold, this is the abomination of desolation, that is, the abominable idol laying all waste, foretold three hundred years before by Daniel, chapter 11, verse 31.

Concerning Gerizim: the temple of the Gerizimites, that is, the Samaritans, of which John 4:20 speaks, Antiochus ordered dedicated to Jupiter Hospitalis, because the inhabitants of Gerizim and the Samaritans were guests and strangers. For they had been brought from Assyria into Samaria as guests and foreigners by Shalmaneser, 3 Kings 17. Hence the Samaritans in their letter to Antiochus, which Josephus records in book 12, chapter 9, call themselves hospites, that is, strangers and foreigners. Jupiter Hospitalis was so called because as god of hospitality the care and protection of guests fell to him, lest anyone defraud or harm them with impunity. Hence Aeneas invokes him when journeying as a stranger to Italy, saying in Aeneid 1: "Jupiter (for they say you give laws to guests) may you wish this day to be joyful for Tyrians and for those departed from Troy."

The Jews were led in crowds and in great numbers with bitter compulsion, that is by violent coercion, on the birthday of King Antiochus Epiphanes, to the sacrifices which were offered to idols, so that they might assist and cooperate in them, and eat from them, as if consenting to the idols and to idolatry.

When the rites of Liber (Bacchus) were celebrated, they were compelled crowned with ivy to process around in honor of Liber. That is, when the Bacchanalia, the feasts of Bacchus, were celebrated with drinking, dancing, playing, fornication, and every licentiousness of the flesh and freedom of concupiscence (whence Bacchus was called Liber, because he granted everyone full freedom of the flesh in his feasts and sacred rites), the Jews were compelled in honor of Liber or Bacchus to be crowned with ivy and thus to process around the temple or city. Ivy was dedicated to Bacchus because by its coldness it resists wine and drunkenness, of which Bacchus was the god, as Pliny attests in book 16, chapter 34, Tertullian in his book On the Crown of the Soldier chapter 7, and Caelius Rhodiginus in book 14, chapter 49. The Greeks indulging in their revels, that is their drinking parties (and especially Antiochus Epiphanes) worshiped Bacchus; hence they feigned that Bacchus was born among them from the thigh of Jupiter, as Dionysius says. Bacchus was called Liber as if the god of liberty, either because wine dissolves cares and frees the mind from them and makes one bolder in all endeavors, or as Varro derives it from "sliding," because the slippery mind slides wherever it pleases. St. Augustine in book 6, On the City of God, chapter 9, teaches that Liber is so called because males in copulation are freed by his benefit through the emission of seed. The same office in females is performed by Libera, whom they also identify with Venus. Moreover, how great was the turpitude of the rites of Liber, the same Augustine describes in book 7, On the City of God, chapter 21.

The decree compelling the Jews to worship Bacchus went out to the neighboring cities of the Gentiles at the suggestion of the Ptolemies, that is, the two brothers Ptolemy Philometor and Ptolemy Physcon, who reigned in Egypt. But because these at this time were almost boys and did not occupy the cities near Jerusalem in Syria, and were moreover well-disposed toward the Jews, it is better to understand by the Ptolemies here those who were of the family of the aforementioned Ptolemies and served as governors under Antiochus Epiphanes over Cilicia and Phoenicia, especially Tyre and Sidon, such as Ptolemaeus, who is mentioned in chapters 4, 44 and 8:8. For since Antiochus...

# GEM The author exhorts readers not to be dismayed on account of adverse events, but to consider that what has befallen is not for the destruction but for the correction of our race. For it is a sign of great beneficence not to allow sinners long to act according to their pleasure, but to apply chastisements immediately.

It is a sign of great beneficence not to permit sinners long to act according to their pleasure, but to apply punishments immediately. For God deals otherwise with the other nations: He waits patiently for them so that when the day of judgment comes He may punish them in the fullness of their sins; but with His own people He has determined otherwise, so that once our sins have devolved to their end, He may then at last take vengeance upon us.

Therefore God never removes His mercy from us; and though He chastises His people in adversity, He does not abandon them. This is said briefly for the admonition of readers. Now we must proceed to the narrative.
Eleazar, one of the foremost scribes, a man advanced in age and of comely countenance, was being compelled with open mouth to eat swine's flesh.
He, embracing a most glorious death rather than a hateful life, went forward voluntarily to the place of punishment.
Considering how he ought to approach, bearing it with patience, he resolved not to admit unlawful things for love of life.
