But those who stood by, moved by wicked compassion on account of the old friendship they had with the man, taking him aside privately, begged that meat be brought to him which was lawful for him to eat, so that he might simulate having eaten what the king commanded, namely the flesh of the sacrifice.
That by this act he might be freed from death; and on account of their old friendship with the man, they showed him this kindness.

But he began to reflect on the dignity befitting his advanced age and old age, and the grey hairs of his inborn nobility, and his conduct of an excellent life from boyhood; and according to the ordinances of the holy law established by God, he answered quickly, saying he preferred to be sent to hell rather than comply.
For it is not worthy of our age, he said, to dissemble, lest many of the young, supposing that Eleazar at ninety years of age had gone over to the manner of life of the foreigners...
...and they themselves through my dissimulation, and for the sake of a brief period of corruptible life, be led astray, and through this I bring stain and execration upon my old age.

# GEM For though in the present time I might escape the punishments of men, yet neither living nor dead shall I escape the hand of the Almighty. This is a most notable confession of faith in divine omniscience and the judgment after death, worthy of admiration in this aged martyr.
Therefore by departing bravely from life, I shall show myself worthy of my old age.

And to young men I shall leave a noble example, if with a ready and courageous mind I undergo an honorable death for the sake of the most venerable and holy laws. With these words he was immediately dragged off to punishment.
But those who led him, who had been somewhat gentle a little before, were turned to anger on account of the words spoken by him, which they considered to have been uttered out of arrogance.

But when he was being struck down with blows, he groaned and said: O Lord, who have holy knowledge, you clearly know that when I might have been freed from death, I endure harsh bodily pains; yet in soul I willingly suffer these things out of fear of you.
And thus he departed this life, leaving by his death a memorial not only to the young but to the whole nation, as an example of virtue and fortitude.
Lysias, the king's guardian and kinsman, who had charge of the government, was greatly displeased at what had happened.
He assembled about eighty thousand men and all his cavalry and came against the Jews, thinking he would make the city a home for Greeks.
and that he would levy tribute on the temple, as he did on the sacred places of the other nations, and that he would put up the high priesthood for sale every year.
He took no account whatever of the power of God, but was elated with his ten thousands of infantry, and his thousands of cavalry, and his eighty elephants.
So he invaded Judea, and approaching Beth-zur, which was a fortified place about five stadia from Jerusalem, he pressed it hard.
When Maccabeus and his men got word that Lysias was besieging the strongholds, they and all the people, with lamentations and tears, besought the Lord to send a good angel to save Israel.
Maccabeus himself was the first to take up arms, and he urged the others to risk their lives with him to aid their kindred. Then they eagerly rushed off together.
And there, while they were still near Jerusalem, a horseman appeared at their head, clothed in white and brandishing weapons of gold.
And together they all praised the merciful God, and were strengthened in heart, ready to assail not only men but the most savage beasts or walls of iron.
They advanced in battle order, having their heavenly ally, for the Lord had mercy on them.
They hurled themselves like lions against the enemy, and slew eleven thousand of them and sixteen hundred horsemen, and forced all the rest to flee.
Most of them got away stripped and wounded, and Lysias himself escaped by disgraceful flight.

And as he was not without intelligence, he pondered over the defeat which had befallen him, and realized that the Hebrews were invincible because the mighty God fought on their side. So he sent to them
and persuaded them to settle everything on just terms, promising that he would persuade the king, constraining him to be their friend.
Maccabeus, having regard for the common good, agreed to all that Lysias urged. For the king granted every request which Maccabeus delivered to Lysias in writing, concerning the Jews.
The letter written to the Jews by Lysias was to this effect: "Lysias to the people of the Jews, greeting.
John and Absalom, who were sent by you, have delivered your signed communication and have asked about the matters indicated therein.
I have informed the king of everything that needed to be brought before him, and he has agreed to what was possible.
If you will maintain your good will toward the government, I will endeavor for the future to help promote your welfare.
And concerning these matters and their details, I have ordered these men and my representatives to confer with you.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Dioscorinthius twenty-fourth."
The king's letter ran thus: "King Antiochus to his brother Lysias, greeting.
Since our father has gone on to the gods, we wish that the subjects of the kingdom be undisturbed in caring for their own affairs.
We have heard that the Jews do not consent to our father's desire to change them over to Greek customs but prefer their own way of living and request that their own customs be allowed them.
Accordingly, since we choose that this nation also be free from disturbance, our decision is that their temple be restored to them and that they live according to the customs of their ancestors.
You will do well, therefore, to send word to them and give them pledges of friendship, so that they, knowing our mind, may be of good cheer and go about their own affairs."
To the nation the king's letter was as follows: "King Antiochus to the senate of the Jews and to the other Jews, greeting.
If you are well, it is as we desire. We also are in good health.
Menelaus has informed us that you wish to return home and look after your own affairs.
Therefore those who go home by the thirtieth of Xanthicus will have our pledge of friendship and full permission
for the Jews to enjoy their own food and laws, just as formerly, and none of them shall be molested in any way for what he may have done in ignorance.
And I have sent Menelaus to encourage you.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth."
The Romans also sent them a letter, which read thus: "Quintus Memmius and Titus Manius, ambassadors of the Romans, to the people of the Jews, greeting.
With regard to what Lysias the kinsman of the king has granted you, we also give consent.

But as for the matters which he decided are to be referred to the king, as soon as you have considered them, send some one promptly, so that we may make proposals appropriate for you, for we are on our way to Antioch.
Therefore make haste and send some men, so that we may have your decision.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth."

The Jews were compelled to abandon their ancestral laws and customs, and to defile the temple with heathen practices, being forced to forsake the law of God, so that by this sign as it were they might testify that they had apostatized from the law and from God to Antiochus and his gods.
Was dragged to punishment (in Greek, "to the tympanum," of which see verse 19). Josephus adds in book 1 On...

Cornelius à Lapide commentary on 2 Maccabees 6: [On the torture of Eleazar] His teeth were set against him, so that they might break his resolve — they apply fire to him, and torture him stretched upon dreadful instruments, and pour the foul stench of his burning flesh into his nostrils. He, already burned to the bone and on the point of giving up his soul, with eyes lifted up to God, said: "Thou knowest, O God, that when I might have escaped, I choose to die in the torments of fire for the sake of the Law. Wherefore be merciful to Thy people, content with our punishment on their behalf. Grant that my blood may be an expiation for them, and in place of their life accept mine." Having spoken thus, this holy man died nobly. So Josephus. These things do not altogether agree with the tympanum, unless we say that those condemned to the tympanum were customarily tortured not only with cudgels but also with fires and every manner of punishment, which is clear from authors and the Acts of the Martyrs. Hence Lipsius, in his book on tortures, says: "The tympanum comprises all these kinds of torment."

I ENDURE SEVERE PAINS OF BODY, BUT IN MY SOUL I WILLINGLY SUFFER THESE THINGS FOR FEAR OF THEE. # GEM This martyrdom of Eleazar was therefore illustrious and glorious, who as a kind of leader went before the seven brothers and all the others toward that same end. Wherefore he himself is called by Gregory Nazianzen the "firstfruits of the martyrs of the Old Testament," just as Stephen is of the New. For even though before Eleazar, Abel, Isaiah, Zechariah, Jeremiah, and the other prophets had suffered martyrdom, nevertheless Eleazar was the first who expressly for the true faith in God and religion, publicly opposing Antiochus who wished to subvert it, underwent the solemn contest of martyrdom.

Hear Gregory Nazianzen: "This Eleazar is the firstfruits of those who suffered before Christ, just as Stephen is of those who suffered after Christ — a man who was a priest and of great age, gray both in hair and in wisdom, who had previously offered sacrifices and prayers on behalf of the people, but now offered even himself to God, a most perfect victim and expiation for the whole people, a blessed foretaste of the contest, an exhortation both in speaking and in silence; and moreover offering seven sons, the fruit of his upbringing, a living sacrifice, holy, pleasing to God, more glorious and pure than any legal sacrifice. For it is most just and right to ascribe the virtues of children to their parents." Hear also the praises which are rightly given to Eleazar.

Cornelius à Lapide here introduces his commentary on 2 Maccabees chapter 6, treating the persecution of the Jews under Antiochus Epiphanes, who compelled them to abandon the Law of Moses and adopt Greek customs.

The text of the eulogy on Eleazar reads: "O holy priest! you did not defile those sacred teeth, nor did you contaminate the belly, that guest of religion, with the eating of unclean things. O harmonizer of the Law, and zealous student of the divine word! Such ought they to be who discharge the sacred ministry of the Law with their own blood, and resist the pains of death with noble sweat."

Lapide continues the eulogy: "You, O father, have gloriously confirmed by your patience the goodness of our laws, and have established holiness by the gravity of your words, and have gained credence for the sayings of divine discipline by your deeds. O old man, more powerful than torture, more ardent than fire in your old age, and greater than your passions, O Eleazar! O rare old age, what a faithful seal of death you have completed!"
CHAPTER SEVEN. SYNOPSIS: Seven brothers, the Maccabees, urged on by their most worthy mother, most steadfastly overcome the torments of Antiochus and win the crown of martyrdom.

It came to pass also that seven brothers together with their mother were apprehended, and compelled by the king to eat swine's flesh against the law, being tormented with scourges and cords. Lapide notes that these seven brothers are commonly called the Maccabee martyrs, celebrated throughout the Church, whose feast is observed on the first of August.

Lapide observes that the mother is here named before the children in dignity, though the children are named first in the narrative order, because she surpassed them all in fortitude of soul, encouraging each of them to martyrdom, as will appear below.

But one of them who was the first said: What wouldst thou ask, and learn of us? we are ready to die rather than to transgress the laws of God received from our fathers. Lapide notes that this first brother speaks on behalf of all with great boldness, declaring their common resolve to die rather than violate the divine law, showing that true fortitude despises death for the sake of virtue.

Then the king being angry, commanded frying pans and brazen caldrons to be made hot: which forthwith being heated. Lapide remarks that Antiochus, unable to break their constancy by threats, resorts to the most savage tortures, demonstrating that tyrants, when overcome by words, turn to violence.

He commanded to cut out the tongue of him that had spoken first, and the skin of his head being drawn off, to cut off also the extremities of his hands and feet, the rest of his brethren and his mother looking on. Lapide notes the particular cruelty of compelling the other brothers and the mother to watch, so that their courage might be broken by the spectacle of their brother's agony; yet this only increased their admiration and their own resolve.

And when he was now disabled in all things, he commanded him to be brought to the fire, and to be fried in the pan while he was yet alive; and as the fume of the pan spread abroad, they and their mother also exhorted one another to die manfully. Lapide observes that the martyrs mutually encouraged one another, so that fraternal charity served as a new instrument of martyrdom, each being strengthened by the example of the others.

Saying: The Lord God will look upon the truth, and will take comfort in us, as Moses declared in the profession of the canticle: And in his servants he will take comfort. Lapide identifies this citation as from Deuteronomy 32:36, where Moses prophesies that God will vindicate His servants; the martyrs thus draw strength from Sacred Scripture, showing that knowledge of God's word sustains souls in extremity. # GEM

So when the first was dead after this manner, they brought the next to make him a mocking stock; and when they had pulled off the skin of his head with the hair, they asked him if he would eat, before he were punished throughout the whole body in every limb. Lapide notes the refinement of cruelty in the interrogation, designed to offer a final opportunity for apostasy while the victim beheld the fate of his brother.

But he answered in his own country's language, and said: I will not do it. Wherefore he also, in the next place, received the torments of the first. Lapide praises the brevity and firmness of this answer, noting that true constancy needs few words; the briefness itself is a form of contempt for the tyrant's power.

And when he was at the last gasp, he said thus: Thou indeed, O most wicked man, destroyest us out of this present life: but the King of the world will raise us up, who die for his laws, in the resurrection of eternal life. Lapide emphasizes this as a clear testimony to the resurrection of the body and to eternal life, drawn from divine faith; he notes that this confession of resurrection is one of the most explicit in the Old Testament. # GEM

After him the third was made a mocking stock, and when he was required, he quickly put forth his tongue, and courageously stretched out his hands. Lapide observes the alacrity and joy with which this third brother offers the very members that were to be cut off, showing that the martyrs had risen above natural fear through supernatural grace.

And with confidence said: These I have from heaven, but for the laws of God I now despise them, and from him I hope to receive them again. Lapide notes that here the third brother explicitly professes his belief in the resurrection and the restoration of the body, which is a clear teaching of the resurrection of the flesh against those who deny it. # GEM

So that the king, and they that were with him, wondered at the young man's courage, because he esteemed the torments as nothing. Lapide remarks that even the pagan persecutors were compelled to admire the supernatural courage of the martyrs, and that this admiration itself serves as a testimony to the truth of the faith for which they suffered.

And after he was dead, they tormented the fourth in the like manner. Lapide notes the relentless progression of the torture, each martyrdom serving both as witness and as exhortation to the remaining brothers.

And when he was now ready to die, he spoke thus: It is better, being put to death by men, to look for hope from God, to be raised up again by him: for, as to thee, thou shalt have no resurrection unto life. Lapide observes that the fourth brother not only confesses the resurrection of the just but also pronounces the condemnation of Antiochus, declaring that the tyrant shall have no share in the resurrection unto life, which Lapide interprets as denying him the resurrection to glory, not denying the resurrection to judgment. # GEM
And when they had brought the fifth, they tormented him. Lapide notes the brevity of the text here, the sacred writer hastening through the successive martyrdoms, each confirming the same truths.

But he looking upon the king, said: Whereas thou hast power among men, though thou art corruptible, thou dost what thou wilt: but think not that our nation is forsaken by God. Lapide comments that the fifth brother draws a contrast between the transitory power of the tyrant, who is corruptible and mortal, and the eternal power of God who protects His people; this is a great lesson on the vanity of earthly power. # GEM

But stay patiently a while, and thou shalt see his great power, in what manner he will torment thee and thy seed. Lapide notes this as a prophecy of the punishment of Antiochus, which was indeed fulfilled in the terrible death described later in this same book.

After him they brought the sixth, and he dying said: Be not deceived without cause: for we suffer these things for ourselves, having sinned against our God, and things worthy of admiration are done to us. Lapide observes that the sixth brother humbly acknowledges that the sufferings of Israel are a divine chastisement for their own sins, yet also a cause of wonder in that God turns punishment into an occasion of glory for His faithful servants.

But do not think that thou shalt escape unpunished, for that thou hast attempted to fight against God. Lapide notes the prophetic boldness of this dying declaration, warning Antiochus that his war against God's people is a war against God Himself, and therefore must end in his own ruin.

But the mother was to be admired above measure, and worthy to be remembered by good men, who beheld seven sons dying within the space of one day, and bore it with a good courage, for the hope that she had in God. Lapide extols the mother as surpassing all the martyrs in fortitude, because she suffered in each son's death and yet remained steadfast in all seven, comparing her to a general who inspires his soldiers while himself bearing the greater burden of command. # GEM

And she bravely exhorted every one of them in her own language, filled with wisdom: and joining a man's heart to a woman's thought. Lapide explains that "a man's heart" (animum masculinum) refers to her virile and unconquered fortitude of soul, which transcended the natural weakness of her sex; this is grace elevating nature to heroic virtue.

She said to them: I know not how you were formed in my womb: for I neither gave you breath, nor soul, nor life, neither did I frame the limbs of every one of you. Lapide draws from this verse a clear teaching on the immediate creation of the rational soul by God, not by the parents, and on God's providential forming of each member of the body in the womb; this passage is used against those who would attribute too much to secondary causes in the origin of human life. # GEM

But the Creator of the world, that formed the nativity of man, and that found out the origin of all, he will restore to you again in his mercy both breath and life, as now you despise these for the sake of his laws. Lapide comments that here the mother grounds her exhortation to martyrdom in the explicit doctrine of bodily resurrection, which is to be effected by the Creator who first formed these bodies; this is a most clear Old Testament testimony to the resurrection of the flesh. # GEM

Now Antiochus, thinking himself despised, and withal despising the voice of the upbraider, when the youngest was yet alive, did not only exhort him by words, but also assured him with an oath, that he would make him a rich and a happy man, and, if he would turn from the laws of his fathers, would take him for a friend, and furnish him with things necessary. Lapide observes that when violence fails, the tyrant turns to flattery, promises, and oaths, showing that persecution employs both cruelty and seduction; he warns that the temptations of pleasure and wealth are often more dangerous to the soul than open torments.

But when the young man was not moved with these things, the king called the mother, and counselled her to deal with the young man to save himself. Lapide notes that Antiochus here makes use of maternal affection as an instrument of temptation, calculating that love of a mother for her youngest and last remaining child would succeed where threats and promises had failed.

Lapide begins his commentary on verse 26, where the mother is urged by the king to counsel her son to apostatize, noting that this represents the supreme test of her virtue, in which maternal love and divine love are placed in direct opposition.

The satellites brought forward into the midst the eldest of them, as commanded, and tearing his tunic, bound his hands and arms on both sides. Then after they had beaten him with scourges to the point of exhaustion, achieving nothing, they fastened him to a wheel: stretched upon which, the young man was dismembered, all his limbs broken, and he cursed the tyrant in this manner: "Most wicked tyrant, enemy of heavenly justice and cruel one, you torture me in this way not for any murder or impious deed, but for the defense of the divine Law." When the satellites urged him to promise that he would eat, that he might be freed from tortures, he said: "Your wheel is not so powerful, O impure ministers, as to destroy my reason. Cut my limbs, burn my flesh, and rack my joints; for through all these torments I will persuade you that the Hebrews alone are unconquered in virtue." As he spoke these words they applied fire beneath him, and stretched the wheel ever more and more, so that it was wholly drenched with blood, and the pile of the pyre was extinguished by drops of gore, and the axles of the machine overflowed with flesh. But that magnanimous youth, descended from Abraham, though now stripped of the framework of his bones, did not groan, but as though being transformed in the fire into immortality, bore his sufferings bravely. "Imitate me, brothers," he said, "and do not abandon the manner of my life, nor renounce the eager spirit of your brother. Fight the sacred and noble warfare for piety: by which may that just and ancestral providence of our God, made propitious to our people, inflict punishment upon the wicked tyrant." Having spoken thus, that holy young man breathed his last.
THE REST TOGETHER WITH THEIR MOTHER EXHORTED ONE ANOTHER TO DIE BRAVELY, saying to one another,

[General introduction to ch. 13] Lapide here transitions to commentary on the Maccabean martyrs' mutual exhortation: "Brothers, let us die for the Law; let us imitate those three young men in Assyria who despised that vast furnace; let us not grow soft in displaying our piety." One said to another: "Be of good courage, brother." Another: "Endure nobly." Another, reminding the rest of their forebears, said: "Remember whence you come, and by whose father's hand Isaac suffered himself to be sacrificed for piety." Finally all, looking upon one another joyfully and very eagerly, said: "Let us consecrate ourselves with whole heart to God who gave us our souls, and let us spend our bodies in keeping the law." And shortly after: "Thus shall Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob receive us when we are dead, and all the fathers shall praise us." Then each of those brothers who remained, as they were being torn away, exhorted thus: "Do not disgrace us, brother, nor degenerate from our brothers who have already died."

[On "God will look upon the truth" (v. 6)] "God will look upon the truth" means God will consider and render what is just — namely, that we are innocent and just, and are unjustly tormented by you, O tyrant, and therefore He will crown us with the laurel of martyrdom. "He will comfort in us" means He will be our aid and consolation, pouring His consolations into us, that we may nobly overcome you and your torments, as Moses sang in the Canticle of Deuteronomy 32:36, promising that God would do this.

[On the second martyr, v. 8 — "with a paternal voice"] "Answering with a paternal (that is, manly) voice" means he responded not with a child's thin voice but with the strong, full voice of a father and man, as described in v. 21. He said (the second, named Aber): "I will not do this, I will not eat swine's flesh. This is my firm purpose, this my steadfast will." Hear Josephus adding much and adorning the martyr with his eloquence — perhaps also, as is his custom, adding and embellishing some things from his own invention: "The guards bring forward the second in age and, fastening iron hands with sharp claws upon him, bind him to a catapult (which is a machine). Then, having asked him before torturing whether he wished to escape, when they heard his noble reply, beginning from the soles of his feet, they tore all his flesh with those iron hands up to his chin, and lacerated the skin of his head with leopard-like savagery. He, enduring that most grievous pain, said: 'Every kind of death is sweet for the religion of our fatherland. Do you not think, O most cruel of all tyrants, that you are tormented more than I, when you see this cruel system of tyranny overcome by our patience for piety? For I ease pain with the pleasures of virtue; you are tormented in the threats of your impiety. Nor will you escape, most impure tyrant, the punishments of divine wrath.'" Following Josephus, St. Ambrose (De Jacob et Vita Beata, bk. 2, ch. 11) says: "The second came forward and, not unworthy of his brother, fulfilled the duties of holy confession. And when the membrane of his head was being stripped off, he answered: 'You take away the membrane indeed, but I have a spiritual helmet which you cannot take away.'" # GEM And truly no one can take away this helmet, as the Apostle afterward taught in the Church of the Lord, because Christ is the head of man and we are His members. Rightly did this boy, taught by the divine Spirit, foresee this Apostolic doctrine. The savage beasts stripped the skin of his head and raged with leopard-like ferocity. And he, at the point of death, said: "How sweet it is to die for religion! How pleasant is every bitterness of death for piety! because the reward of these labors remains. Your torments, O king, are the more grievous; you are vehemently tormented by your own punishments, because you see yourself overcome in power."

[On the third martyr, v. 10 — "the third is mocked"] "After this the third is mocked, and being asked, quickly put out his tongue and steadfastly extended his hands" — that with the tongue which had despised the king, and the hands might be cut off and fried in the pan. St. Cyprian (De Exhortatione Martyrii) presses upon "he extended his hands" and says: "Most blessed in this kind of punishment, he steadfastly extended his hands to be cut off — he to whom it fell, with hands extended for punishment, to imitate after a fashion the suffering of the Lord." # GEM

[On the third martyr's confession, v. 11 — "from heaven I possess these"] "And with confidence he said: 'From heaven I possess these, but for the laws of God I now despise these very things, since I hope to receive them back from Him.'" Josephus (ch. 10) adds: "But he cried out: 'Do you not know that I was begotten by the same father and born of the same mother as these who are dead, and that we were reared in the same household? I will not renounce so noble a brotherhood.' Then they, indignant at the man's liberty, disjointed his hands and feet with tearing machines, and loosening his joints, tore him limb from limb and broke his fingers and arms and legs and elbows. And when they could in no way destroy him, they tore away the skin together with the tips of his fingers. Then they led him immediately to the wheel; fastened upon it, as he was being disjointed joint by joint, he saw his flesh being rent and drops of blood dripping from his entrails, and now about to die he said: 'We indeed, O most impure tyrant, suffer these things for the discipline and virtue of God; but you for your impiety and unspeakable murders will bear immense tortures.'" Following Josephus, St. Ambrose (De Jacob et Vita Beata, ch. 11) says that this third, while being tortured, said: "I will not do your will, I will not yield to your command. By the blessed passion of my brothers and their noble life I will not deny our holy brotherhood. Apply whatever punishments you will, press harder — you will gain this much by the harshness of your penalties: that you receive greater testimonies of our brotherhood." He ordered therefore that his tongue be cut off. And he cried out: "You are defeated, Antiochus, who command the instrument of voice to be cut off. You have confessed that you cannot answer reason, and you prove the scourges of our tongue greater than your blows. For we do not fear your blows; you cannot endure the scourging of our voice. But these are the scourges of piety; your scourges are those of faithlessness. But even when removed, it will scourge you the more heavily with its falling murmur. Do you think you escape, Antiochus, if you take away the voice? God hears those who are silent, and hears them more. Behold I have opened my mouth, I have loosed my tongue — cut off the tongue; but you will not cut off constancy, you will not take away virtue, you will not blot out reason, you will not wrest away the testimony of truth, you will not wrest away the cry of the heart. If the tongue is cut off, the blood will cry out, and it will be said to you: 'The voice of your brother's blood cries to me.' And shortly after: wounds are more eloquent; even if wounds are covered, even if the scar is hidden, faith is not hidden. Yet do not applaud yourself that by removing the tongue you take away the confession of praise. For I have sufficiently praised God with speech; now let us praise Him with suffering." # GEM

[On the fourth martyr, v. 13] "And when he had thus died, they vexed the fourth." These later brothers suffered more than the earlier ones: First, because the king's anger boiled more fiercely against them on account of the many sharp reproaches the earlier ones had hurled at the king. Second, because these were younger in age, and therefore weaker in body and spirit. Third, because in watching their brothers so cruelly tortured they suffered the same things through compassion. Fourth, because they saw themselves about to suffer the same things shortly. Thus they were tormented more grievously by the harsh torture of their brothers and the prolonged anticipation and expectation of their own torture. Hence St. Leo, in his sermon on the Nativity of the Seven Maccabees, says: "The king's impiety devised this, that he might promise himself victory even from the first."

The reasons why the Maccabean martyrs are celebrated with such devotion are many: first, because they were the first to fight openly and publicly for the true faith and worship of God; second, because they did so in the Old Testament, when faith and knowledge of God, Christ, and the future life and resurrection were obscure; third, because they suffered in tender and youthful age, when life is most pleasant and death most bitter; fourth, because their persecutor and torturer was Antiochus Epiphanes, the most savage and cruel tyrant; fifth, because they resisted him most bravely, and each one rebuked him sharply and called him most wicked; sixth, because they endured torments of every kind.

Learn from these Jews, O Christian, what you must do in any temptation, persecution, or affliction—which is far less than theirs—and how nobly you ought to overcome it: "You have not yet resisted unto blood" (Heb. 12). Who stretched you on the tympanum and beat you? Who boiled you in a pot? Who fried you in a pan? as happened to these seven brothers, who nevertheless endured all things with brave and cheerful spirit, so that you in your lesser trials might do the same in hope of blessed eternity.

The spurs to enduring and overcoming all these things nobly were, for the Maccabees: first, faith and hope in Christ soon to come, from whose foreseen merits God gave them abundant grace, fortitude, and constancy; second, hope of a better life and of blessed resurrection, namely that the members they exposed to torments would be restored to them by God in the resurrection, splendid and glorious; third, that the suffering would be brief and moderate, while the reward in heaven would be immense and eternal.

This animated all martyrs in even the most bitter torments; whence St. Sebastian strengthened St. Mark and Marcellian, wavering at the weeping of their parents, with this dilemma: The torments to be inflicted on you are either severe or light. If severe, they will quickly bring death and send you to heaven. If light, they will be easy to bear. Therefore endure either bravely, for brief pain prepares eternal joy for you. For the moment that tortures is brief; the joy that delights is eternal.

Fourth, because their mother bravely encouraged each one and sharply spurred them to persevere in their ancestral religion; fifth, the example of the old man Eleazar, shown in the dreadful torments of the tympanum in the preceding chapter.

The mother is called Salome in the Greek Calendar; Salomona by Josephus; Anna, meaning grace, by Gorionides and Genebrard; Machabaea by St. Thomas, because she was the mother of the Maccabees. Again by Josephus (ch. 14) she is called Abraa, because, he says, she was mindful of holy Abraham's endurance, and as Abraham offered his Isaac, so she offered her seven sons to God. Josephus celebrates her with wonderful praises: "Not so does the moon stand beautiful among the stars in heaven, as you, who illumined seven stars—namely your seven sons—to the glory of God, stand glorious before God, and are fixed in heaven together with them."

Whence in ch. 15, admiring her strength, he exclaims: "O mother of the nation, champion of the Law, defender of piety, and victress of the contest fought through your very bowels! O more noble in continence than men, and stronger in patience than warriors. For as the ark of Noah in that universal flood of the whole world, bearing the pure, steadfastly endured the waves: so you also, that guardian of the law, when overwhelmed on all sides by the flood of affections and beaten as by violent winds of torments, bore admirably the contest undertaken for piety." # GEM

Wherefore St. Cyprian (De exhortatione ad martyrium): "She offered to God as great a martyrdom by the virtue of her eyes as her sons offered by the torments and suffering of their members." And St. Chrysostom (Hom. 14 on the words of Isaiah): "The mother of the martyrs herself performed martyrdom seven times; for while they were being tortured, she was receiving the blow." # GEM

St. Ambrose (De Officiis, bk. 1, ch. 41): "She looked joyfully upon as many funerals of her sons as there were trophies, and was more delighted by the voices of the dying than by the chanting of psalms; beholding the most beautiful harp of her womb—strung, as it were, with seven strings—in her sons, and the harmony of piety sweeter than all the music of the lyre." # GEM

St. Gregory Nazianzen (Orat. 22), admiring this virtue beyond measure: "O truly a man's soul in a woman's body! O sacrifice greater and more excellent than Abraham's sacrifice! For he offered one with ready spirit, but she consecrated to God a whole people of sons." # GEM

She encouraged each of them in their ancestral tongue—that is, Hebrew, says Josephus. Salianus prefers to interpret "ancestral voice" as brave and manly, such as is customary for a father's voice; as if to say, she encouraged them not with a maternal and feminine voice, but with a paternal and virile one. Hence the Greek πατρίδος is derived not from "country" but from "father," meaning "paternal"; she spoke manfully, as a father, not effeminately, as a mother.

Wherefore the text adds in explanation: "filled with fortitude" (in Greek, with nobility), "and stirring up her womanly thought with a manly spirit." Hear Nazianzen (Orat. 22) painting graphically the feelings and gestures of this heroine: "That vigorous mother, truly the parent of men endowed with such great virtue, was affected at once with joy and fear, and stood on the border of two emotions: for as she took joy from their fortitude and what she saw, so again when she pondered the uncertain outcome of the fight and the incredible magnitude of the torments, she was tormented by fear." # GEM

And so she hovered around them like a little bird when a serpent or some other treacherous beast seizes her chicks, crying out, beseeching, striving together with them, leaving nothing unsaid or undone to make them readier and more eager for victory. She caught drops of blood, received fragments of limbs, venerated the relics, gathered one, offered another, prepared a third, cried out to all: 'Well done, my sons, well done, my brave soldiers, well done, almost incorporeal in your bodies, well done, defence and bulwark of the law and of my aged years and of the city that nourished you. A little longer and we have conquered; the executioners grow weary. This one thing I fear. A little longer, and I shall be blessed among women, and you blessed among the young. But perhaps longing for your mother troubles you? I will not abandon you. This I promise you, I do not hate my children so...' # GEM
Antiochus was tormented by the such great constancy of the martyrs, because he could not overcome them but was defeated by each one in turn.
Lysias, the king's guardian and kinsman, who had charge of the affairs of state, was greatly displeased at what had happened.
He gathered about eighty thousand infantry and all his cavalry and came against the Jews, intending to make Jerusalem a Greek settlement.
He also intended to levy tribute on the temple, as he did on the other sacred places of the Gentiles, and to put the high priesthood up for sale every year.
He took no account whatever of the power of God, but was elated with his ten thousands of infantry, his thousands of cavalry, and his eighty elephants.
So he invaded Judea, approached Beth-zur, which was a fortified place about five leagues from Jerusalem, and pressed it hard.
When Maccabeus and his men got word that Lysias was besieging the strongholds, they and all the people, with lamentations and tears, besought the Lord to send a good angel to save Israel.
Maccabeus himself was the first to take up arms, and he urged the others to risk their lives with him to aid their kindred. Then they eagerly rushed off together.
And there, while they were still near Jerusalem, a horseman appeared at their head, clothed in white and brandishing weapons of gold.
And together they all praised the merciful God, and were strengthened in heart, ready to assail not only humans but the most savage beasts or even walls of iron.
They advanced in battle order, having their heavenly ally, for the Lord had mercy on them.
They hurled themselves like lions against the enemy, and laid low eleven thousand infantry and sixteen hundred cavalry, and forced all the rest to flee.
Most of them got away stripped and wounded, and Lysias himself escaped only by shameful flight.
As he was not without intelligence, he pondered over the defeat that had befallen him, and realized that the Hebrews were invincible because the mighty God fought on their side. So he sent to them
and persuaded them to settle everything on just terms, promising that he would persuade the king, constraining him to be their friend.
Maccabeus, having regard for the common good, agreed to all that Lysias urged. For the king granted every request that Maccabeus delivered to Lysias in writing concerning the Jews.
The letter written to the Jews by Lysias was to this effect: "Lysias to the people of the Jews, greetings.
John and Absalom, who were sent by you, have delivered your signed communication and have asked about the matters indicated therein.
I have informed the king of everything that needed to be brought before him, and he has agreed to what was possible.
If you will maintain your goodwill toward the government, I will endeavor in the future to help promote your welfare.
And concerning these matters and their details, I have ordered these men and my representatives to confer with you.
Farewell. The one hundred forty-eighth year, Dioscorinthius twenty-fourth."
The king's letter ran thus: "King Antiochus to his brother Lysias, greetings.
Now that our father has gone on to the gods, we desire that the subjects of the kingdom be undisturbed in caring for their own affairs.
We have heard that the Jews do not consent to our father's policy concerning Greek customs but prefer their own way of living and ask that their own customs be allowed them.

Accordingly, since we choose that this nation also should be free from disturbance, our decision is that their temple be restored to them and that they shall live according to the customs of their ancestors.
You will do well, therefore, to send word to them and give them pledges of friendship, so that they may know our policy and be of good cheer and go on happily in the conduct of their own affairs."
To the Jews the king sent the following communication: "King Antiochus to the council of the Jews and to the other Jews, greetings.
If you are in good health, it is as we desire. We also are in good health.
Menelaus has informed us that you wish to return home and look after your own affairs.
Therefore those who go home by the thirtieth day of Xanthicus will have our pledge of friendship and full permission
for the Jews to enjoy their own food and laws, just as formerly, and none of them shall be molested in any way for what may have been done in ignorance.
And I have also sent Menelaus to encourage you.
Farewell. The one hundred forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth."
The Romans also sent them a letter, which read thus: "Quintus Memmius and Titus Manius, envoys of the Romans, to the people of the Jews, greetings.
With regard to what Lysias the kinsman of the king has granted you, we also give our consent.

But as for the matters that he decided are to be referred to the king, as soon as you have considered them, send someone promptly, so that we may make proposals appropriate for you. For we are on our way to Antioch.
Therefore make haste and send some people, so that we may make known what is your preference.
Farewell. The one hundred forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth."

Finally, after her sons, the mother also perished. The Greek has ἐτελεύτησε, that is, she died. But by what kind of death? First, some think she died from immense joy, seeing all her sons triumph so gloriously over the tyrant. So Marius Victorinus in his poem on the Maccabees: "While she endured these things," he says, "joy dissolved the mother; and now, as she was already weakened by her woes, now with voice denied, sighing, and collapsing into the hands of her own people, she fell lifeless, and her loosened limbs found rest; thus the very mother of the Saints was received into her portion." Similarly, certain Roman mothers were struck dead by joy when they suddenly saw sons whom they had heard had fallen in battle against Hannibal, as the witness records.

The martyrs surpassed men in their fortitude and alacrity; for as has been rightly said: "Greater virtue reigned in their small bodies, and divine virtue is perfected in human weakness." In the small, the great Christ conquers and triumphs; and therefore He Himself chose the weak things of the world to confound the strong, so that he who glories should glory not in himself but in the Lord (1 Cor. 1). # GEM

Gorionides relates that this woman also asked death from God, and obtained it. "This woman," he says, "standing strong upon their corpses, spread out her palms toward heaven and said: O God most high, God of ages, receive me, I pray, with my sons, me thy handmaid, into the place which thou hast prepared for them. And when she had said these things, she gave up her soul, and her spirit departed, and she herself fell upon her sons, and died with them; and so at last a most blessed mother was joined to her most faithful sons." Third, it is more probable that she was fried in the pan like her sons, for this is what "was consumed" signifies; and this was demanded both by her own magnanimity and her magnanimous mockery of the tyrant, and also by the tyrant's own indignation and fury against her. Josephus adds that she threw herself into the fire prepared for her, as did St. Apollonia by the instinct of God. Hear Josephus from his Greek text: "At last, when all her sons had been consumed, the admirable parent of so many athletes, suddenly falling to her knees in the bloody carnage of their punishments, entreated God for the dissolution of her body. For it was not out of desire for life, but only for the sake of her sons that she had prolonged so long a time. The most devoted mother therefore received seven triumphs. Antiochus gnashes his teeth, orders the noble mother to be afflicted, is tyrannically carried away, she is stripped of her tender limbs, her hands are bound to another's, she is more savagely mocked with scourges, mourns with her breasts beaten, and thrown into the fire-breathing pan she willingly accompanies her burned sons into punishment."

Who, I ask, would deny the blessedness of her who, as though surrounded by seven walls, felt no stroke of death amid the bodies of her sons? Who, I say, would doubt her blessedness, who, encompassed by seven towers, lifted her head into the seat of paradise; who, encircled by seven sons, led into the heavenly altars a most sacred choir to God, not only melodious with voices, but also harmonious in sufferings for singing the praises of the Lord? How good is this offspring of faith, how safe this harbor of piety, how splendid this lamp of the Church, shining with sevenfold light, and with an eighth womb supplying oil to all the lights! Wherefore St. Augustine speaks of her in Sermon 109 on various matters, chapter 6: Let women learn from the great patience and ineffable virtue of that mother, who knew how to preserve her sons — she had been accustomed to have those whom she did not fear to lose — each one feeling it individually in himself, while she suffered it in all by watching. Made the mother of seven martyrs, herself seven times a martyr, not separated from her sons by watching and...
The mother said to her son: "There Christ will preserve you, whence Antiochus cannot take you." Again, St. Ambrose, book 7, On Jacob,
# GEM St. Ambrose (lib. 7, De Jacob) draws out the heroic faith of the Maccabean mother, showing she trusted Christ to preserve her son beyond the reach of Antiochus in the resurrection.

Judas Maccabaeus and those with him were going secretly into the strongholds, and calling together their kinsmen and friends, and those who had remained in Judaism, they gathered six thousand men to themselves.
And they called upon the Lord to look upon the people who were trodden down by all, and to have mercy on the temple which was defiled by the impious;
to have mercy also on the city which was being utterly destroyed and was about to be leveled to the ground, and to hear the voice of blood crying to him;
and to remember also the most wicked deaths of innocent little ones, and the blasphemies committed against his name, and to be indignant at these things.
But Maccabaeus, having gathered the multitude together, became intolerable to the Gentiles, the wrath of the Lord having turned to mercy.
And coming upon towns and cities unawares, he burned them; and occupying advantageous positions, he put to flight not a few of the enemy.
But especially he was drawn to such excursions by night, and the fame of his valor was spread everywhere.

Now Philip, seeing that the man was gaining ground little by little, and that things were succeeding with him more frequently and prosperously, wrote to Ptolemy, governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, to send aid to the king's affairs.

And he quickly sent Nicanor, son of Patroclus, one of his chief friends, giving him no fewer than twenty thousand armed men of mixed nations, to wipe out the whole race of Judaea; and he joined with him also Gorgias, a military man and one very experienced in the affairs of war.
Nicanor determined to make up for the king the tribute due to the Romans, two thousand talents, out of the captivity of the Jews.

And immediately he sent to the cities on the seacoast, inviting them to buy Jewish slaves, promising to sell ninety slaves for one talent, not reflecting on the vengeance that was to follow him from the Almighty.
But when Judas learned of this, he told the Jews who were with him of Nicanor's coming.
And some of them, being afraid and distrusting the justice of God, fled away.
Others sold all that they had left, and withal besought the Lord to deliver them from the impious Nicanor, who had sold them before he came near them;
and if not for their own sake, yet for the sake of the covenant made with their fathers, and because his holy and magnificent name had been invoked upon them.

But Maccabaeus, having called together seven thousand who were with him, urged them not to be reconciled to the enemies, and not to fear the multitude of enemies who came unjustly against them, but to fight bravely,
keeping before their eyes the injury unjustly done to the holy place by those men, and likewise the abuse of the city that had been mocked, and furthermore the overturning of the ancestral customs.
For, said he, they trust in arms and bold deeds, but we trust in the Almighty God, who is able with a nod to cast down both those who come against us and the whole world.

JUDAS VERO MACCABAEUS, etc., EDUXERUNT AD SE SEX MILLIA VIRORUM.] With these few troops he overthrew the greatest armies of Antiochus Epiphanes, both because these men were like desperados fighting for hearth and home, and because they entered battle with great hope and invocation of God; wherefore God added courage and strength to them, while taking it away from the enemy.
Et vocem sanguinis (of the innocent Jews slain by Antiochus, as we heard in c. 7 and the preceding) ad se (with mute voice, like Abel against the murderer Cain) clamantis audirit.]
column4: The blood of the innocent slain Jews cried to God silently, as Abel's blood cried against Cain.

column3: AT MACHABAEUS CONGREGATA MULTITUDINE INTOLERABILIS (in Greek ἀνυπόστατος, impassable, intolerable, whom none could tolerate, whose arms and strength no one could withstand, but all must yield and succumb) GENTIBUS EFFICIERATUR; IRA ENIM DOMINI (with which He had until now chastised the sin of the Jews through Antiochus) IN MISERICORDIAM CONVERSA EST] so that through Judas He would make the Jews, formerly conquered and trampled by Antiochus, his conquerors and those who would trample him. This is what the last of the Maccabees, when tortured the previous year, had foretold prophetically to Antiochus, c. 7. v. 38: In me, he said, and in my brothers shall the wrath of the Almighty cease. For that this might come to pass, he with his brothers merited it by their most heroic endurance of martyrdom, by which they both expiated the sins of the Jews before God and converted His wrath into mercy and grace; by which it came about that God raised up Judas Maccabaeus, adding to him courage and strength by which he routed the forces of Antiochus everywhere. # GEM

ET SUPERVENIENS CASTELLIS ET CIVITATIBUS IMPROVISUS, SUCCENDEBAT EAS.] For this is the stratagem of distinguished military commanders, to fall upon enemies unexpectedly, and thus by night upon unarmed and unprepared enemies, even those buried in sleep and wine, and thus to slaughter them as it were like cattle, as Judas did here. Hannibal did the same to the Romans, and conversely the Romans to Hannibal, as witnessed by Livy, Justin, Plutarch and others.

VIDENS AUTEM PHILIPPUS] who had been appointed by Antiochus as governor of Judaea, seeing that Judas was prevailing everywhere, wrote to Ptolemy the commander of Coele-Syria and Phoenicia for help against the Jews.

The preceding seven verses of this chapter are, in order of time and events, to be appended to verse 1 of c. 3 of book 1; the remaining verses to the end of the chapter seem to be inserted between v. 37 and 38 of the same c. 3. Until now we have heard of the passive fortitude of the Jewish martyrs; now until the end of the book we shall hear of the active fortitude of the Jewish soldiers, namely of Judas Maccabaeus and his brothers. It was said to be Roman to do brave deeds, but it is equally Maccabean as it is Christian both to endure such hardships and to do brave deeds.

Judas assembled his men and exhorted them not to fear the enemy's numbers nor their violent assault, but to fight bravely; for they trusted not in arms or boldness, but in the omnipotent Lord who can destroy both those coming against them and the whole world with a single nod.

ADMONUIT AUTEM EOS ET DE AUXILIIS DEI, QUAE FACTA SUNT ERGA PARENTES; ET QUOD SUB SENNACHERIB CENTUM OCTOGINTA QUINQUE MILLIA PERIERUNT.] He reminded them of the divine assistance given to their fathers, and how under Sennacherib 185,000 perished in a single night, struck down by the angel of the Lord.

ET DE PRAELIO QUOD EIS ADVERSUS GALATAS FUIT IN BABYLONIA, UT OMNES, UBI AD REM VENTUM EST, MACEDONIBUS SOCIIS HAESITANTIBUS, IPSI SEX MILLIA SOLI PEREMERUNT CENTUM VIGINTI MILLIA, PROPTER AUXILIUM ILLIS DATUM DE COELO.] He recalled the battle against the Galatians in Babylonia, where when it came to the engagement, while their Macedonian allies hesitated, six thousand Jews alone slew 120,000, on account of the help given to them from heaven, and received many benefits on account of this.
HIS VERBIS CONSTANTES EFFECTI SUNT, ET PRO LEGIBUS ET PATRIA MORI PARATI.] By these words they were made steadfast, and ready to die for the laws and their fatherland.

CONSTITUIT ITAQUE FRATRES SUOS DUCES UTRIQUE ORDINI, SIMONEM, ET JOSEPHUM, ET JONATHAN, SUBJECTIS UNICUIQUE MILLENIS ET QUINGENTENIS.] He appointed his brothers as commanders of each division—Simon, Joseph, and Jonathan—with fifteen hundred men assigned to each.

AD HOC ETIAM AB ESDRA LECTO ILLIS SANCTO LIBRO, ET DATO SIGNO ADJUTORII DEI, IN PRIMA ACIE IPSE DUX COMMISIT CUM NICANORE.] Moreover, after Esdras had read to them from the holy book, and the watchword "God's help" had been given, Judas himself as commander engaged Nicanor in the front line.

ET FACTO SIBI ADJUTORE OMNIPOTENTE, INTERFECERUNT SUPER NOVEM MILLIA HOMINUM; MAJOREM AUTEM PARTEM EXERCITUS NICANORIS VULNERIBUS DEBILEM FACTAM FUGERE COMPULERUNT.] With the Almighty as their helper, they killed more than nine thousand men, and compelled the greater part of Nicanor's army, weakened by wounds, to flee.

PECUNIIS VERO EORUM, QUI AD EMPTIONEM IPSORUM VENERANT, SUBLATIS, IPSOS USQUEQUAQUE PERSECUTI SUNT.] They seized the money of those who had come to buy them as slaves, and pursued them in every direction.

SED REVERSI SUNT HORA CONCLUSI; NAM ERAT ANTE SABBATUM: QUAM OB CAUSAM NON PERSEVERAVERUNT INSEQUENTES.] But they turned back, being constrained by the hour, for it was the eve of the Sabbath; for which reason they did not continue the pursuit.

ARMA AUTEM IPSORUM ET SPOLIA CONGREGANTES, SABBATUM AGEBANT; BENEDICENTES DOMINUM QUI LIBERAVIT EOS IN ISTO DIE, MISERICORDIAE INITIUM STILLANS IN EOS.] Gathering the arms and spoils of the enemy, they observed the Sabbath, blessing the Lord who had delivered them on that day, who had begun to pour forth mercy upon them.

POST SABBATUM VERO DEBILIBUS, ET ORPHANIS, ET VIDUIS DIVISERUNT SPOLIA; ET RESIDUA IPSI CUM SUIS HABUERE.] After the Sabbath they divided the spoils with the weak, orphans, and widows; and what remained they kept for themselves and their own.

HIS ITAQUE GESTIS, ET COMMUNITER AB OMNIBUS FACTA OBSECRATIONE, MISERICORDEM DOMINUM POSTULABANT, UT IN FINEM SERVIS SUIS RECONCILIARETUR.] These things having been accomplished, and supplication having been made in common by all, they besought the merciful Lord that He might be fully reconciled to His servants.

ET EX HIS, QUI CUM TIMOTHEO ET BACCHIDE ERANT CONTRA SE CONTENDENTES, SUPER VIGINTI MILLIA INTERFECERUNT, ET MUNITIONES EXCELSAS OBTINUERUNT; ET PLURES PRAEDAS DIVISERUNT, AEQUAM PORTIONEM DEBILIBUS, PUPILLIS ET VIDUIS, SED ET SENIORIBUS, FACIENTES.] From those who were fighting against them with Timothy and Bacchides, they killed more than twenty thousand, took their high strongholds, and divided great plunder, making equal shares for the weak, orphans, widows, and even the elderly.

ET CUM ARMA EORUM DILIGENTER COLLEGISSENT, OMNIA COMPOSUERUNT IN LOCIS OPPORTUNIS, RESIDUA VERO SPOLIA JEROSOLYMAM DETULERUNT.] And when they had carefully collected the arms of the enemy, they stored all of them in convenient places; the remaining spoils they brought to Jerusalem.

ET PHILARCHEN, QUI CUM TIMOTHEO ERAT, INTERFECERUNT, VIRUM SCELESTUM, QUI IN MULTIS JUDAEOS AFFLIXERAT.] They also killed Philarches who was with Timothy, a wicked man who had greatly afflicted the Jews in many ways.

ET CUM EPINICIA AGERENT JEROSOLYMIS, EUM, QUI SACRAS JANUAS INCENDERAT, ID EST CALLISTHENEM, CUM IN QUODDAM DOMICILIUM REFUGISSET, INCENDERUNT, DIGNA EI MERCEDE PRO IMPIJETATIBUS SUIS REDDITA.] While celebrating their victory at Jerusalem, they burned Callisthenes, who had set fire to the sacred gates, as he had taken refuge in a certain dwelling—a fitting reward rendered to him for his impieties.
FACINOROSISSIMUS AUTEM NICANOR, QUI MILLE NEGOTIANTES AD JUDAEORUM VENDITIONEM ADDUXERAT.] But that most wicked Nicanor, who had brought a thousand merchants for the sale of the Jews as slaves—

HUMILIATUS AUXILIO DOMINI, AB HIS QUOS NULLOS EXISTIMAVERAT, DEPOSITA VESTE GLORIAE PER MEDITERRANEA FUGIENS, SOLUS VENIT ANTIOCHIAM, SUMMAM INFELICITATEM DE INTERITU SUI EXERCITUS CONSECUTUS.] humbled by the help of the Lord, by those whom he had regarded as nothing, stripping off his glorious garment and fleeing through the inland roads, came alone to Antioch, having obtained the greatest misfortune from the destruction of his army.

ET QUI PROMISERAT ROMANIS SE TRIBUTUM RESTITUERE DE CAPTIVITATE JEROSOLYMORUM, PRAEDICABAT NUNC PROTECTOREM DEUM HABERE JUDAEOS, ET OB IPSUM INVULNERABILES ESSE, EO QUOD SEQUERENTUR LEGES AB IPSO CONSTITUTAS.] And he who had promised the Romans that he would restore the tribute from the captivity of the people of Jerusalem, now proclaimed that the Jews had God as their protector, and that on His account they were invulnerable, because they followed the laws established by Him.

Ptolemy, prefect of Coele-Syria, fearing Judas's growing strength—daily augmented by victories, recruits, and supplies flowing to him as to a conqueror—sought help, and Ptolemy swiftly sent Nicanor son of Patroclus with no fewer than twenty thousand armed men drawn from mixed nations; but later far more were added to these, as is clear from ch. 3, vv. 38 and 42.

Nicanor, elated by such forces and devouring all the Jews in certain hope of victory, resolved that he would make up to King Antiochus the tribute owed to the Romans—for the Romans had defeated his father Antiochus the Great in war and condemned him to tribute, the sum of this tribute being two thousand talents—by means of the captivity of the Jews; that is, he would raise the greater part of this sum from the sale of Jews he intended to take captive. Hence he summoned the inhabitants of Tyre, Sidon, and neighboring cities to purchase Jewish slaves at the lowest price.

Promising to sell ninety slaves for a talent of silver, that is, for six hundred gold coins, so that each slave would cost no more than five and a half gold coins; whereas shortly before, Hyrcanus had sold a single young man for a talent, that is, five hundred gold coins, as Josephus attests in book 12, ch. 4. This indignity and contempt stung the Jews, so that they streamed in crowds to Judas's camp, resolved to fight to the death for the law and their liberty. Nicanor therefore acted imprudently in provoking the Jews, causing them to fight like men in despair, and so he was defeated and cut down by them.
Not believing in God's justice—that is, not believing that God would be the patron and avenger of the just and innocent cause of the Jews, and would cast down the unjust Nicanor through them.

Others, however, sold whatever remained to them, and with the proceeds purchased arms and provisions, so that joining Judas's camp they might fight courageously against Nicanor to the death for their fatherland and faith.

And if not for their own sake, yet for the covenant made with their fathers—that is, they besought the Lord to deliver them from Nicanor, not for their own merits, but for the sake of the Fathers—Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and others—with whom God had made a testament, that is, a covenant, promising to be their God and the God of their posterity, that is, their guardian and protector, if they in turn would worship him as their God.

Maccabeus having assembled seven thousand men—in Greek six thousand, for that number is named in vv. 1 and 22; but Judas himself led the seventh thousand, while his brothers led the remaining six thousand as commanders. So Salianus.

He urged them not to be reconciled with the enemies, but to wage against them an ἀσπόνδον πόλεμον, that is, an irreconcilable war, thereby fearful and formidable to the enemy—such as Hannibal waged against the Romans, who, as Plutarch relates in his life, was bound by oath when still a boy at the sacrificial altars by his father Hamilcar to be the enemy of the Romans as soon as age permitted. So too the Numantines in Spain, besieged by the Romans for fourteen years, refused to be reconciled with them, and destroyed forty thousand Romans; but at last, pressed by famine under Scipio, they burned all their possessions and themselves in a fire kindled in the forum. So Florus, book 2. Hasdrubal, prefect of Carthage besieged by Scipio, mutilated Roman captives on the walls—gouging out eyes, cutting off tongues, fingers, or feet—and cast them living over the cliffs, so as to leave the Carthaginians no hope of obtaining pardon from the Romans, but to place all hope of safety in arms alone, says Appianus. For war against the obstinate is hard and dangerous. Such a war must every faithful person wage throughout life against Lucifer, against the flesh and the world, against pride, anger, gluttony, and lust, admitting no terms of peace but engaging them continually in war of extermination.

And of the battle they had in Babylonia against the Galatians, when eight thousand Jews with four thousand Macedonians joined battle, and the Macedonians being hard pressed, the six thousand Jews alone slew one hundred twenty thousand. This seems to have occurred under Antiochus I, called Soter, son and successor of Seleucus I, king of Asia and Syria after Alexander. For, as Appianus narrates in his Syriaca, Antiochus repelled the Galatians invading Asia from Europe, aided by the Jews, for which both he and his son Antiochus surnamed Deus granted them the highest honors and privileges in thanksgiving, as Josephus in book 12, ch. 3, Justin in books 24 and 25, Plutarch in his life of Demetrius, and others relate. So Serarius, Mariana, and others.
He therefore appointed his brothers commanders over each division: Simon and Joseph—Joseph here is called by another name, John, in book 1, ch. 2, v. 2. So Serarius.

And besides this, having read to them from the holy book of Esdras—this Esdras was not the famous scribe who wrote the book of Esdras, but one far later than he, a priest or distinguished doctor of the law, who, in order to add courage and hope of victory to the soldiers about to go into battle, read to them from Deuteronomy ch. 28, v. 7, where God promises certain triumph over enemies to Jews who worship him rightly. And God himself had commanded this to be done before battle in Deut. 20, saying: "When the battle draws near, the priest shall stand before the battle line and speak thus: Hear, O Israel, do not fear, for the Lord your God is in your midst." The Greek text adds that Judas also appointed Eleazar, his fourth brother, as a military commander, assigning him likewise 1,500 soldiers; for thus he distributed six thousand soldiers among four brothers as commanders, assigning to each a quarter, that is, 1,500 soldiers. Then follows another statement in the Greek: that Judas, acting as both pontiff and supreme commander, as he had done before, read to the soldiers the book of Deuteronomy.

And having given the signal—that is, the military watchword of God's help. Judas gave his men this battle-watchword, by which allies could be distinguished from enemies: "God is the helper of his people." This was the omen and cause of all their victories—that Judas placed all hope of victory not in his own strength but in God's. So the Gentiles too often gave this battle-watchword: "God is with us," says Vegetius, book 3, ch. 3. So Cyrus's watchword in war was "Jupiter, companion and leader in battle," says Xenophon, book 3. Pompey's watchword at the battle of Pharsalus was "Hercules the Unconquered"; Julius Caesar's was "Venus the Victorious," as Appianus attests in book 2 on the civil wars. So Sanchez, Serarius, Salianus, and others.
The commander himself, Judas, engaged Nicanor.
And having made the Almighty their helper, they slew more than nine thousand men, the rest being put to flight or captured.

But they turned back, constrained by the hour—that is, when the evening hour preceding the Sabbath fell upon them, they were compelled and forbidden to pursue the fleeing enemy. For by the law they were obliged to rest on the Sabbath, which began from the preceding evening hour.

They divided the spoils among the disabled, orphans, and widows. See here the charity and generosity of Judas and his soldiers, by which in thanksgiving for the victory obtained from God they divided the spoils...
SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER 9: The disease and death of Antiochus Epiphanes is described at length, which was briefly described earlier.

Antiochus, returning from Persia after his shameful retreat from Persepolis, where he had attempted to plunder the temple of Diana (or Nanea), learns that his armies have been routed in Judaea by Judas Maccabaeus, and that his generals Nicanor and Timothy have been defeated. Filled with rage and pride, he resolves to take vengeance on the Jews and make Jerusalem a common graveyard.

He commanded his chariot-driver to drive without ceasing, yet divine judgment overtook him; for God the all-seeing struck him with an incurable and invisible plague. Scarcely had he spoken these arrogant words when he was seized by a painful torment of the bowels, with bitter internal suffering — a fitting punishment for one who had tormented the bowels of others.

As he was rushing headlong and with intolerable arrogance, divine vengeance laid hold of him suddenly with painful affliction in his bowels, and with sharp internal torments — a just retribution from God, who repays the proud according to their deeds. # GEM

Yet in his arrogance he did not desist from his fury, but swelled with pride the more, breathing fire in his rage against the Jews, and commanding that the journey be hastened. Thus the judgment of God often uses the very impetuosity of sinners as an instrument of their punishment.

But the Lord God of Israel struck him with an incurable and invisible blow. For as soon as he had finished his threatening words, he was seized with a pain of the bowels for which there was no cure, and sharp internal torments — just as he had tormented others, so was he now tormented himself. This is the law of divine talion.

He who had afflicted the bowels of others with unusual torments, his own bowels were wasted away, and this very justly. For he had tormented the bowels of others most cruelly by scourging and torturing them. Thus God renders to each according to their works. # GEM

Yet he did not at all desist from his arrogance, but was still more filled with pride. Breathing fire in his rage against the Jews, he ordered the journey to be hastened. But it came to pass that he fell from his chariot as it was rushing along, and by a severe fall was so injured in all the members of his body that he had to be carried in a litter.

Thus he who had thought to command the waves of the sea with his pride, and to weigh the heights of the mountains in a balance, was now brought to the ground and carried in a litter, making manifest to all the power of God. For he who but a little while before had seemed to touch the stars of heaven, could now endure no one to carry him, by reason of his intolerable stench.

So then worms swarmed out of the body of this man, and while he was still living in anguish and pain, his flesh fell off, and the filthiness of his smell was noisome to all the army. A wretched end befits a wretched life. This putrefaction of the living body was a singular divine punishment, visited also on Herod Agrippa, Sulla, Pherecydes, and others.

The man therefore who but a little before had thought he could reach the stars of heaven, no man could endure to carry for his intolerable stench. Here therefore divine justice is manifest: he who had been puffed up with pride unto the heavens was now cast down unto the depths and made an object of universal horror and disgust.

Then it was that he himself, broken in spirit, began to abate much of his arrogance, being brought to knowledge by the scourge of God, his pains increasing every moment. He who had never before humbled himself now began to know himself by force of pain. Pain is often the beginning of conversion.

And when he could not endure his own stench, he said: It is just to be subject to God, and that a mortal man should not think equal things with God. This was a most remarkable confession wrested from him by pain, though even so it was not a true conversion of the will but only an acknowledgment forced by suffering.

Then this wicked man prayed to the Lord, of whom he was not to obtain mercy. Note that God does not always hear the prayers even of the dying and penitent, if their penance is not sincere but only servile fear. The prayer of Antiochus was not true penance but only the desire to escape punishment.

He made many promises, namely that he would make Jerusalem free, which before he was hastening to make a common burying place; that he would make all the Jews equal to the citizens of Athens. These promises made under duress carry no moral weight, for a vow extorted by fear and self-interest is not acceptable to God unless confirmed by subsequent free will.

And the holy temple which before he had spoiled, he would adorn with goodly gifts; and would restore all the holy vessels, multiplied many times over; and the charges necessary for the sacrifices he would supply out of his own revenues. Note the extravagance of promises made in extremity — promises he had no intention of keeping had he recovered.

And besides all this he would also become a Jew himself, and would go through every place of the earth, and declare the power of God. An extraordinary promise — that the most bitter persecutor of the Jews would become a Jew himself. But such deathbed conversions, wrung from fear alone, are not to be trusted.

But all these words were spoken vainly, for the pains of his bowels did not cease, which was a sign that his penance was not accepted by God. For when God truly accepts penance He frequently grants some alleviation of temporal punishment as a sign thereof, as He did with Ahab and with Manasseh.

And when he could not now endure his own stench, he wrote to the Jews the following letter in the form of a supplication. He who had formerly written edicts breathing menace and tyranny, now writes as a suppliant. So greatly does suffering humble the proud. The letter follows verbatim, as a document of historical and moral interest.

The letter which he wrote to the Jews was as follows: To the good Jews his citizens, Antiochus their king and general, much health and happiness and welfare. Note how he has changed his tone from proud tyrant to humble petitioner, styling them his citizens and wishing them health, when but lately he had been their tormentor.

If you and your children are well, and your affairs are according to your mind, I give very great thanks to God, having my hope in heaven. He now acknowledges a heavenly power which he had previously despised. This is the typical language of those who turn to God only when pressed by necessity.

As for me, being weak, but yet kindly remembering your honour and good will, returning out of the places of Persia and being taken with a grievous disease, I thought it necessary to take care for the common good. He disguises his disease and the shameful circumstances of his retreat with diplomatic language. He calls his mortal affliction merely a grievous disease.

Not distrusting my health, but having great hope to escape the sickness. He still flatters himself with hope of recovery, which shows that his conversion was not absolute. A soul that truly believes itself dying and truly repents does not preoccupy itself with hopes of recovery.

But considering that my father also, at what time he led an army into the higher countries, appointed who should reign after him: This is a reference to Antiochus III the Great, who designated his successor before his Persian campaign. Antiochus Epiphanes now does the same, appointing his son Eupator.

To the end, that if any thing contrary to expectation should fall out, or any ill tidings should come, they that were in the countries, knowing to whom the government was left, might not be troubled. He provides for an orderly succession so that his kingdom should not fall into chaos at his death — a prudent political arrangement, though made under compulsion.

Moreover considering that the neighbouring princes, and borderers upon my kingdom, watch opportunities, and expect what shall be the event, I have appointed my son Antiochus king, whom I often committed and recommended to many of you, when I went into the higher provinces; and I have written to him what is written below. He names his son Antiochus Eupator as successor. This political testament is here interpolated into the letter.

Therefore I pray and request of you, that remembering the benefits you have received publicly and privately, you will each of you continue to be faithful to me and to my son. He appeals to past benefits and to gratitude, which is a natural human motive. But benefits conferred by tyrants are often only the bribes of self-interest. # GEM

For I am confident that he, following my counsel, will behave himself with clemency and goodness, and will follow your desires. Thus Antiochus, though he has been a persecutor, now counsels his son to clemency. It is often the case that dying tyrants counsel moderation to their successors, having learned by experience the fruits of tyranny.

So this murderer and blasphemer, being struck with God's hand, and in a strange land, among the mountains, died in misery; having been brought back to Antioch, he died miserably. The sacred author now draws the narrative to its close with a solemn moral judgment on Antiochus: he is called murderer and blasphemer, and his end in a strange land is noted as part of his punishment.

And Philip his foster-brother carried off his body: and being afraid of the son of Antiochus, went into Egypt to Ptolemy Philometor. Philip, who had been left as regent, now fears the power of Eupator and flees to Egypt. Thus the instruments of tyranny often perish or are driven out after the tyrant's death. So ended Antiochus Epiphanes, one of the most notorious persecutors of the people of God before the coming of Christ.

Antiochus was returning dishonorably from Persia at the same time. For he had entered the city called Persepolis, and attempted to plunder the temple and to oppress the city; but when a great multitude rushed to arms, they were put to flight: and thus it came about that Antiochus returned shamefully after his flight.
And when he had come near Ecbatana, he learned what had happened concerning Nicanor and Timothy.

And being lifted up in anger, he thought he could turn the injury done by those who had put him to flight against the Jews; and therefore he commanded his chariot to be driven on, pressing forward without interruption, the heavenly judgment urging him on, because he had spoken so arrogantly that he would come to Jerusalem and make it a mass of sepulchres for the Jews.

But the Lord God of Israel, who looks upon all things, struck him with an incurable and invisible plague. For as soon as he had finished speaking these very words, a dreadful pain seized his bowels, and bitter torments of his inward parts;

and indeed justly enough, seeing that he had tormented the bowels of others with many strange tortures. # GEM Lapide notes the divine poetic justice: the same organs Antiochus used cruelty to destroy in others became the very seat of his own divine punishment, illustrating that God's judgments mirror the sins of the wicked with precise equity.

The death of Antiochus, briefly narrated in book 1, chapter 6, is here more fully recounted for the consolation of the Jews afflicted by him, and as a perpetual example of divine vengeance upon the impious and sacrilegious.

HE HAD ENTERED INTO IT (that is, the province or region and dominion of Persia) WHICH (from its chief city or metropolis) IS CALLED PERSEPOLIS in Greek; for in Hebrew it is called Elam or Elymais, as is clear from chapter 6, book 1, verse 1. Pererius, in book 9 on Daniel, at the words "And he shall be broken without hand," thinks this was Susa. The meaning is: Antiochus entered the dominion of Persepolis with his army and attempted to attack the chief city itself, which was likewise called Persepolis or Elymais; but he was repulsed by the citizens who rushed to the walls, and indeed suffered great slaughter of his men: for those who pursued him as he retreated caused him to lose very many men, and he retreated to Babylon in the manner of a fugitive, as Josephus says, book 12, chapter 13.

AND HE ATTEMPTED TO PLUNDER THE TEMPLE of Diana, which is the most august in those regions, says Pliny, book 6, chapters 26-27. So also Josephus, Polybius, and St. Jerome on Daniel chapter 11, verse 30, from Diodorus; though Appian in his Syriac history says it was the temple of Venus.

AND WHEN HE HAD COME NEAR ECBATANA (a most celebrated city of Persia) THE KING LEARNED (from messengers and letters sent to him) WHAT HAD BEEN DONE AGAINST NICANOR AND TIMOTHY (his generals) by Judas Maccabaeus, namely that he had routed, killed, and put them to flight.
