THAT HE WOULD COME TO JERUSALEM AND MAKE IT A MASS OF TOMBS FOR THE JEWS. In Greek: that he would make it a polyandreion, that is, a cemetery into which the corpses of many men are customarily heaped. The meaning is: Antiochus boasted that he would avenge the defeat of his men received at the hands of the Jews, and would overturn Jerusalem so utterly from its foundations that the whole city would become a sepulchre of Jewish funerals and corpses.

THE GOD OF ISRAEL STRUCK HIM WITH AN INCURABLE AND INVISIBLE PLAGUE, namely most bitter pains of the bowels, because he had tortured the bowels of others (the Maccabees) with novel torments, as follows, and with the most grievous melancholy and choler. St. Jerome adds in the cited place from Diodorus that he was driven to madness by certain phantasms and terrors. Externally moreover God struck him with the louse-disease, with a running of flesh, and with intolerable stench, as is described in verse 9.

COMMANDING THAT THE BUSINESS BE HASTENED (in driving his chariot so as to arrive quickly at Jerusalem), IT HAPPENED THAT AS HE WAS GOING AT SPEED HE FELL FROM THE CHARIOT AND HIS MEMBERS WERE AFFLICTED WITH A SEVERE BODILY COLLISION. Gorionides more fully and probably narrates the matter, book 3, chapter 22: It came to pass, he says, when he was already being conveyed and accompanying his army, that his chariot passed near a certain elephant, and the elephant began to trumpet, and from its cry the horses were terrified, kicked, and overturned the chariot, and Antiochus fell from the chariot and all his bones were broken, for he was a heavy and corpulent man.

SO THAT FROM THE BODY OF THE IMPIOUS MAN WORMS (lice) SWARMED FORTH. This disease is called by physicians phthiriasis, that is the louse-disease, by which these worms boil forth from the body and devour and consume it with foul horror and intolerable itching. It arises sometimes naturally from a delicate and sweet bodily constitution or nutrition, or from filth and putrefaction, as happens in the poor; but often God inflicts it supernaturally upon the proud, blasphemers, the sacrilegious, and persecutors and torturers of the faithful and saints, as he inflicted it upon Herod the Ascalonite, who was a persecutor of Christ and murderer of the innocents, as Josephus testifies.

# GEM This same disease God inflicted upon Herod Agrippa, persecutor of the Apostles, as is related in Acts 12; and similarly upon the Emperor Galerius, persecutor of Christians, as Eusebius and Lactantius record. Thus divine justice repays persecutors of the holy in kind, consuming with worms those bodies that had wallowed in luxury and cruelty.

FROM THIS THEREFORE HE BEGAN, BROUGHT DOWN FROM HIS GREAT PRIDE, TO COME TO KNOWLEDGE OF HIMSELF, ADMONISHED BY THE DIVINE PLAGUE, THE PAINS INCREASING EVERY SINGLE MOMENT. Note that God's chastisements are ordered not only to punishment but to the conversion of sinners, even the most hardened, so that no one should despair of any person's conversion while life remains.

AND WHEN HE COULD NO LONGER BEAR HIS OWN STENCH, HE SAID: IT IS JUST TO BE SUBJECT TO GOD, AND FOR A MORTAL NOT TO THINK HIMSELF EQUAL TO GOD. A confession wrested by pain rather than freely offered by piety; yet it shows that even the most obstinate tyrants are at last compelled to acknowledge the power of God when divine punishment overtakes them.

THIS WICKED MAN PRAYED TO THE LORD, FROM WHOM HE WOULD OBTAIN NO MERCY. His prayer was not heard because it was not sincere repentance but was born of pain, despair, and fear of death rather than of true contrition and conversion of heart, as the sequel makes clear.

AND THE CITY TO WHICH HE WAS HASTENING TO COME IN ORDER TO LEVEL IT TO THE GROUND AND MAKE IT A MASS OF TOMBS, HE NOW DESIRES TO SET FREE. How great is the change that divine punishment works: he who threatened to make Jerusalem a cemetery now promises it liberty.

AND THE JEWS, WHOM HE HAD SAID HE WOULD NOT EVEN COUNT WORTHY OF BURIAL BUT WOULD GIVE OVER TO BE TORN BY BIRDS AND BEASTS, AND WOULD DESTROY WITH THEIR LITTLE ONES, HE NOW PROMISES TO MAKE EQUAL TO THE ATHENIANS. That is, he would grant them the same civic privileges and freedom that the Athenians enjoyed, who were most celebrated among all peoples for liberty and the rights of citizens.

AND THE HOLY TEMPLE WHICH HE HAD PREVIOUSLY PLUNDERED, HE WOULD ADORN WITH THE FINEST GIFTS, AND MULTIPLY THE SACRED VESSELS, AND PROVIDE FROM HIS OWN REVENUES THE EXPENSES PERTAINING TO THE SACRIFICES. All these promises are the vows of a man in desperation, not the resolutions of true piety; they were made under compulsion of pain and were never fulfilled.

BESIDES THIS, THAT HE HIMSELF WOULD BECOME A JEW AND WOULD TRAVEL THROUGH EVERY INHABITED PLACE OF THE EARTH AND PROCLAIM THE POWER OF GOD. This is the most extraordinary of all his promises: the arch-persecutor vows to become a proselyte and a preacher of the God of Israel. Yet God did not accept this forced conversion, for He looks upon the heart, not merely the words.

BUT THE PAINS NOT CEASING (FOR THE JUST JUDGMENT OF GOD HAD COME UPON HIM) HE IN DESPAIR WROTE TO THE JEWS A LETTER IN THE MANNER OF A SUPPLICATION CONTAINING THESE THINGS. He who had issued edicts of persecution now writes supplications; divine justice thus humbles the proud and makes the persecutor a petitioner.

TO THE EXCELLENT JEWISH CITIZENS, MUCH GREETING, AND GOOD HEALTH AND HAPPINESS, THE KING AND PRINCE ANTIOCHUS. Mark the extraordinary reversal: he who had decreed the extermination of the Jewish people now addresses them as excellent citizens and wishes them happiness.

IF YOU ARE IN GOOD HEALTH, AND YOUR CHILDREN, AND ALL THINGS ARE GOING ACCORDING TO YOUR MIND, WE GIVE GREATEST THANKS. The formulaic courtesy of this royal greeting stands in stark contrast to the murderous hatred he had shown toward this same people so shortly before.

AND I, PLACED IN INFIRMITY, BUT KINDLY MINDFUL OF YOU, RETURNING FROM THE REGIONS OF PERSIA AND SEIZED BY A GRAVE ILLNESS, JUDGED IT NECESSARY TO TAKE THOUGHT FOR THE COMMON WELFARE. He conceals the true gravity of his condition and presents his concern for succession as solicitude for the common good rather than as the desperation of a dying man.
NOT DESPAIRING OF MYSELF, BUT HAVING GREAT HOPE OF ESCAPING THE ILLNESS. A lie, for as verse 18 states, he wrote in despair; but tyrants dissemble their weakness even in their last moments.

CONSIDERING MOREOVER THAT EVEN MY FATHER, AT THE TIMES WHEN HE LED AN ARMY IN THE UPPER REGIONS, SHOWED WHO SHOULD TAKE UP THE LEADERSHIP AFTER HIM. He refers to Antiochus the Great, who designated his successor during his eastern campaigns, following the prudent custom of kings going to war in distant lands.

SO THAT IF ANYTHING CONTRARY SHOULD HAPPEN OR ANYTHING DIFFICULT BE REPORTED, THOSE WHO WERE IN THE REGIONS, KNOWING TO WHOM THE CHIEF AUTHORITY HAD BEEN LEFT, MIGHT NOT BE TROUBLED. This was indeed sound political prudence, to designate a successor in advance so that the kingdom would not fall into chaos upon the sudden death of its ruler.

IN ADDITION CONSIDERING THAT THE POWERFUL NEIGHBORS ON EVERY SIDE ARE WATCHING FOR OPPORTUNITIES AND AWAITING EVENTS, I HAVE DESIGNATED MY SON ANTIOCHUS AS KING, WHOM I OFTEN COMMITTED TO MANY OF YOU WHEN I HASTENED TO THE UPPER KINGDOMS, AND I HAVE WRITTEN TO HIM WHAT IS SUBJOINED. He designates Antiochus Eupator, who was then a young boy, as his successor and co-regent.

I THEREFORE BEG AND BESEECH YOU, REMEMBERING BENEFITS BOTH PUBLIC AND PRIVATE, THAT EACH ONE PRESERVE LOYALTY TO ME AND TO MY SON. A remarkable petition from one who had violated every obligation of good faith toward the Jewish people; he appeals to benefits he claims to have conferred, though his record was one of spoliation and persecution.

FOR I AM CONFIDENT THAT HE WILL ACT WITH MODERATION AND KINDNESS, AND FOLLOWING MY PURPOSE WILL BE GRACIOUS TO YOU. An ironic commendation: he recommends his son to follow his own purpose, which had been the destruction of Judaism. Either he deceives himself or he deliberately deceives the Jews.

SO THE MURDERER AND BLASPHEMER, MOST GRIEVOUSLY STRICKEN, AS HE HIMSELF HAD TREATED OTHERS, DIED A MISERABLE DEATH IN A STRANGE LAND IN THE MOUNTAINS. Divine justice rendered to him exactly what he had meted out to others: he who had inflicted miserable deaths upon the innocent died himself most miserably, far from home, his body consumed by worms and stench.

AND PHILIP HIS FOSTER-BROTHER WAS CARRYING HIS BODY, WHO FEARING THE SON OF ANTIOCHUS WENT TO PTOLEMY PHILOMETOR IN EGYPT. Philip had been appointed regent of the kingdom by the dying Antiochus (cf. 1 Mac 6:14-15), but when Antiochus Eupator came to power under the guardianship of Lysias, Philip found himself in danger and fled to Egypt, thus ending his charge of the royal corpse. So perishes the tyrant: his very funeral attendant abandons his cause and flees.

The prior part of this chapter, concerning the purification of the Temple (verses 1-9), must be placed in chronological order after 1 Maccabees 4:25, and the remainder of this chapter and chapter 11 must be inserted into 1 Maccabees chapter 16 before verse 18. For the purification of the Temple by Judas, although narrated here after the death of Antiochus Epiphanes, actually occurred before it, namely in the year 148 of the Greeks, as is clear from 1 Maccabees 4:52. Antiochus however died in the year 149 of the Greeks, as was said at 1 Maccabees 16:16.

[And having kindled fire from flint stones, they offered sacrifices.] By "kindled stones" Salianus and Mariana understand flints and similar stones, from which fire is easily struck when struck together and caught in tinder placed beneath, just as we do naturally every day. But the Greek reads πυρπολήσαντες λίθους, that is, "making the stones fiery" and catching fire from them. Flints when struck do not become fiery but only emit sparks. Therefore it seems more truly that by the prayers of Judas and the priests, fire was called down from heaven and ignited the stones, and from these the priests received the fire by which the sacrifices were burned. For thus Nehemiah had done before Judas, and thus God had ordained in Leviticus 6:12, that the sacrificial victims should be burned not by common fire but by heavenly and divine fire. For this reason Judas instituted the feast of the given, or rather restored, fire, as we heard in chapter 1:19 and following. So say Serarius, Sanchez and others. So also Gorionides, book 3, chapter 13: "When they had arranged the wood they found no sacred fire, but they cried out to the Lord, and fire leapt from a stone that was placed beneath the altar, and they added wood to it. That fire lasted until the third captivity, which was accomplished by Titus and Vespasian, when the Temple was burned."

[After two years.] This is to be reckoned not from the profanation of the Temple, as some will have it (for from that event three years had elapsed), but from the beginning of Judas's leadership, as I said at 1 Maccabees 4:54. So say Serarius, Salianus and others. It is remarkable that certain learned men compute this period of two years from the death of Antiochus; for Antiochus died after the purification and sacrifice of the Temple, as I said in the synopsis of that chapter.

[And they celebrated with joy for eight days after the manner of the feast of Tabernacles.] That is to say, they celebrated the feast of the Dedication (Encaenia) for eight days with the same rite and rejoicing with which, according to the law, they celebrate each year the feast of Tabernacles on the fifteenth day of the month Tishri, and this in thanksgiving.

[Therefore they carried thyrsus-rods and green branches and palms before him who had prospered the purification of his place.] They carried palm branches as signs of victory and thanksgiving, just as is customary at the feast of Tabernacles according to Leviticus 23:40.

[And they decreed by common precept and decree that the whole nation of the Jews should keep these days every year.] This is the feast of Dedication, called Encaenia or Hanukkah, which is celebrated to this day by the Jews for eight days beginning on the twenty-fifth of Chislev.

[And the end of Antiochus, who was called Noble (Epiphanes), was thus.] He is called Noble or Illustrious (Epiphanes) by way of derision, for he was truly Epimanes, that is, mad, as we explained in the first book.

[Now we will narrate what was done concerning Eupator, the son of the impious Antiochus, abbreviating the evils that were done in the wars.] Antiochus Eupator was nine years old when he came to the throne, and Lysias governed as regent in his name.

[For he, having received the kingdom, appointed over the affairs of the kingdom a certain Lysias, commander of the army of Phoenicia and Syria.] Lysias had already been appointed regent by Antiochus Epiphanes before his death, as related in 1 Maccabees 3:32.

[For Ptolemy, who was called Macron, resolved to maintain justice toward the Jews, and especially on account of the wrong that had been done to them, and to deal peaceably with them.] This Ptolemy Macron had formerly governed Cyprus under Ptolemy Philometor king of Egypt, but had defected to Antiochus Epiphanes, as stated below in verse 13.

[But on this account being accused before Eupator by his friends, since he was frequently called a traitor because he had abandoned Cyprus which had been entrusted to him by Philometor, and having gone over to Antiochus Epiphanes had also withdrawn from him, he ended his life by poison.] Thus this man, who had shown justice and mercy toward the Jews, perished by the treachery of those around him — a warning that earthly favor cannot protect those abandoned by the powerful.

[Now Gorgias, who was commander of those places, taking on foreign mercenaries, frequently made war upon the Jews.] This Gorgias had already appeared as an enemy of the Jews in 1 Maccabees 3:38 and 2 Maccabees 8:9, where he was defeated by Judas.

[But the Jews who held the advantageous strongholds received those who had been driven out from Jerusalem and attempted to carry on the war.] That is, the Jewish garrisons in the fortresses of Judea received as allies those Jews expelled from Jerusalem by the enemy.

[But those who were with Maccabaeus, entreating the Lord by prayers to be their helper, made an attack upon the strongholds of the Idumeans.] Note how Judas always begins with prayer before battle, teaching that divine assistance must be sought before human effort.

[And pressing on with great force, they gained possession of the places, drove back and slew all they encountered, and killed together no fewer than twenty thousand.] God thus rewarded their piety and prayer with a great victory over the Idumeans.

[But some had fled into two very well-fortified towers, having every equipment for resistance.] These towers served as the last refuge for those who had escaped the general slaughter of the Idumean strongholds.

[Maccabaeus, leaving Simon and Joseph and likewise Zacchaeus and those who were with them in sufficient number for the siege of these towers, himself turned to those battles that were more urgently pressing.] Judas wisely divides his forces, entrusting the siege to subordinates while he himself attends to more pressing engagements elsewhere.

[But those who were with Simon, led by greed, were persuaded by some of those in the towers with money; and having received seventy thousand drachmas, they allowed certain of them to escape.] This is a shameful example of avarice overcoming military duty and betraying the common cause for private gain.

[But when it was reported to Maccabaeus what had been done, having assembled the leaders of the people, he accused them of having sold their brothers for money, letting their enemies go free.] Judas rightly indicts these traitors before the assembly, for soldiers who betray their charge for money are traitors to their nation and to God.
[These traitors therefore he killed, and immediately seized the two towers.] Justice was swiftly executed: those who sold their duty for silver received death as their wages.

[And prospering in everything with arms and with hands, in the two strongholds he destroyed more than twenty thousand.] Thus divine favor attended Judas's arms, granting him victory not only over external enemies but enabling him to purge treachery from within his own ranks.

[But Timotheus, who had previously been defeated by the Jews, gathered a force of foreign multitude and assembled Asian cavalry, and came as if about to take Judea by arms.] This is the same Timothy who had been defeated before in chapter 8. His return shows the persistence of Israel's enemies, undeterred by past defeats.

[But Maccabaeus and those with him, as he approached, were beseeching the Lord, sprinkling earth upon their heads and girding their loins with sackcloth.] # GEM This is a notable example of the saints' manner of prayer in extremity: prostrate in body, humble in spirit, clothed in penitential sackcloth, they cast themselves entirely upon God's mercy. Augustine remarks that such external signs of humility truly express and also intensify interior contrition.

[Lying prostrate at the base of the altar, they prayed that he would be propitious to them, and be an enemy to their enemies and an adversary to their adversaries, as the law says.] The phrase "as the law says" refers to Exodus 23:22: "I will be an enemy to your enemies and will afflict those who afflict you." They thus ground their prayer in God's own covenantal promise, making Scripture itself the basis of their petition.

[And so after prayer, taking up their arms and advancing a considerable distance from the city, and having drawn near to the enemy, they encamped.] Note the proper order: first prayer, then action. They do not presume on God's help without seeking it, nor do they neglect human effort after obtaining the divine promise.

[At the first light of dawn both sides joined battle; the one side having the Lord as a guarantor of victory and success together with valor, while the others had only their own spirit as a guide in battle.] A pointed contrast: the Jews fight with God as their champion and pledge of victory; their enemies have only human courage and military spirit, which are insufficient against divine power.

[But when the battle was fierce, there appeared to the enemies from heaven five men on horses adorned with golden bridles, leading the Jews.] God visibly manifested his assistance through five heavenly warriors on horseback, a miracle confirming that this was no merely human conflict. Similar apparitions of heavenly warriors are recorded elsewhere in Scripture and history as signs of divine intervention on behalf of God's people.

[Two of these, with Maccabaeus between them and covering him with their armor, kept him safe and unharmed; but upon the adversaries they cast darts and thunderbolts, so that they fell, blinded with confusion and filled with disorder.] # GEM The protective encircling of Maccabaeus by two heavenly warriors prefigures the angelic guardianship of righteous leaders. Chrysostom notes that God often assigns special angelic protection to those who lead his people in defense of true religion.

[There were slain twenty thousand five hundred, and six hundred horsemen.] The exact numbers recorded here emphasize the completeness and miraculous scale of the victory granted by divine intervention.

[Timotheus however fled into the stronghold of Gazara, a well-fortified place, over which Chaereus was in command.] Timothy sought refuge in this fortress, trusting in its strength to preserve him from the victorious Maccabaeus.

[But Maccabaeus and those with him, rejoicing, besieged the stronghold for four days.] Even in the midst of victory and siege, the spirit of joy and thanksgiving characterized the Jewish forces, conscious that God had given them the victory.

[But those within, trusting in the strength of the place, blasphemed beyond measure and uttered wicked words.] Pride in their fortifications led the besieged to the further sin of blasphemy against God, thus adding spiritual guilt to their military guilt.

[But when the fifth day was dawning, twenty young men from those who were with Maccabaeus, inflamed in spirit because of the blasphemies, boldly approached the wall and with fierce courage climbed up.] Holy indignation at blasphemy against God moved these young men to an act of extraordinary valor. Their anger was righteous, not merely personal, being directed against the dishonor done to God.

[And others likewise climbing up began to set fire to the towers and gates, and to burn alive the blasphemers themselves.] Retributive justice is visible here: those who had blasphemed with their mouths perished by fire, a foreshadowing of the eternal fire prepared for the impenitent blasphemer.

[And after the stronghold had been ravaged for two continuous days, Timotheus, who was found hiding in a certain place, was killed; and his brother Chaereus and Apollophanes were slain.] The ignominious hiding of Timothy, once a proud commander of great armies, illustrates the fate of those who trust in military strength against God's chosen people.

[These things accomplished, they blessed the Lord with hymns and thanksgivings, who had done great things in Israel and had given them the victory.] As they began with prayer, so they end with thanksgiving — the perfect circle of the devout soul, which acknowledges God as both the source and the completion of all victory.

Ptolemy ended his life by poison. Some Greek manuscripts add that Ptolemy, through weakness of spirit, voluntarily resorted to poison and drank it of his own accord, because he could not bear to hear and endure the slander and infamy of treachery laid upon him. For it belongs not to a firm and great spirit, but to a weak and petty one, to be broken by men's taunts and therefore to kill oneself; it is the mark of courage to endure all these things manfully and to overcome them with magnanimity, according to that saying of Martial: "He acts bravely who is able to be wretched." Holy Scripture recounts this fate of Ptolemy to signify that the Jews were deprived of so great a friend and patron, and therefore became entangled in new wars with Eupator.

Gorgias, taking foreign troops. He calls "foreigners" the soldiers conscripted from Thrace, Egypt, Africa, and other foreign regions. Hence in v. 24 they are called the army of foreign multitude. For the native troops were Asiatic and Syrian.

The Jews who held the advantageous strongholds received those driven from Jerusalem and attempted to make war. That is to say, the Jews seized certain strongholds from Gorgias and the Antiochians, and into these they admitted their fellow Jews who were being driven from their homes in Jerusalem by the enemy; from among whom many flocked to Judas and swelled his forces, so that he might boldly attack the Antiochians and successfully overthrow them, as shortly after he laid low twenty thousand of them. So Sanchez. But in place of "Jews" it seems one should read "Idumaeans," as the Greek codices read: for these strongholds are called those of the Idumaeans in the following verse. For the author wishes to signify two enemies of the Jews, namely Gorgias and the Idumaeans. The sense therefore is: the Idumaeans, perpetual enemies of the Jews, occupied certain Jewish strongholds, into which they received Jewish apostates and deserters, namely those who fled to them from Jerusalem on account of apostasy and crimes, and with these men attempted to wage war and to bring war upon Jerusalem and the Jews. But Judas, having invoked God, rushed with armed force upon these Idumaean strongholds and slew twenty thousand, as follows. So Serarius, Salianus, and others.
Twenty thousand. The Greek has nine thousand.
Joseph. This is Joseph, brother of Judas, Jonathan, and Simon, mentioned in ch. 8:22.

But those who were with Simon, led by greed, accepted seventy thousand didrachms of silver (which if Attic amount to 5,833 gold pieces; if Hebrew, twice as much) and released certain of the enemies who were shut up in the towers to escape.

When it was reported to Maccabaeus, he accused them of having sold their brethren to the adversaries by releasing them. For one betrays the safety of citizens when captured enemies or those about to be captured are released; for once released they will again attack, plunder, and kill the citizens, especially in order to recover the ransom they paid for themselves.

But at the first rising of the sun both sides joined battle; the former (those with Judas) having the Lord as guarantor of victory and prosperity together with valour. For God inspired Judas and his men with certain hope of victory, which was increased by valour, that is the uprightness, courage, and nobility of Judas and his companions, who were prepared to fight for God's law and worship even unto death, and had resolved not to leave the battle except as victors, but either to conquer or to fall. The Greek reads: "Having as refuge unto the Lord a guarantor of victory and prosperity together with valour"; for these two things, namely invocation of God and one's own valour and noble effort of spirit toward battle or any other undertaking, are most efficacious remedies which as it were guarantee certain victory and happy outcome from God. But the others (the enemies of the Jews) had the spirit of war as their leader (Greek: thymon, that is, fieriness, anger, pride), because trusting in the number of their soldiers they despised the small number of the Jews and thought they would devour them like a morsel of bread; but God crushed their fieriness and pride, and showed that victory depends not on numbers but on God's favour.
There appeared to the adversaries five men on horses, adorned with golden bridles, leading the Jews.

Two of these kept Maccabaeus in the middle, surrounded by their own arms, and preserved him unharmed; against the adversaries they hurled javelins and thunderbolts, so that, confounded by blindness and filled with confusion, they fell. # GEM These five were Angels fighting for Judas and the faithful people by God's command. Thus Angels fought for Theodosius against Eugenius, turning back the weapons of his soldiers upon themselves, as Theodoret attests in book 5, ch. 24. Thus an Angel fought for Saint Wenceslas, as his Life records, when he was doing single combat with Radislaus, Duke of Kouřim, and struck the Duke so that he fell prostrate and begged for pardon and his life. Thus Angels fought for Constantine the Great, who, as the sixth Panegyric says, blazed with a certain awe-inspiring...

[On the recapture of Jerusalem and purification of the Temple] The Maccabees recovered the Temple and the city, and purified the sanctuary, making a new altar; and they celebrated the dedication of the altar for eight days with joy, offering burnt offerings and sacrifices of deliverance and praise.
[On the removal of pagan altars] They removed the altars which the foreigners had built in the marketplace, and also the sacred enclosures.

[On the purification of the Temple] Having purified the Temple, they made another altar; and striking fire from flints, they offered sacrifices after a lapse of two years, burned incense, lit lamps, and set out the showbread.

[On the prayer of the Jews after purification] When they had done these things, they fell prostrate and prayed the Lord that they might never again fall into such evils, but that, if ever they should sin, they might be chastened by him with clemency, and not handed over to blaspheming and barbarous heathen.

[On the date of the rededication] Now on the same day on which the sanctuary had been profaned by the foreigners, on that very day it came to pass that the purification of the sanctuary was made, on the five and twentieth day of the same month, which was Chislev.

[On the eight days of celebration] And they kept eight days with gladness in the manner of the feast of tabernacles, remembering how, not long before, during the feast of tabernacles they had been wandering in the mountains and in caves after the manner of wild beasts.

[On the carrying of thyrsus-rods and branches] Therefore, carrying wands wreathed with leaves, and fair boughs, and palms, they offered hymns to him who had given success to the purifying of his own holy place.
[On the public decree for the feast] They also ordained by a public decree, ratified by vote, that all the nation of the Jews should observe these days every year.
[On the death of Antiochus Epiphanes] Such then were the last days of Antiochus called Epiphanes.
[On the transition to Antiochus Eupator] Now will we declare the acts of Antiochus Eupator, who was the son of that ungodly man, and will give a brief summary of the calamities of the wars.
[On Lysias as regent] For this man, when he took over the kingdom, appointed one Lysias to be chancellor and supreme governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia.

[On Ptolemy Macron's justice toward the Jews] For Ptolemy that was called Macron, leading the way in showing justice to the Jews because of the wrong that had been done them, tried to maintain peaceful relations with them.

[On the accusation and death of Ptolemy Macron] Whereupon being accused before Eupator by the king's friends, and hearing himself called traitor at every turn, because he had abandoned Cyprus which had been entrusted to him by Philometor, and had gone over to Antiochus Epiphanes, he ended his life by poison, an ignoble end.

[On Gorgias as governor of Idumaea] But Gorgias, when he was governor of the district, maintained a force of mercenaries, and at every turn kept up war against the Jews. # GEM Lapide notes that God permits enemies to persist in order to exercise and strengthen the virtue of his people.

[On the Idumaeans as enemies] Together with him the Idumaeans, masters of important strongholds, were harassing the Jews; and receiving those who were banished from Jerusalem, they assayed to keep up the war.

[On Judas's prayer and attack on the Idumaean strongholds] But Maccabaeus and his men, having made solemn supplication and having besought God to fight on their side, rushed upon the strongholds of the Idumaeans.

[On the assault and victory] Assaulting them vigorously, they gained possession of the positions, kept off all who fought upon the wall, slaughtered those whom they encountered, and killed no fewer than twenty thousand.
[On the flight of the survivors into two towers] And because no fewer than nine thousand took refuge in two towers well fortified and having all things necessary for a siege.

[On Judas's departure and the assignment of Simon and Joseph] Maccabaeus, leaving Simon and Joseph, and moreover Zacchaeus and those with him, in sufficient number to besiege them, departed to places where he was more urgently needed.

[On the corruption of Simon and Joseph] But they that were with Simon, being led by covetousness, were persuaded by some of those who were in the towers, and on receiving seventy thousand drachmas let some of them slip away.

[On Judas's anger at the betrayal] But when word was brought to Maccabaeus of what had been done, he gathered the leaders of the people together, and accused those men of having sold their brothers for money, since they had let their enemies go free to fight against them.
[On the punishment of the traitors] So he killed these men as traitors, and immediately took the two towers.
[On further success] Having good success with his arms in all things he took in hand, he destroyed more than twenty thousand in the two strongholds.

[On Timothy's campaign against the Jews] Now Timothy, who had been defeated by the Jews before, having gathered together a tremendous body of foreign troops, and having collected the horsemen from Asia, came as though he would take Judaea by storm.
[On the Jews' prayer against Timothy] But as he drew near, Maccabaeus and his men sprinkled earth upon their heads and girded their loins with sackcloth, in supplication to God.

[On prostration before the altar] Falling down at the foot of the altar, they besought him to be merciful to them, and to be an enemy to their enemies, and an adversary to their adversaries, as the law declares.
[On the Jews' courage after prayer] And rising from their prayer they took up their arms, and advanced some distance from the city; and when they had come near to the enemy they halted.

[On the dawn attack] And when the dawn was now breaking, the two armies joined battle; the one having, beside their virtue, the pledge of success and victory from the Lord, the other taking fury for their captain.

[On the appearance of five heavenly horsemen] # GEM But when the battle was at its height, there appeared to the enemy from heaven five men on horses with golden bridles, leading on the Jews; and they surrounded Maccabaeus, and shielding him with their own armour, kept him invulnerable, while on the adversaries they shot arrows and thunderbolts, so that, bewildered and blinded, they were routed with great confusion. Lapide: these five angels appeared visibly as heavenly warriors to confirm that God fights for his people; the gleaming shields and terrible light of celestial armour signify divine glory and wrath against the impious.
[On the angelic aid] And twenty thousand five hundred were slaughtered, beside six hundred horsemen.
[On the flight of Timothy] But Timothy himself fled into a stronghold called Gazara, a very strong fortress, where Chereas was in command.

[On the siege of Gazara] Then Maccabaeus and his men were glad and besieged the fortress four days. Lapide: Gorionides thinks this is Gaza, but others, following Serarius, distinguish the two places. Note here the successive victories granted by God to the Jews, especially the slaughter of twenty thousand of Timothy's men, as shortly before twenty thousand Idumaeans had been slain, and finally Timothy himself along with Chereas and Apollophanes was killed.
[On those within relying on the strength of the position] And they that were within, relying on the strength of the place, blasphemed exceedingly, and hurled forth abominable words.

[On the impiety of the besieged] But when the fifth day dawned, twenty young men of Maccabaeus' company, inflamed with anger because of the blasphemies, assaulted the wall manfully and with fierce anger, and cut down whomever they encountered.

[On the twenty young men and the effect of youthful ardour] # GEM Lapide: Note here what the spirit and ardour of young men can accomplish. Note again how foolish and dangerous it is to provoke and inflame the spirits and anger of enemies by mocking and blaspheming them. So Alexander the Great, as Curtius relates near the end of Book 7, besieging a sheer and precipitous rock held by Arimazes the Sogdian with thirty thousand armed men, was mocked when he asked for surrender and taunted whether he could fly; stung by this taunt, Alexander said: 'I will show on the next night that Macedonians can also fly,' and so he inspired three hundred young men with great rewards and promises to climb the rock from the rear by night over inaccessible places, while he himself attacked from the front where a path existed. The young men, fired with enthusiasm, penetrated the rock with immense toil—in memory of this deed Alexander said: 'Nature has placed nothing so high that virtue cannot strive to reach it.' Terrified, Arimazes surrendered himself with his kinsmen to Alexander, who ordered them all to be flogged and crucified at the very foot of the rock.

[On others following up the assault] Others likewise, mounting after them, wheeled around against the defenders and set fire to the towers, kindled fires and burned the blasphemers alive; while others broke open the gates and let in the rest of the army, and captured the city.

[On the killing of Timothy] And they killed Timothy, who had hidden himself in a certain place; and his brother Chereas, and Apollophanes. Lapide: Timothy was found hidden in a certain place—the Greek text specifies this more fully.
[On the thanksgiving after victory] When they had accomplished these things, they blessed the Lord with hymns and thanksgivings, who had done great things for Israel, and given them the victory.
[On Lysias's expedition against the Jews] Now after a very little time Lysias, the king's guardian and kinsman, who had charge of the government, being greatly displeased at what had happened.
[On the size of Lysias's army] Having gathered together about fourscore thousand, and all his cavalry, he came against the Jews, thinking to make the city a habitation of Greeks.
[On Lysias's impious intentions regarding the Temple] And to make a gain of the temple, as of the other temples of the Gentiles, and to put up the high priesthood for sale every year.

[On Lysias's vain confidence in numbers] Taking no account at all of the power of God, but puffed up with his ten thousands of infantry, and his thousands of cavalry, and his fourscore elephants. Lapide: He nowhere reconsidered the power of God, but with unbridled mind trusted in the multitude of footsoldiers, horsemen, and eighty elephants.

[On Lysias's approach to Beth-zur] And he entered into Judaea, and drew near to Beth-zur, which was in a narrow place, at about five stades' distance from Jerusalem, and he pressed that fortress hard.

[On the prayer of Judas and the people] But when Maccabaeus and his company learned that he was besieging the strongholds, they and all the people with lamentation and tears besought the Lord to send a good angel to save Israel.
[On Maccabaeus arming and exhorting the others] And Maccabaeus himself first of all took up arms, and exhorted the others to jeopardize themselves together with him, and to succour their brethren.

[On the preceding context about exploring soldiers' sentiments] It is fitting to carefully inquire what the soldiers think. For confidence or fear is discerned from the face, words, gait, and movements. Nor should you trust if a raw recruit desires battle; for battle is sweet to the inexperienced, and you should know you must hold back if seasoned warriors fear to fight. Nevertheless, through the admonitions and speech of the commander, the army's virtue and spirit grows: especially if they receive such an account of the coming contest that they hope to attain victory easily. Then the cowardice or error of the enemies must be shown; or if they have previously been defeated by us, this should be recalled. It must also be said by what means the soldiers' minds may be moved to hatred of the adversaries, with anger and indignation.

THEY PROSTRATED OF THEM ELEVEN THOUSAND, AND SIXTEEN HUNDRED HORSEMEN, RUSHING UPON THE ENEMIES IN THE MANNER OF LIONS.] This battle is another and different from that which was narrated in Book 1, chapter 4, whence a different number of the slain is recorded there. For although Judas was not of the tribe of Judah, to whom this oracle was given by his father Jacob, but of the tribe of Levi, nevertheless he stood in the place of Judah as leader of all the Jews, to whom this oracle pertains.

TROPOLOGICAL: When Lysias, that is the devil, attacks you and suggests movements of anger, pride, lust, etc., resist and meet him like a lion, and you will immediately put him to flight; for the devil, before resolute and courageous souls, is as timid as an ant; but to the timid and hesitant he is a terrible lion. Hence St. Gregory, Moralia bk. 5, c. 17, on Job 4: "The tiger perished because it had no prey," citing the Septuagint who translate "tiger" as Myrmecoleon, that is the ant-lion, which, he says, is fierce against ants and kills them as they carry grain, and devours them once killed — applying this mystically to the devil: "Rightly," he says, "is it called myrmecoleon, that is lion and ant: for to ants, as we said, it is a lion; to the flying it is an ant, because the ancient enemy is strong against those who consent to him, but weak against those who resist. For if assent is given to his suggestions, he can scarcely be borne as a lion; but if he is resisted, he is crushed like an ant. To some therefore he is a lion, to others an ant; because carnal minds can scarcely bear his cruelty, but spiritual souls tread down his weakness under the foot of virtue." # GEM

And because he was not without sense — that is, Lysias was sensible and prudent — he easily recognized from so great a slaughter of his men that God through an Angel was fighting for the Jews, and therefore they were insurmountable. Wherefore he sought peace from them and obtained it.

AND CONCERNING THE REST (of the articles proposed by you) I HAVE GIVEN ORDER BY WORD FOR EVERY PARTICULAR BOTH TO THESE (namely to John and Absalom sent by you) AND TO THEM THAT ARE COME FROM ME (that is, my legates, whom I send to you together with yours) TO CONFER WITH YOU — so that they may transact each matter with you and give satisfaction in all things.

IN THE YEAR 148. You will say: In this year 148 of the Greeks, Antiochus Epiphanes was still living and reigning; how then are these letters written as if from Antiochus Eupator his son, as though already reigning after his father's death? For Epiphanes died in year 149 of the Greeks, as was said in book 1, chapter 6, verse 16.

I reply that the years of the Greeks are computed in two ways: first, from the twelfth year after the death of Alexander the Great, and from the month of March, because in that year — which was 442 from the founding of Rome and of Olympiad 117, as Eusebius, Ptolemy, St. Jerome, Josephus, Dio, Appian and others testify — and in that month of March, Seleucus, having defeated Antigonus king of lesser Asia, placed the diadem of all Asia upon himself. By this reckoning the Jews number the years of the Greeks or Seleucids, and therefore the first book of Maccabees used the same as the Egyptians, and for this reason these years of the Greeks are called now Jewish, now Macedonian, now Alexandrian.

Second, the years of the Greeks begin a year and a half later, perhaps because at that time Seleucus returned in triumph from lesser Asia into greater Asia or Syria; whence this reckoning of years is called Syrian, Chaldaic, and Antiochene. By this second reckoning these years begin in year 13 after the death of Alexander, in the seventh month Tishri, which corresponds to our September, that is a year and a half later than the former Jewish or Alexandrian years. The second book of Maccabees uses this reckoning. Hence in this place year 149 of the Greeks, in which

The year 148 in Greek reckoning: because the year which by the Jewish and Alexandrian computation was 147, was by the Antiochene or Chaldean computation 148. So Salianus, Serarius, and others. Scaliger answers differently, namely that it was year 148 if one begins the year from Nisan, that is March; and again the same year was 169 Greek if one begins the year from Tishri, that is September.

The month of Dioscorus: in Greek Dios Korinthiou, that is, of the Corinthian Jupiter. This month is not found among the ordinary Greek months, whence Scaliger and Salianus think it was an embolismic or intercalary month. But Serarius rightly refutes this, holding that Dioscorus is Dysrius, which corresponds to our March, in which month kings are accustomed to go forth to war.

King Antiochus to his brother Lysias, greeting: Lysias was the king's guardian. It is remarkable therefore that the king, who according to Appian was a boy of nine years, does not call him father rather than brother; but this was done deliberately by the king, or rather by the king's secretary, to conceal from the Jews the king's youth, lest he be despised by them on account of it: as though already grown and more mature, he calls Lysias brother, not father. Add also: he does not call Lysias father, because he shortly calls Antiochus Epiphanes his own father, and so adds:

Our father having been translated among the gods (Gorionides says: among the Angels): The flatterers of kings among the Gentiles pretended that kings after death were numbered among the gods. Hence at the funerals of kings and emperors their apotheosis, or enrollment among the gods, took place; with what ceremonies this was done among the Romans, Herodian elegantly describes at the beginning of book 4, where among other things he says that an eagle was accustomed to fly forth from the pyre, which carried the soul of Caesar to heaven to the gods. But the impious Antiochus Epiphanes, on account of his crimes and sacrileges, was translated not among the gods of heaven but of hell, namely among the demons.

That the Jews etc. request that there be granted to them their lawful customs: namely that it be permitted to them to live by their own laws and rites in their own faith and worship of the one God, and that they not be compelled to live after the manner of the Gentiles and worship their gods and idols.

We have decided that the temple be restored to them: Already Judas and the Jews had recovered the temple by arms and had taken it back. The king nevertheless here arrogates this to himself, to display his power over the Jews and his generosity, when in reality he does not restore the temple to them, but approves and confirms the restoration already made.

On the occasion of Philip's rebellion and his occupation of Antioch, Lysias, in the name of the king, sought peace from the Jews and obtained it, in order to subdue Philip. From this you may gather that this treaty did not last long, but was soon broken when war was renewed, as will be clear in the following chapter.

Thus the Turk easily makes peace with Christians when pressed by the Persian war or some similar conflict; but once that is quieted, he easily finds occasion to break the peace, if he judges war more advantageous to him than peace.

Menelaus approached us — not that pseudo-pontiff and betrayer of the Jews mentioned in chapter 4, verses 34 and following, as some would have it, but another Jew sent as envoy to the king and Lysias. So Salianus and others hold. He said that you wish to come down to your people who are with us, that is, that you might visit your Jewish kinsmen and friends who dwell with me in Syria.

To those therefore who travel up to the thirtieth day of the month Xanthicus — which immediately follows Dystrus and corresponds to our April — "we grant pledges of security." The king therefore gives the Jews 16 days of safe conduct (for on the 15th day of that same Xanthicus these royal letters were written, as is clear from verse 33), during which they may freely come down into Syria; because peace between him and the Jews had not yet been concluded, but was being negotiated through envoys with the Jews. Once concluded, it would be free for the Jews to travel into Syria at any time.

Let no one of them suffer any harm in any way concerning those things which were done through ignorance. The king decrees that whatever had hitherto been done in hostility between his people and the Jews should be regarded as excused, indeed blotted out, as having been committed, as it were, through ignorance.
We have also sent back Menelaus — sent to me by you — "to speak to you," and to report those things which he treated with me, and to announce to you my thoughts and words.

The Romans also sent a letter — namely the envoys sent from the Romans to Antiochus, who were to command him to burn the ships he had beyond the number prescribed by the Roman Senate and to kill the elephants, so as to remove from the Romans any fear or suspicion of war — as Appian narrates, and from him Sigonius at the year 591 of the city. These Roman legates therefore likewise sent a letter to the Jews, signifying that if they desired any other just conditions of peace with Antiochus, they should write them out and send them, for they would see to it that Antiochus would subscribe to them. For the Romans, as Salianus rightly observes, readily assisted all suppliants unjustly vexed — which was the first origin of their power and growth in Sicily, in Spain, in Greece, in Asia, in Africa, and among all peoples, as the course of Roman history up to this point demonstrates. When therefore the Jews had betaken themselves to the Roman legates who were at that time in Syria, and had perhaps already shown themselves desirous of their friendship and alliance, they asked them to confirm what had been conceded by Lysias and to transact certain other matters on behalf of their nation with the king.
After these covenants had been made, Lysias returned to the king, and the Jews applied themselves to agriculture.

But those who had remained behind — Timothy, and Apollonius the son of Gennaeus, and also Hieronymus, and Demophon over these, and Nicanor the Cypriarch — would not suffer them to live in silence and quiet.

The people of Joppa committed a crime of this kind: they invited the Jews who dwelt among them to embark on boats which they had prepared, together with their wives and children, as though there were no enmity between them.
By a common decree of the city, and with the Jews acquiescing, and suspecting nothing, because of the peace: when they had gone out into the deep, they drowned no fewer than two hundred of them.
When Judas learned of this cruelty done to men of his own nation, he gave orders to the men who were with him, and calling upon God the just judge,
he came against the murderers of his brethren, and set the harbor on fire by night, burned the boats, and put to the sword those who had fled from the fire.
And when he had done these things, he departed as if he would return again and root out all the Joppites.
But when he learned that those at Jamnia likewise intended to do the same to the Jews who dwelt among them,
he came upon the Jamnites also by night, and set fire to the harbor and the fleet, so that the light of the fire was visible at Jerusalem, two hundred and forty stadia distant.
When they had gone nine stadia from there and were marching toward Timothy, the Arabs attacked him with five thousand foot soldiers and five hundred horsemen.

After a fierce battle, in which the aid of God made things go well, the surviving Arabs, having been defeated, asked Judas to give them his right hand, promising to give him pastures and to assist him in other matters.
Judas, judging that they would indeed be useful in many respects, promised them peace; and having received the pledge of right hands, they withdrew to their tents.
He also attacked a certain strong city, surrounded by bridges and walls, inhabited by a mixed multitude of Gentiles, called Casphin.

But those who were within, trusting in the strength of the walls and the supply of provisions, behaved somewhat carelessly, provoking Judas with insults and blasphemies, and speaking things that were not lawful.
But Maccabaeus, calling upon the great Lord of the world, who without battering-rams and engines of war threw down Jericho in the time of Joshua, rushed fiercely against the walls;
and having taken the city by the will of the Lord, he made an immense slaughter, so that the adjoining lake, two stadia in width, appeared to flow with the blood of the slain.
From there they departed seven hundred and fifty stadia and came to Characa, to those Jews who are called Tubianeans.
They did not find Timothy in those parts; he had departed without accomplishing anything, having left behind a very strong garrison in a certain place.
But Dositheus and Sosipater, who were commanders under Maccabaeus, slew ten thousand men left by Timothy as a garrison.

And Maccabaeus, having marshaled six thousand men around himself and arranged them by cohorts, marched against Timothy, who had with him a hundred and twenty thousand foot soldiers and two thousand five hundred cavalry.

When Timothy learned of the approach of Judas, he sent ahead the women and children and the rest of the baggage to a fortress called Carnion, for it was impregnable and difficult of access because of the narrowness of the passes.
But when the first cohort of Judas appeared, terror fell upon the enemy at the presence of God who sees all things, and they turned to flight, one rushing this way
Judas pressed hard, punishing the profane, and laid low from them thirty thousand men.

Timotheus himself fell into the hands of Dositheus and Sosipater, and with many entreaties begged to be let go alive, because he held the parents and brothers of many among the Jews, by whose death it would come about that these would be deceived.
And when he had given his pledge that he would restore them according to agreement, they let him go unharmed for the sake of their brethren's safety.
But Judas marched to Carnion, where twenty-five thousand were slain.

After the flight and slaughter of these, he moved the army to Ephron, a fortified city, in which a multitude of various nations dwelt; and strong young men standing before the walls resisted boldly; and in it were many engines and a store of missiles.
But when they had called upon the Almighty, who by His power breaks the strength of enemies, they took the city, and laid low twenty-five thousand of those who were within.
From there they went to the city of the Scythians, which was six hundred stades from Jerusalem.
But the Jews who were among the Scythopolitans testifying that they were treated kindly by them, and that even in times of misfortune they had dealt with them with moderation:
giving thanks to them, and exhorting them also for the future to be well-disposed toward their nation, they came to Jerusalem, the solemn day of Weeks being at hand.
And after Pentecost they went against Gorgias, the commander of Idumaea.
He came out with three thousand foot-soldiers and four hundred horsemen.
When they engaged, it came to pass that a few of the Jews fell.

But a certain Dositheus, a horseman of Bacenor's company, a valiant man, had hold of Gorgias; and when he wished to take him alive, a certain horseman of the Thracians rushed upon him and cut off his shoulder; and so Gorgias escaped to Marisa.
But when those who were with Esdris had fought long and were weary, Judas called upon the Lord to show Himself their helper and leader of the battle;
then raising the battle-cry in his ancestral tongue, and with hymns lifting up the shout, he put Gorgias's soldiers to flight.
And Judas, having gathered his army, came to the city of Odollam; and as the seventh day was coming on, they purified themselves according to custom and kept the sabbath in the same place.
And on the following day, Judas came with his men to take up the bodies of the slain and to lay them with their kinsmen in their ancestral sepulchres.

But they found under the tunics of the slain some of the donated offerings of the idols that were at Jamnia, which the law forbids the Jews: it therefore became plain to all that this was the cause of their fall.
All therefore blessed the just judgment of the Lord, who had made hidden things manifest.

And so turning to prayers, they besought that the sin which had been committed might be consigned to oblivion. But the most valiant Judas exhorted the people to keep themselves free from sin, having before their eyes what had happened on account of the sins of those who had fallen. # GEM Lapide notes here that this passage powerfully confirms the Church's doctrine of Purgatory and of prayer for the dead, against those heretics who deny any intermediate state of purification after death, for Judas would not have prayed for the fallen if he believed their souls were utterly perished or already in fixed and final damnation.

And making a collection, he sent twelve thousand drachmas of silver to Jerusalem to be offered for a sacrifice for the sins of the dead, acting well and religiously in his thought of the resurrection.
For if he had not hoped that those who had fallen would rise again, it would have seemed superfluous and vain to pray for the dead.
And because he considered that those who had fallen asleep with godliness had laid up the best grace in store for them.

It is therefore a holy and wholesome thought to pray for the dead, that they may be loosed from their sins. # GEM Lapide observes that this verse is the clearest scriptural foundation for the Catholic doctrine of Purgatory: the dead prayed for are neither in heaven, where they would need no loosing, nor in hell, where loosing is impossible, but in a middle state of purification — Purgatory — whence they can be aided by the prayers and sacrifices of the living. This truth, received by the universal Church from the Apostles, is here confirmed by the Holy Spirit through the inspired author.

The inhabitants acted more insolently. The Greek has ἀναγωγότερον ἐχώρουν, which Vatablus renders: "they showed themselves more stubborn." For ἀνάγωγος means contumacious, stubborn, untamed, intractable, undisciplined, one who has lacked proper upbringing and was raised loosely and indulgently; and thus became more petulant, dissolute, intemperate, and impudent. For a lax and soft upbringing produces the contumacious, according to that saying of Eccl. 30:8: "An unbroken horse becomes hard, and an indulged son becomes headstrong." They acted more insolently, therefore, meaning that given over to their pleasures they neglected and despised Judas, and impudently insulted him, because they were protected by their walls and thought themselves impregnable.

He rushed fiercely against the walls, battering and overthrowing them by the stroke of a battering ram or ballista. Gorionides, however, asserts that Judas smeared the gates with pitch and heaped thorns from the desert against them, and so having set fire to the gate entered the city, and in the end wrought such slaughter in the city that a broad pool flowing in every direction for two stadia ran with blood as with water.

Thence they departed seven hundred and fifty stadia, which make ninety-three Italian miles with six stadia, that is nearly thirty French leagues. And they came to Characa to those who are called the Tubianean Jews. Salianus considers that this place was called Characa because it was fortified with a notable rampart, whether natural or man-made: for χάραξ means a rampart. The Tubianeans were named from the place Tubin which they inhabited, concerning which see Book 1, ch. 5:13. Therefore impertinent is what Rabanus writes, and from him the author taken for St. Thomas: "I have heard," he says, "a certain Hebrew arguing that the Tubianean Jews were so called because they were gathered from foreigners and came as helpers to Judas, and were called Tubianeans as if hired by foreign trumpets (tubis)." For the Tubianeans were Hebrews, not Latins; wherefore they were named not from tubae (trumpets) but from Tubin, perhaps from the Hebrew טוֹב tob, meaning good, as if Timothy had persecuted Jews who had received their name from distinguished goodness, and was therefore repressed by Judas. So Sanchez.
And two thousand five hundred horsemen. The Greek has fifteen hundred; but twenty thousand infantry easily required twenty-five hundred cavalry. So Vatablus, Salianus and others.

The stronghold (a fortress or fortified place) which is called Carnion, and therefore distinct from the city called Carnaim, meaning "of two horns" or "two-horned," concerning which see Book 1, ch. 5. Therefore the battle narrated here is different from that narrated there. If anyone should wish them to be the same, I shall not drag a contentious rope with him; indeed, all things duly considered, this opinion seems to me as probable as it does to Salianus.
Terror was struck into the enemies by the presence [of God]...
Judas Maccabaeus and his men, under God's leadership, recovered the temple and the city of Jerusalem.
They demolished the altars erected by foreigners in the public square, and likewise the sacred enclosures.

Having purified the temple, they made another altar; and striking fire from flints, they offered sacrifices for the first time after two years, burning incense, lighting lamps, and setting out the showbread.

When this was done, they fell prostrate and implored the Lord that they might no longer incur such great evils, but that if they ever sinned again, they might be chastened by Him with forbearance and not handed over to blaspheming and barbarous nations.
On the same day on which the temple had been profaned by foreigners, the purification of the temple came to pass, on the twenty-fifth day of the same month, which is Chislev.

They celebrated eight joyful days in the manner of the Feast of Tabernacles, remembering that not long before they had kept the feast of Tabernacles while living like wild animals in the mountains and in the caves.
Therefore bearing wands wreathed with leaves and fair boughs and palms, they offered hymns of praise to Him who had prospered the cleansing of His own place.
They also decreed by public ordinance and vote that the whole Jewish nation should celebrate these days every year.
Such was the end of Antiochus called Epiphanes.
Now we will describe the acts of Antiochus Eupator, the son of that wicked man, briefly summarizing the calamities of the wars.
For when he took over the kingdom, he appointed one Lysias as regent, the chief governor of Coele-Syria and Phoenicia.
Ptolemy, who was called Macron, took the lead in maintaining justice for the Jews, and especially because of the wrong that had been done to them, attempted to treat them peaceably.

On this account he was accused before Eupator by the king's friends; and as he heard himself called a traitor at every turn, because he had abandoned Cyprus which Philometor had entrusted to him, and had gone over to Antiochus Epiphanes, and, failing to maintain honorable conduct, had departed from that province, he took poison and ended his life.
But Gorgias, when he had become general of the district, maintained an army of foreign soldiers, and at every turn kept waging war against the Jews.
Along with him the Idumeans also, in control of important fortresses, were harassing the Jews; and receiving those who had been expelled from Jerusalem, they attempted to keep up the war.
But Maccabaeus and his men, having made solemn supplication and prayed God to fight on their side, moved against the strongholds of the Idumeans;
and assaulting them vigorously, they gained possession of the positions, and beat off all who fought upon the walls, and slew those whom they encountered, killing no fewer than twenty thousand.
Since not fewer than nine thousand had fled into two towers well fortified and with all supplies needed for a siege,
Maccabaeus left Simon and Joseph, and also Zacchaeus and his men, a force sufficient for the siege; and he himself set out for places where he was more urgently needed.
But the men with Simon, being avaricious, were bribed by certain of those who were in the towers, and on receiving seventy thousand drachmas let some of them slip away.

When word of what had happened came to Maccabaeus, he gathered the leaders of the people, and accused those men of having sold their kindred for money by setting their enemies free to fight against them.
Then he slew those who had turned traitor, and immediately captured the two towers.

Having success at arms in everything he undertook, he destroyed more than twenty thousand in the two strongholds. # GEM Lapide notes that by the angel's aid Judas slew thirty thousand from Timothy's camp (v.23), twenty-five thousand at Carnion (v.26), and twenty-five thousand at Ephron (v.28), all within roughly one month from Xanthicus to Pentecost—an astonishing demonstration of divine power working through human arms.

Now Timothy, who had been defeated by the Jews before, gathered a tremendous force of foreign troops and collected the cavalry from Asia in no small number, and came on as though he would take Judea by storm.
As he drew near, Maccabaeus and his men sprinkled dust upon their heads and girded their loins with sackcloth, in supplication to God.
Falling upon the steps before the altar, they besought Him to be gracious to them and to be an enemy to their enemies and an adversary to their adversaries, as the law declares.
And rising from their prayer they took up their arms and advanced a considerable distance from the city; and when they came near to the enemy they halted.

Just as dawn was breaking, the two armies joined battle, the one having as pledge of success and victory not only their valor but also their reliance upon the Lord, and the other making rage their leader in the fight.

When the battle became fierce, there appeared to the enemy from heaven five resplendent men on horses with golden bridles, and they were leading the Jews. # GEM Lapide, following Salianus, identifies the leader of these five heavenly horsemen as Michael, protector of the Synagogue and guardian of the Jews, appearing visibly with a terrible and threatening countenance against Timothy's forces, rushing upon them with horrifying onset, bringing terror, flight, madness and destruction upon them.

Two of them took Maccabaeus between them, and shielding him with their own armor, kept him from being wounded; and they showered arrows and thunderbolts upon the enemy, so that, confused and blinded, they were thrown into disorder and were cut to pieces.
Twenty thousand five hundred were slaughtered, together with six hundred horsemen.
Timothy himself fled to a stronghold called Gazara, an especially strong fortress, where Chaereas was in command.
Then Maccabaeus and his men were glad and besieged the fortress for four days.
And those within, relying on the strength of the place, blasphemed exceedingly, and hurled out wicked words.
But at dawn of the fifth day, twenty young men in the army of Maccabaeus, inflamed with anger because of the blasphemies, bravely stormed the wall and with savage fury cut down every one they met.

Others who came up in the same way turned upon the defenders and set fire to the towers; they kindled fires and burned the blasphemers alive. Others broke open the gates and let in the rest of the force, and they occupied the city.
And they killed Timothy, who was hiding in a cistern, and his brother Chaereas, and Apollophanes.

When they had accomplished these things, they blessed the Lord with hymns and thanksgivings, who does great good to Israel and gives them the victory. # GEM Lapide notes they first purified themselves from the bloodshed and contact with corpses before offering praise, showing that even in legitimate holy war external legal purity was to accompany interior devotion.

They prayed that what had been done as a sin might be consigned to oblivion. The Greek has: that it might be perfectly blotted out. They supposed therefore that those slain had repented of the theft before death, and had deleted the chief part thereof, namely the guilt, by contrition; wherefore the other part, namely the debt of punishment to be expiated after this life in Purgatory, alone remained, which was to be expiated by sacrifices, so that the sin might be perfectly blotted out, and thus their souls, freed from the pains of Purgatory, might be transferred to the limbo of the fathers, or to the bosom of Abraham.

Add that the sin of some was not mortal but venial: both because some, being unlearned and soldiers, were ignorant of the law forbidding such thefts of idols' things, or at least thought it bound only under venial sin; and because some were in grave or extreme necessity, in which they thought it lawful for them to steal in order to preserve life; and because some had taken very little, whom the lightness of the matter excused from mortal sin. For expressly at verse 45 he says they received their sleep (that is, death) with piety. They were therefore in a state of grace. Finally it was just and pious that Judas and his men should judge and hope that some among them had sinned only venially, or if mortally, had repented before they expired; and therefore applied to them the prayers and suffrages customarily offered for the dead, as the Church even now applies them to sinners. For by every reason and in every way it can, the Church strives to aid the souls of the faithful and free them from punishments. For piety and charity and the very communion of saints demands this. # GEM

But the most valiant (Greek: generous) Judas exhorted the people to keep themselves without sin, for the reason that their strength consisted in innocence and justice; for God preserved the innocent and just in battles and made them victors, but permitted the guilty and rapacious to be conquered and slain, as they had seen with their own eyes had happened here, as follows.

He sent twelve thousand drachmas of silver (which amount to a thousand gold coins if the drachmas were Attic; if Hebrew, to two thousand gold coins, with which many sin-offerings could be bought and offered) to Jerusalem to be offered for the sins of the dead. Hence it is clear that Judas, acting as a Pontiff, believed in Purgatory, in which souls after this life are cleansed from their sins.

This was the faith of the Synagogue and of the ancient Jews as well as the moderns, as is clear from their Rituals, Commentaries and writings, which Serarius here, Genebrard, Bellarmine and others writing on Purgatory cite.
The same is clearly evident from that passage of Tobias 4:18: "Place your bread and wine upon the burial of the just." See what is said there.

WELL AND RELIGIOUSLY (in Greek εὐσχήμως, that is, urbanely, elegantly, beautifully, wisely, uprightly) THINKING OF THE RESURRECTION.] For it is urbane, civil, honest and upright to help one's fellow citizens who have departed this life with prayers, that they may rise to the blessed resurrection, which is most full of all elegance, beauty and glory.

FOR IF HE HAD NOT HOPED THAT THEY WHO HAD FALLEN SHOULD RISE AGAIN, IT WOULD HAVE SEEMED SUPERFLUOUS AND VAIN TO PRAY FOR THE DEAD.] One might say: This would not be vain, because the soul, which is immortal, would be freed from punishments through prayers, even if the body should not rise again.

The answer is: because the soul is immortal, this very fact demands the resurrection of the body. For the soul by nature inclines toward the body, just as form inclines toward matter. Wherefore the state of the soul would be more violent if it endured for eternity without the body.

Therefore these two things, namely the immortality of the soul and the resurrection of the body, are so connected that whoever posits and believes the one, posits and believes the other also; and whoever removes and denies the one, removes and denies the other also. # GEM

WHO WITH PIETY (that is, contending and fighting in the state of grace for the true faith, piety and worship of God) HAVE TAKEN THEIR SLEEP,] that is, have received it. For death in Scripture is called sleep by analogy and likeness; for death is as it were a long sleep, and sleep is as it were a brief death; but most especially death is called sleep on account of the hope of resurrection, in which we shall as it were awake from sleep to eternal life.

THEREFORE IT IS A HOLY AND WHOLESOME THOUGHT TO PRAY FOR THE DEAD (in Greek εὐσεβής, that is, pious) THAT THEY MAY BE LOOSED FROM SINS (that is, from the punishments of sins).] This is the epiphonema of the Canonical Writer, which clearly establishes the belief of the ancient Synagogue concerning Purgatory and suffrages for souls detained therein.
This therefore has been the certain and undoubted faith of the Church in every age.

The sacrifice for the dead is therefore holy, because it is holily offered to the holy God. It is likewise pious: both toward souls, which it frees from the horrible torments and fires of Purgatory; and toward the blessed inhabitants of heaven, whose number, and thereby joy and glory, it increases; and toward the Church, for which it procures new patrons and advocates before God; and toward the sacrificing priest himself, for whom the souls freed by him repay vicarious grace, praying to God continually for his salvation.

Therefore it is very pious and profitable to pray frequently for the dead, and this procures many goods for the one who prays: as our Martín de Roa learnedly shows in a fine work published on this subject.

Hear St. Augustine, Book 1: "In the books of the Maccabees," he says, "we read that sacrifice was offered for the dead; but even if it were read nowhere in the old Scriptures, the authority of the universal Church which shines forth in this custom is not small, where in the prayers of the priest poured out to the Lord God at his altar, the commendation of the dead also has its place." # GEM

And Damascene in his Oration for the Dead says: "Consider what Sacred Scripture has handed down to memory concerning Judas Maccabaeus, how when he saw the people over whom he presided cut down and maimed by foreign enemies in the city of the great King of Zion, and searching their garments found idols hidden upon them, he at once, as cause for expiation of this sin, made the Lord propitious and ready to show mercy, and offered gifts for each of them, and in that act fulfilled a supreme office of piety and charity. Whence it also came about that Divine Scripture, just as with all other names, so also with this name raised him to the highest praises and wonderfully commended him."

SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER 13: Antiochus Eupator, with Lysias as commander, renews war against Judas with 110,000 infantry and 5,000 cavalry, and casts down Menelaus the pseudo-pontiff from a height. At v. 10, Judas opposes Eupator, and having invoked God, enters his camp by night and slays many. Then at v. 18, Eupator besieges Bethsura and takes it by surrender. Finally at v. 22, fighting with Judas he is defeated: whereupon hearing that Philip was rebelling, he sought peace from Judas and obtained it.
IN THE YEAR ONE HUNDRED AND FORTY-NINE, JUDAS LEARNED THAT ANTIOCHUS EUPATOR WAS COMING WITH A GREAT FORCE AGAINST JUDAEA.
AND WITH HIM LYSIAS, HIS GUARDIAN AND REGENT, EACH HAVING A HUNDRED AND TEN THOUSAND FOOTMEN, FIVE THOUSAND HORSEMEN, TWENTY-TWO ELEPHANTS, AND THREE HUNDRED CHARIOTS ARMED WITH SCYTHES.
MENELAUS ALSO JOINED HIMSELF TO THEM, AND WITH GREAT DISSIMULATION ENCOURAGED ANTIOCHUS, NOT FOR THE WELFARE OF HIS COUNTRY, BUT HOPING TO BE SET IN THE PRINCIPALITY.

BUT THE KING OF KINGS STIRRED UP THE MIND OF ANTIOCHUS AGAINST THE SINNER: AND LYSIAS SUGGESTING THAT HE WAS THE CAUSE OF ALL THE EVILS, THE KING COMMANDED HIM TO BE APPREHENDED AND PUT TO DEATH IN THE SAME PLACE, AS IS THE CUSTOM OF THAT COUNTRY.
NOW THERE WAS IN THAT PLACE A TOWER OF FIFTY CUBITS HIGH, HAVING A HEAP OF ASHES ON EVERY SIDE: THIS HAD A PROSPECT STEEP DOWN.
FROM THENCE HE COMMANDED THE SACRILEGIOUS WRETCH TO BE THROWN DOWN INTO THE ASHES, ALL MEN PUSHING HIM FORWARD UNTO DEATH.
AND BY SUCH A LAW IT HAPPENED THAT MENELAUS THE TRANSGRESSOR OF THE LAW DIED, NOT HAVING SO MUCH AS BURIAL IN THE EARTH.
AND INDEED MOST JUSTLY: FOR INSOMUCH AS HE HAD COMMITTED MANY SINS AGAINST THE ALTAR OF GOD, WHOSE FIRE AND ASHES WERE HOLY, HE WAS CONDEMNED TO DIE IN ASHES.
BUT THE KING, WITH HIS MIND FULL OF RAGE, WAS COMING TO SHOW HIMSELF FAR WORSE TO THE JEWS THAN HIS FATHER WAS.
WHICH WHEN JUDAS UNDERSTOOD, HE COMMANDED THE PEOPLE TO CALL UPON THE LORD DAY AND NIGHT, THAT AS HE ALWAYS HAD DONE, SO NOW ALSO HE WOULD HELP THEM.

SINCE THEY WERE AFRAID OF BEING DEPRIVED OF THE LAW, AND THEIR COUNTRY, AND THE HOLY TEMPLE: AND THAT HE WOULD NOT SUFFER THE PEOPLE THAT HAD OF LATE TAKEN A LITTLE BREATH TO BE AGAIN SUBJECT TO BLASPHEMOUS NATIONS.
SO WHEN THEY HAD ALL DONE THIS TOGETHER, AND WERE BESEECHING THE MERCIFUL LORD WITH WEEPING AND FASTING, LYING PROSTRATE THREE DAYS CONTINUALLY, JUDAS EXHORTED THEM TO MAKE THEMSELVES READY.
AND HE, BEING APART WITH THE ANCIENTS, DETERMINED BEFORE THE KING SHOULD BRING HIS ARMY INTO JUDAEA AND GET POSSESSION OF THE CITY, TO GO OUT AND COMMIT THE EVENT OF THE THING TO THE JUDGMENT OF GOD.

SO COMMITTING ALL TO GOD THE CREATOR OF THE WORLD, AND HAVING EXHORTED HIS PEOPLE TO FIGHT MANFULLY AND TO STAND UP EVEN TO DEATH FOR THE LAWS, THE TEMPLE, THE CITY, THEIR COUNTRY AND THEIR CITIZENS, HE PLACED HIS ARMY ABOUT MODIN.

AND HAVING GIVEN HIS MEN THE WATCHWORD OF VICTORY OF GOD, WITH MOST VALIANT CHOSEN YOUNG MEN, HE ATTACKED THE KING'S QUARTERS IN THE NIGHT AND SLEW FOUR THOUSAND MEN IN THE CAMP, AND THE GREATEST OF THE ELEPHANTS, WITH THEM THAT HAD BEEN UPON HIM.
AND HAVING FILLED THE CAMP OF THE ENEMIES WITH VERY GREAT FEAR AND TUMULT, THEY DEPARTED WITH GOOD SUCCESS.
NOW THIS WAS DONE WHEN THE DAY WAS BEGINNING TO DAWN, BY THE PROTECTION OF THE LORD THAT HELPED HIM.
BUT THE KING, HAVING HAD A TASTE OF THE BOLDNESS OF THE JEWS, ATTEMPTED TO TAKE THE STRONG PLACES BY POLICY.
AND HE MARCHED TOWARDS BETHSURA, WHICH WAS A STRONG GARRISON OF THE JEWS: BUT HE WAS REPULSED, HE FAILED, HE LOST HIS MEN.
NOW JUDAS SENT NECESSARIES TO THEM THAT WERE WITHIN.
BUT RHODOCUS, ONE OF THE JEWISH ARMY, DISCLOSED THE SECRETS TO THE ENEMIES.
Lapide notes that the number of infantry (100,000) and cavalry (20,000) and elephants (32) varied on different days, as new troops arrived and old ones were sent elsewhere.

Menelaus, appointed pseudo-pontiff of the Jews by Antiochus Epiphanes, joined with them, seeking from Eupator, son of Epiphanes, confirmation of his pontificate and, consequently, his principate (for at that time the pontiff was simultaneously the prince). See what was said at ch. 4, vv. 23 and last.

But the King of kings (God) stirred the mind of Antiochus against the sinner (that is, against the apostate and betrayer of his country, Menelaus, to punish him with indignation as his deeds deserved), when Lysias suggested that he was the cause of all evils — that is, of all the seditions and wars which the Jews stirred up against Antiochus, because they could not endure the insolent and sacrilegious pseudo-pontiff Menelaus. So Josephus.

He commanded that the man, when seized, be killed in the same place (as was their custom) — namely, to be hurled headlong from a high tower. For thus the Syrians were accustomed to punish traitors, the sacrilegious, and the wicked, concerning which more in the following verse.

There was in that place a tower of 50 cubits having heaped ash on all sides. Our interpreter appears to have had a different Greek exemplar, for the modern text reads: "There is in that place a tower of 50 cubits, full of ash, having a heap of ash at the bottom. This device had a rounded instrument precipitous on all sides into the ash; into this they all thrust the guilty of sacrilege and the instigator of certain other evils, or one having transgressed, to their destruction." The Tigurinus version has: "In that place was a tower of 50 cubits full of dust, which had a revolving mechanism sloping down on all sides into the dust. Into it they all thrust whoever had been condemned for sacrilege or had committed any other notable evil, to their destruction."
This type of punishment was devised by Darius Ochus, king of the Persians, so that he might punish the authors of a conspiracy against whom he had sworn not to kill them with the sword.
Lapide notes that the same author (Ovid in Ibis) teaches concerning punishment by ash/cinders: "And as the second Darius slain by fraud, so may his own burning ash devour his bones."

THEN HE ORDERED THE SACRILEGIOUS MAN TO BE CAST INTO ASHES.
