Here observe the just and fitting punishment of retaliation inflicted by God the avenger upon Menelaus: for God caused him to be overwhelmed and buried in profane ashes, because he himself had scattered the ashes of God's altar and defiled them with his sacrileges, as is expressly stated in verse 8.

But the king (Antiochus Eupator), with unbridled mind (indignant and raging against the Jews who had rebelled against him), came to show himself more wicked (more savage) than his father (Antiochus Epiphanes, most cruel) to the Jews.

WHICH WHEN JUDAS KNEW, HE COMMANDED THE PEOPLE TO CALL UPON THE LORD DAY AND NIGHT. For three days, as follows. Behold here is the prelude and as it were the origin of public litanies, or continuous prayer, as much by night as by day, for forty, indeed seventy-two hours. These were the spiritual weapons of Judas, with which he always triumphed over his enemies.

THEREFORE GIVING ALL POWER TO GOD. That is to say, Judas committing himself and his men, and resigning them to the good pleasure of God as Lord of all, invaded the camp of Antiochus by night, so that he might either conquer or fall for God and country, as God should will.

AND HAVING GIVEN TO HIS MEN THE WATCHWORD OF GOD'S VICTORY. That is, Judas gave to his men this military watchword or password, namely 'God the victor,' or 'the victory of God,' or 'God the helper.' For he was accustomed to give this watchword as a kind of omen of victory to his men, as is clear from chapter 8 verse 23. Wherefore Judas, with God as leader, presently slew four thousand of the enemy and struck down the rest so that they thought of flight.

BUT THE KING, HAVING TASTED THE BOLDNESS OF THE JEWS. That is, when he had tasted, i.e. felt and actually experienced, the boldness of Judas, by which he had dared with a few men to break into his camp and slaughter four thousand, fearing lest he might attempt more and greater things, turning from force to cunning, "HE ATTEMPTED BY CRAFT THE DIFFICULTY OF THE PLACES." That is, he attempted through skill, treachery and betrayal to occupy the more difficult positions of Judea for assault.

HE MOVED HIS CAMP AGAINST BETHSURA, ETC., BUT WAS PUT TO FLIGHT, WAS REPULSED, WAS DIMINISHED. Vatablus: he was worn down, both by famine and by disease and by the arms of the besieged, as well as by Judas rushing upon him unexpectedly. Bethsura was a most strongly fortified citadel situated upon rock and cliff (for Bethsur in Hebrew means the same as 'house of rock'), whence Jo[sephus records...]

[On Beth-zur] Beth-zur was the most strongly fortified place in all Judaea. It lay to the south of Jerusalem, distant from it five stadia, that is 624 paces, so as to defend it against the incursions of the Idumeans and other enemies. Wherefore Antiochus could not take Beth-zur by force, especially since Judas was present and continually supplied the besieged with food and reinforcements, as follows.

[On Rhodocus] Rhodocus disclosed to the enemies the mysteries (that is, the secrets): Salianus probably thinks that these mysteries were certain secret paths and means by which food was being supplied to the people of Beth-zur, which the treacherous Rhodocus had revealed to the enemies, intending to desert to them; but when the matter was exposed, he was investigated and found out and thrown into prison. That this is so is gathered from the following verse.

[On the king's second parley with Beth-zur] The king again spoke to those who were in Beth-zur, gave his right hand, received theirs, and departed: that is to say, the king first addressed the soldiers of Beth-zur that they should surrender, but was repulsed by them, because Judas was supplying food to the besieged through hidden paths. But when Antiochus learned of these paths through the traitor Rhodocus and blocked them, he addressed the soldiers again and, now that they were suffering from hunger, persuaded them to surrender.

[On Antiochus defeated by Judas] He engaged in battle with Judas and was defeated: that is to say, Antiochus, having taken Beth-zur, proceeded to nearby Jerusalem; but there he was repulsed and defeated by Judas. See what was said in 1 Macc. 6:51. So Sanchez, though Salianus refers this to the four thousand slain in the camp of Antiochus by Judas at v. 15, before Beth-zur was taken. # GEM But when he learned that Philip was rebelling: Philip, to whom the dying Antiochus Epiphanes had handed his ring and diadem, and had entrusted his son Eupator together with the kingdom. Philip, hearing that Lysias was the guardian of Eupator and was administering the kingdom, rebelled against both of them and seized Antioch. Wherefore Lysias, in order to besiege, capture, and kill him at Antioch, sought peace with Judas and the Jews.

[On v.1] Judæos deprecans subditusque eis — Vatablus, handling the matter more freely, translates thus: "Thrown into confusion, he humbly submitted himself to the Jews, swore to whatever conditions, made peace, offered sacrifice..."
Nicanor appointed Judas as commander and prince from Ptolemais to the territory of the Gerraseni, so that he might administer those regions in the name of Antiochus as a kind of viceroy.
The Greek reads: 'He left Judas as military commander from Ptolemais to the principality of the Gerrheni, to administer those regions in the name of Antiochus as a kind of viceroy.'

On the indignation of the Ptolemaeans: the Greek says they were indignant because certain parties wished to render the treaties void. Some interpreters explain this as: the Ptolemaeans were indignant at certain concessions made by Antiochus to the Jews, on account of which the Ptolemaeans themselves wished to render the peace treaties void.

But this sense does not accord with the Latin Vulgate, which plainly attributes the fear of breaking the treaty to the Jews. That is: the Ptolemaeans were indignant at this treaty concluded by Antiochus with the Jews, and especially at having been subjected to Judas Maccabaeus as a kind of viceroy, because they feared that Judas and the Jews, now grown more powerful, might become insolent and break the treaty made with the king, and might also compel them and the neighboring regions, now subject to Judas, to rebel with them against the same king.
For the Ptolemaeans were enemies of the Jews, as is evident from the fact that they themselves treacherously captured Jonathan the brother of Judas and killed him with his men.

SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER 14. DEMETRIUS, AFTER KILLING LYSIAS AND ANTIOCHUS EUPATOR, AT THE URGING OF THE TRAITOR ALCIMUS, SENDS NICANOR WITH A GREAT ARMY AGAINST JUDAS AND THE JEWS; BUT HE MAKES A TREATY WITH JUDAS. DEMETRIUS TAKES THIS ILL AND ORDERS THAT JUDAS BE SENT TO HIM IN CHAINS. JUDAS, FORESEEING THIS, WITHDRAWS TO A SAFE PLACE. THEN NICANOR VENTS HIS ANGER AGAINST JUDAS UPON RAZIAS, A MAN OF GREAT RENOWN. BUT RAZIAS INFLICTS A VIOLENT DEATH UPON HIMSELF BY THREE MEANS, LEST HE FALL INTO NICANOR'S HANDS.
After three years' time, Judas and those with him learned that Demetrius son of Seleucus had come up with a strong force and ships through the port of Tripolis to places of advantage.
And that he had taken possession of the regions against Antiochus and his commander Lysias.
Now a certain Alcimus, who had been high priest but had willingly defiled himself in the times of mingling with the Gentiles, considering that there was no safety for him nor any access to the altar,
came to king Demetrius in the one hundred and fiftieth year, offering him a golden crown and a palm, and besides these, branches which seemed to belong to the temple. And on that day he was silent.
But having found a fitting opportunity for his madness, being summoned by Demetrius to council,

Alcimus says the Hasideans, led by Judas Maccabeus, are the source of war and sedition, refusing to let the kingdom have peace. Note he shrewdly names the Hasideans first, as if Judas merely follows them, to make the accusation seem broader and more credible to Demetrius.

Alcimus claims he was defrauded of the high priesthood belonging to his fathers, using personal grievance to gain the king's sympathy. He presents himself as wronged, not as an aggressor, to justify his treachery.

He professes two motives: loyalty to the king and care for his fellow citizens, cloaking his malice under the pretence of public virtue. Lapide notes this is the typical device of sycophants, who always dress personal revenge in the garb of the common good.
He flatters Demetrius by appealing to his well-known clemency, urging him to provide for the region and the people. Flattery is the instrument by which he hopes to move the king to destroy Judas.
As long as Judas survives, it is impossible for affairs to have peace. This is the whole aim of Alcimus's speech: to make Judas appear the sole obstacle to peace, and so procure his death. # GEM

The other friends of the king, already hostile to Judas, inflamed Demetrius further. Lapide notes that one accuser easily draws others with him, especially at court where envy of the virtuous is common.

Demetrius immediately sent Nicanor, commander of the elephants, into Judea. The speed of his response shows how completely he was persuaded by Alcimus. Nicanor had previously been defeated by Judas (1 Mc 7:27), which makes his commission here noteworthy.

His orders were threefold: capture Judas, scatter his followers, and install Alcimus as high priest of the great temple. Thus the political and religious usurpation are tied together—destroying Judas was the means to establishing the impious Alcimus.

The Gentiles who had fled from Judas now flocked in crowds to Nicanor, counting the misfortunes and slaughters of the Jews as their own prosperity. Lapide observes that those who hate the servants of God rejoice at anything that afflicts them.

When the Jews heard of Nicanor's advance and the gathering of the nations, they sprinkled earth on themselves and prayed to him who established his people to guard them forever and who protects his own portion with manifest signs. Lapide notes the threefold ground of their prayer: God's institution of the people, his eternal covenant, and his visible past protection.
At the commander's order they immediately moved out and met the enemy at the fortress of Dessau. The promptness of the Jewish response shows their constant readiness under Judas.
Simon, brother of Judas, had engaged Nicanor but was thrown into alarm by the sudden appearance of the enemy. This is not cowardice but prudent caution in the face of a larger force.

Nicanor, hearing of the valor of Judas's companions and their greatness of soul in fighting for their fatherland, feared to decide the matter by bloodshed. Lapide notes that even a pagan general respected the virtue of men who fight for religion and fatherland.
Therefore he sent ahead Posidonius, Theodotius, and Matthias to give and receive pledges of friendship. When force seems dangerous, diplomacy is attempted.

After long deliberation, and after the general had consulted the multitude, the unanimous opinion was to agree to friendship. Lapide notes the democratic element here: the general submits the question to the whole army before concluding peace.
They fixed a day to treat privately between themselves, and chairs were brought out and set for each. The equality of seating signified the equality of the parties in the negotiation.

Judas prudently ordered armed men to be stationed in suitable places, lest any harm might suddenly arise from the enemy, and so they held a proper conference. Lapide praises this as wise precaution: trust, but verify.
Nicanor remained in Jerusalem and did nothing unjust, and dismissed the crowds that had gathered. His good behavior here contrasts with his later threats, showing how Alcimus's slanders changed him.
He kept Judas continually dear to him from his heart, and was favorably disposed toward the man. A true and remarkable friendship arose between the pagan general and the Jewish hero.

He urged Judas to marry and have children. Judas married, lived quietly, and they lived in common. Lapide notes that Nicanor's encouragement of Judas to settle down shows genuine goodwill, not mere political calculation. # GEM

But Alcimus, seeing their mutual affection and their meetings, came to Demetrius and said that Nicanor was serving foreign interests and had designated Judas, the enemy of the kingdom, as his own successor. Envy cannot bear to see virtue honored even by former enemies.

The king, exasperated and provoked by this wicked slander, wrote to Nicanor saying he was displeased with the treaty of friendship and ordered him to send Maccabeus in chains to Antioch without delay. Lapide notes how easily a king is turned by a flatterer against a just man.

When Nicanor learned this he was dismayed and bore it hard if he should make void what had been agreed, having suffered no harm from Judas. His conscience reproached him for the injustice demanded of him.

But because he could not resist the king, he watched for an opportunity to carry out the order. Lapide observes that fear of earthly kings often overrides the voice of conscience and the obligations of justice.

But Maccabeus, observing that Nicanor was treating him more harshly and displaying a more savage manner than usual at their customary meetings, understanding that this harshness was not from any good cause, gathered a few of his men and hid himself from Nicanor. Prudence in recognizing a changed friend is a form of wisdom.

When Nicanor realized he had been boldly forestalled by the man, he came to the great and holy temple while the priests were offering the customary sacrifices, and ordered that the man be surrendered to him. The sacrilege of his demand is underscored by its setting at the moment of sacrifice.
When the priests swore with an oath that they did not know where the one sought was, he stretched out his hand toward the temple. This gesture of threatening the sanctuary is itself a grave impiety.

And swore, saying: Unless you deliver Judas to me in chains, I will level this sanctuary of God to the ground, tear down the altar, and dedicate this temple to Lionysius. Lapide notes the triple blasphemy: threatening destruction, profanation of the altar, and consecration to a demon. # GEM
And having said this, he departed. But the priests, stretching their hands toward heaven, called upon him who is always the champion of their nation, saying:

You, O Lord of all, who stand in need of nothing, were pleased to have a temple of your habitation among us. Lapide comments: God needs nothing from us, yet he graciously condescends to dwell among men; this grounds the prayer that he will defend his own dwelling. # GEM

And now, O holy Lord of all holiness, preserve forever undefiled this house that has recently been purified. The recent purification under Judas (2 Mc 10:3) gives urgency to the prayer: what cost so much must not now be lost.

Now a certain Razias, one of the elders of Jerusalem, was accused to Nicanor, a man who was a lover of his city and of good repute, who for his affection was called the father of the Jews. Lapide notes that such men are always the first targets of tyrants, since their virtue and reputation make them dangerous to oppression.

For a long time he had maintained his resolve of continence in Judaism, and was ready to give up body and soul for perseverance in it. His steadfastness over many years is the basis of his reputation and the cause of his danger.

Now Nicanor, wishing to make evident his hatred toward the Jews, sent five hundred soldiers to arrest him. The dispatch of five hundred soldiers against one old man reveals the cowardice that always underlies tyrannical violence.

For he thought that by striking him down he would inflict the greatest blow on the Jews. Lapide notes that the enemy of God always calculates that destroying the most virtuous will break the spirit of the rest.

When the troops were about to force their way into his house, break down the door, and set fire to it, and he was on the point of being captured, he struck himself with his sword. Lapide begins here his extended discussion of this controversial act of self-destruction.

Choosing to die nobly rather than fall into the hands of sinners and suffer treatment unworthy of his birth. Lapide notes the two motives given: noble death vs. servitude to sinners, and preservation of the dignity proper to his condition. He treats this as the key to the moral evaluation of Razias's act.

But when, in his haste, the blow had not gone deep enough into the flesh—for the crowds were pressing in through the doors—he ran bravely up to the wall and threw himself down manfully into the crowd below. Lapide here begins his careful patristic and theological analysis of whether these acts of self-destruction are to be praised or merely excused.

per festinationem incerto ictu plagam dedisset — In his haste he had struck himself with an uncertain blow, and as the crowd was rushing in through the doors, he ran back boldly to the wall and cast himself down manfully upon the crowd.
quibus velociter locum dantibus casui ejus — They quickly making way for his fall, he came down upon the middle of his neck.

et cum adhuc spiraret, accensus animo, surrexit — And while he still breathed, inflamed in spirit, he rose up; and though blood was flowing from him in great streams and he was grievously wounded, he ran through the crowd.

stans supra quamdam petram praeruptam — Standing upon a certain steep rock, now drained of blood, he took his entrails in both hands and cast them upon the crowd, calling upon the Lord of life and spirit to restore these to him again; and so he departed this life. # GEM

Sed post triennii tempus — That is, in the third year after the death of Antiochus Epiphanes, namely in the year 150 of the Greeks, as is stated at verse 4. For the author of this second book supposes that Antiochus Epiphanes died in the year 148 of the Greeks; for although he died in the year 149 according to the Jewish reckoning, yet this year 149 was 148 according to the Antiochene reckoning, as I explained above; and consequently this year 150 of the Greeks according to the Antiochene reckoning was 151 according to the Jewish reckoning.

Demetrium Seleuci — This Seleucus was the elder son of Antiochus the Great; the younger son of the same was Antiochus Epiphanes. Wherefore to Demetrius, as the son of the elder brother, the kingdom of Asia and Syria was owed. Demetrius therefore, a hostage at Rome, hearing that his uncle Antiochus Epiphanes had died, sought from the Romans permission to return to his own people and to occupy the kingdom rightfully due to him. But the Romans denied him this, because they feared him more than Eupator the son of Epiphanes, who was but a boy. Wherefore Demetrius, secretly fleeing from Rome, came into Syria, where he was crowned king by his own people, and having slain Eupator and Lysias, he seized the kingdom, as is told in book 1.

Chapter 7 recounts the martyrdom of seven brothers and their mother under Antiochus Epiphanes, who commanded them to taste swine's flesh in violation of the Law, threatening them with torture and death.
The seven brothers and their mother were arrested together, showing that the tyrant sought to destroy entire families who adhered to the ancestral faith.
The whips and scourges mentioned here were instruments of Roman and Syrian torture; Antiochus used them to break the will of the faithful before resorting to more extreme measures.
The first brother speaks boldly on behalf of all, showing that among the faithful, one voice can represent the courage of many; this is a figure of the Church's martyrs speaking with one voice.
We are ready to die rather than transgress the laws of our fathers — this is the supreme confession of faith, placing divine law above bodily life. # GEM
The king's anger here illustrates how tyrants are most enraged by calm defiance, for they expect fear and obtain contempt instead.
The cutting off of tongue, hands, and feet before death was a Persian and Syrian form of torture designed to maximize suffering; Antiochus added frying in a pan to increase the cruelty.
The tongue is cut first because it had confessed the faith; then the hands and feet, instruments of action. Antiochus attacks the body in order to reach the soul, but fails. # GEM

The rest of the brothers and their mother encouraged one another to die nobly — the mother here already shows herself to be greater than the tyrant, for she arms her children for death while he can only inflict it.
The citation of Deuteronomy 32:36 by the dying martyr shows his familiarity with Scripture even in extremis; he dies with the Law on his lips as he dies for the Law. # GEM
The Lord God will look upon the truth and will be consoled — the martyr interprets his own death as testimony to divine justice, confident that God will vindicate the righteous and punish the wicked.
After the first brother's death, the second is brought forward; the repetition of torture for each brother heightens the dramatic and theological force of the narrative.

The second brother's declaration of resurrection — "the King of the world will raise us up, who die for his laws, in the resurrection of eternal life" — is one of the clearest Old Testament affirmations of bodily resurrection. # GEM

Lapide notes that this text is a decisive proof against those who deny the resurrection of the body; the martyr affirms not merely spiritual survival but resurrection of the very body being destroyed by Antiochus.

The third brother stretched out his hands willingly to be cut off, showing that the martyrs did not merely endure but actively cooperated in their martyrdom, offering their members to God as a sacrifice.

These I had from heaven, and for his laws I despise them, and from him I hope to receive them again — the third brother affirms that our bodies are gifts from God and that God can restore what He gave. # GEM
This verse is cited by the Fathers as proof that the resurrection is not merely of the soul but of the very same members of the body; God who created the hands will restore them.
Even the king and those around him marveled at the courage of the young man, for courage is so natural to man that even enemies cannot fail to admire it when they see it truly displayed.

The fourth brother, as he dies, declares that there is no resurrection for Antiochus — a fearsome threat, showing that the martyrs do not merely hope for their own vindication but announce the damnation of their persecutors. # GEM

For thee there shall be no resurrection unto life — Lapide notes this is not an absolute statement of reprobation but a prophetic warning, calling Antiochus to repentance while announcing the consequences of his crimes.
The fifth brother, though described briefly, shows by his very brevity that constancy had become habitual among the brothers; each successive martyr needed less exhortation.

The fifth brother addresses Antiochus directly: "thou hast power among men, though thou art corruptible" — distinguishing between earthly power which is temporary and divine power which is eternal. # GEM
But think not that our nation is forsaken of God — the martyr here speaks as a prophet, warning Antiochus that the sufferings of Israel are a divine chastisement, not a sign of divine abandonment.
The sixth brother affirms that Israel suffers because of its own sins, not because God is weak or indifferent; this theological interpretation of suffering is central to the entire book. # GEM
Lapide observes that the martyrs thus combine perfect courage with perfect humility, neither claiming innocence before God nor despairing of his mercy; they are models of the penitent soul.

But do not think that thou shalt escape unpunished — the sixth brother turns from self-accusation to prophetic denunciation, showing that while Israel suffers for correction, Antiochus will suffer for condemnation.
The mother is now introduced as superior to all — "most admirable and worthy of honorable memory" — because she endured not once but seven times over, in each child's death dying herself. # GEM

The mother "encouraged each of them in the language of their fathers" — Lapide notes that Hebrew was used, both to conceal the encouragement from Antiochus and to appeal to the ancestral faith through the ancestral tongue.
The mother's speech on creation is philosophically remarkable: she affirms that God created the world from nothing and man from nothing, grounding the resurrection in the power of the Creator. # GEM
I know not how you were formed in my womb — the mother professes ignorance of the mechanism of generation in order to ascribe all to God; what she cannot explain, she attributes to divine power.

The Creator of the world, that formed the nativity of man, and that found out the beginning of all things, will restore to you again in his mercy both breath and life, as now you despise them for the laws' sake. # GEM

Lapide sees in this verse a complete theology of resurrection: God who gave life originally will restore it; mercy is the motive; the law is the occasion; the body itself is the object of the promise.
Antiochus, supposing the youngest son might be persuaded by youth and inexperience, addresses him with promises of wealth and friendship — showing that when terror fails, tyrants resort to flattery.
The king even involved the mother in his attempt to persuade the youngest, expecting maternal love to overcome religious conviction; he misjudged both mother and son. # GEM
The mother, when urged by Antiochus, leaned toward her son — but only to exhort him to death, not to life; she used the appearance of compliance to strengthen his resolve.

Son, have pity upon me — the mother's appeal is to her own suffering as a mother, but the pity she seeks is not that he should live but that he should die worthily; love here inverts the natural order. # GEM

The mother's declaration "God made them out of nothing" is a clear scriptural affirmation of creation ex nihilo, and Lapide uses it against ancient philosophers who taught the eternity of matter. # GEM

Fear not this tormentor, but being made worthy of thy brethren, receive death, that in that mercy I may receive thee again with thy brethren — the mother hopes to be reunited with all her sons in the resurrection. # GEM
The youngest son addresses Antiochus directly and refuses his offer, showing that he has received not weakness but strength from his mother's words, contrary to Antiochus's expectation.

Thou that hast been the author of all mischief against the Hebrews, shalt not escape the hand of God — the seventh brother pronounces definitive judgment on Antiochus, as though he were a prophet commissioned by the dying. # GEM

For we suffer these things for our sins — even in his final speech the youngest acknowledges Israel's fault, showing that the martyrs combined prophetic boldness toward others with penitential humility regarding themselves.

Although the living Lord be angry with us a little while for our chastening and correction, yet shall he be reconciled again with his servants — a beautiful expression of confidence in divine mercy after chastisement. # GEM
But thou, O wicked and most infamous of all men — the contrast with the humble self-accusation of the previous verse is striking; toward God the martyr is humble, toward the tyrant he is bold.

For our brethren having now undergone a short pain are fallen under God's covenant of everlasting life — the deaths of the brothers are here interpreted as the fulfillment of covenant, not its disruption. # GEM

I, like my brethren, offer up my body and my life for the laws of our fathers, calling upon God to be speedily merciful to our nation — the seventh martyr explicitly presents his death as an intercessory sacrifice for Israel. # GEM

Lapide sees in the seventh brother's self-offering a prefiguration of Christ's atoning death: as the youngest brother offers himself for the sins of his people to move God to mercy, so Christ offered himself for all mankind. # GEM

And that in me and my brethren the wrath of the Almighty, which hath justly fallen upon all our nation, may cease — the concept of expiatory martyrdom is here made explicit; the martyr dies that others may live. # GEM
Antiochus, infuriated by the seventh brother's boldness, treated him with greater cruelty than all the rest; tyrants are most cruel when they feel themselves condemned by their victims.
The seventh brother "died pure, and put his whole trust in the Lord" — simplicity and purity of intention is the crown of martyrdom; he died as he had lived, with undivided heart. # GEM
Last of all the mother died — "the last of her children" — having outlived all seven in order to strengthen each one; she who gave them natural life presided over their passage to eternal life. # GEM

Lapide compares the mother of the Maccabees to the Blessed Virgin Mary, who similarly endured the deaths of those dearest to her and yet did not withdraw her consent; both mothers are figures of supreme spiritual courage. # GEM
The author concludes by noting that enough has been said about the sacrificial meals and extreme tortures; the narrative has served its purpose as an example and exhortation to the faithful.

SED VOLUNTARIE COINQUINATUS EST TEMPORIBUS COMMIXTIONIS: that is, when Antiochus Epiphanes commanded the Jews to mingle with the Gentiles and apostatize from Judaism to Gentilism, then Alcimus also apostatized. Wherefore, considering that there was NO WAY OF SALVATION FOR HIM, NOR ACCESS TO THE ALTAR, both because the Jews had excluded him from the temple as an apostate, and because Judas Maccabaeus, now exercising the Pontificate and the Principate, would by no means have tolerated Alcimus as a rival — for this reason Alcimus petitioned Demetrius to send Nicanor against Judas, so that the Pontificate might be conferred upon himself.

OFFERING HIM A GOLDEN CROWN (whereby he congratulated the new king Demetrius on his new kingdom), AND A PALM, by which he wished him victory against Judas and the other enemies, AND BOUGHS, that is, branches from the olive tree, by which he begged for peace — for the olive is a symbol of peace — but this sacrilegious man had taken these things from the temple. Hence what follows: WHO SEEMED TO BELONG TO THE TEMPLE. Moreover, these branches and palms, as well as the crown, were fashioned from gold.
THOSE WHO ARE CALLED HASIDAEANS — who these were
Here it is clear that Judas was in religion and piety an Assidaean, indeed the chief and prince of the Assidaeans.

NICANOR HOWEVER, HEARING OF THE VALOR OF JUDAS'S COMPANIONS, etc., FEARED TO DECIDE THE MATTER BY BLOOD, i.e., he feared to contend in battle and settle the dispute by arms; for he feared to be overcome and slaughtered by Judas; wherefore he sent ambassadors to him for the sake of peace, and made peace with him.
NOW HE ALWAYS HELD JUDAS DEAR FROM HIS SOUL. Greek: en prosōpō, i.e., in the face, as if to say Nicanor always held Judas before his eyes as a friend and intimate.

AND WAS INCLINED TOWARD THE MAN, i.e., favorably disposed and benevolent toward Judas. Some explain the phrase "in face" contrariwise, as if to say Nicanor outwardly feigned friendship toward Judas, but inwardly hated him as a most valiant commander of war whom he could not equal, and therefore urged him to take a wife, so that through her he might be made effeminate and lay aside his warlike spirit, just as Samson was made effeminate through his Delilah and stripped of his strength. But this sense conflicts with the Vulgate, which says: He held Judas dear from his soul.

AND HE ASKED HIM (JUDAS) TO TAKE A WIFE AND BEGET CHILDREN, so that he might beget offspring like himself and pour into them his courage and martial virtue; for the lyric poet says: The brave are born of brave and good. In young bulls and in horses is found the valor of their sires, nor do fierce eagles beget the unwarlike dove. So Horace, Book 4, Ode 3. Gorionides adds that Judas begot a male son by his wife; but that son appears to have perished soon, as did Judas himself, who fell in battle the following year; for no mention is made of his son in Scripture.

AND THEY LIVED TOGETHER IN COMMON, namely Judas and Nicanor were intimate with one another. Serrarius renders the Greek differently: He led a common life, or lived in common, namely Judas, as if to say Judas held nothing as his own but, like an Assidaean or Essene, lived in common with his companions, as religious persons live today.

ALCIMUS HOWEVER, etc., as if to say Alcimus, the rival of Judas, accused Nicanor before King Demetrius, saying that he had made a league with his enemy Judas and had designated him as a successor and insidious claimant of the kingdom (Greek: diadochon, i.e., a deputy to govern the territory in his stead); wherefore Demetrius, enraged, commanded Nicanor to seize Judas and send him to him as a prisoner; for Judas was laying snares against his kingdom.

HAVING COURAGEOUSLY (Greek: generously) ANTICIPATED THE MAN (JUDAS). For it belongs to a noble spirit prudently to foresee future ambushes and dangers, to guard against them, and to place oneself and one's affairs in safety, as Judas here did.

NOW A CERTAIN RAZIAS, ONE OF THE ELDERS (i.e., one of the senators and chief men of Jerusalem), WHO ON ACCOUNT OF HIS AFFECTION (his love for his fellow citizens and his homeland) WAS CALLED THE FATHER OF THE JEWS:

HE FOR MANY YEARS HAD MAINTAINED A PURPOSE OF CONTINENCE IN JUDAISM: continence, namely from a wife, i.e., chastity or celibacy—so some interpret. Or rather continence in the true faith and worship of God, as if to say Razias always, even in persecution, kept himself within Judaism and the religion of his fathers, and did not mingle with the many who under Epiphanes were apostatizing from it to Gentilism: for this is what the Greek amixias, i.e., of non-mingling, signifies; and this is what is meant by the purpose of continence maintained in Judaism, namely constant perseverance even unto death in Judaism; whence, explaining the same thing, it adds: CONTENT TO GIVE BODY AND SOUL FOR PERSEVERANCE IN THE REVERED RELIGION OF THE JEWS. Gorionides adds that Razias was by profession an Assidaean.
FOR HE THOUGHT THAT IF HE DECEIVED HIM. Salianus thinks one should read seized him; for the Greek is syllambanon, i.e., if he had apprehended him.

CHOOSING NOBLY (Greek: gennaiōs, i.e., generously) TO DIE RATHER THAN TO BECOME SUBJECT TO SINNERS AND TO BE DEALT WITH UNWORTHILY AGAINST HIS BIRTH (for Razias was born of noble parents) WITH UNWORTHY INSULTS. Greek: And to be subjected to insults unworthy of his proper nobility. This was the mind of Razias, as it was of other nobles of this world, who think it noble to die rather than to suffer unworthy indignities. But the mind of the faithful and of Christians is higher, especially of the Apostles and martyrs, of whom it is accordingly said in Acts 5: The Apostles went rejoicing from the presence of the council, because they were counted worthy to suffer reproach for the name of Jesus.

IT CAME THROUGH THE MIDDLE OF HIS NECK. Greek: through the middle of his belly, as if to say he fell to the ground not headlong but upon his belly, so that he dashed the middle of his neck against the ground. Razias therefore dispatched himself with a triple death: first he stabbed himself with a sword; second, he cast himself down from the wall; third, with both hands he flung his entrails into the crowd.

One may ask whether Razias sinned by killing himself. I presuppose from the common opinion of St. Augustine, Lactantius, St. Thomas, and the other Doctors of the Church that it is not lawful for a man to kill himself to avoid disgrace, violation of chastity, danger of sinning, or for any other reason. The a priori reason is that God alone is the lord of our life, who as he gave it, so also takes it back and reclaims it whenever it pleases him. Therefore he who kills himself does God an injury, because he steals from God what is properly God's, namely his own life. See St. Augustine, Book 1. # GEM

Razias, called "father of the Jews," chose to die rather than fall into the hands of his enemies, casting himself from a wall into the crowd. Augustine (City of God) praises such acts in pagan heroes but distinguishes them from Christian martyrdom: it is manly but not salutary, courageous but not faithful.

Some (Francis of Victoria, Thomas Aquinas) argue Razias did not sin because he acted under the instinct of the Holy Spirit. But no spark of such instinct appears here, especially since the Holy Spirit does not normally inspire one to kill oneself merely to avoid the disgrace of mockery, as Razias did; this is in itself dishonorable and unlawful.

# GEM Therefore Lapide concludes that Razias is excused from sin by invincible ignorance: he did not know that self-killing in such circumstances was unlawful; indeed he judged it lawful, honorable, noble, and generous (v. 42). Just as many ancients, both philosophers and some Church Fathers, thought lying was sometimes permissible, so likewise they held the same regarding self-killing. This was the doctrine of the Stoics, repeatedly urged by Seneca (Epistles 12, 17, 24, 58, 59, 71, 78), indicated also by Cicero (Letters, bk. 1, epp. 12 and 22), and Pliny; and likewise held by certain Hebrews according to Josephus (bk. 12).

In persecutions it is not lawful to perish by one's own hand, except where chastity is in danger; where he plainly indicates that in danger to chastity it is lawful to kill oneself. So St. Ambrose, book 3, On Virginity.

Was he not convinced that through that act he would be conveyed to the blessed and eternal life? For fixed is that oracle of Christ, and likewise his promise: "He who has lost his life for my sake shall find it," Matt. 10:39. Again, why should he not mortify his concupiscence and his flesh, which through mortification he believes will be vivified and liberated in heaven? For mortification is a kind of living death, by which a man resolutely and persistently cuts off and severs carnal appetites contrary to the deliberate will. Hence he feels this cutting to the quick, and suffers as if a tooth were being torn from him. Hear St. Ambrose, in book 3 On Virgins, celebrating with wonderful praises the holy virgin Pelagia, fifteen years of age, who threw herself with her mother and sisters into a river and drowned herself lest she be violated: "Joining hands, as though they were leading a dance, they advance into the middle of the channel, where the current is more violent, where the depth more abrupt, directing their steps thither. None drew back her foot, none slowed her pace, none tried to find where to plant her step; distressed when the ground appeared, offended by the shallows, joyful in the deep. You might see the pious mother drawing her children close with knotted hands, rejoicing in her pledge, fearing for the chance lest either a wave or the current should snatch her daughters from her. 'These victims, O Christ,' she said, 'I offer to thee, guardians of virginity, leaders of chastity, companions of the passion.'" # GEM Furthermore, the Church venerates these women, who killed themselves to preserve their chastity, as martyrs, because they had been as it were compelled to this act. Hence, since they did not sin in that deed according to moral estimation, they are reckoned to have been slain by their persecutors themselves, and thus are martyrs. If anyone wishes to say the same of Razias, I shall not object.
Nicanor, learning that Judas was in the regions of Samaria, planned to attack with full force on the sabbath day.
The Jews who were compelled to follow him said: Do not act so fiercely and barbarously, but give honor to the day of sanctification, and honor him who sees all things.
The wretched man asked whether there is a powerful one in heaven who commanded the sabbath day to be observed.
They answered: The Lord himself lives in heaven, powerful, who commanded the seventh day to be kept.
He replied: And I am powerful on earth, who command arms to be taken up and the king's business to be carried out. Yet he did not succeed in accomplishing his purpose.
Nicanor, puffed up with the utmost pride, had planned to erect a common trophy from Judas's defeat.
But Maccabaeus continually trusted with full hope that help from God would come to him.
He exhorted his men not to fear the approach of the nations, but to keep in mind the help given to them from heaven, and now to hope that victory would come to them from the Almighty.
Speaking to them from the Law and the Prophets, reminding them also of the contests they had previously fought, he made them more ready.
And having thus raised their spirits, he showed at the same time the faithlessness of the Gentiles and their breaking of oaths.
He armed each of them not with the protection of shield and spear, but with excellent words and exhortations, and having related a dream worthy of belief, through which he made them all joyful.

The vision was of this kind: Onias, who had been high priest, a good and virtuous man, modest in bearing, gentle in manner, graceful in speech, and who from childhood had practiced the virtues, was seen extending his hands and praying for the whole people of the Jews.
After this there appeared also another man, remarkable for his age and glory, and of great and beautiful stature about him.
Onias answering said: This is the lover of his brethren and of the people of Israel; this is he who prays much for the people and for all the holy city, Jeremiah the prophet of God.
Jeremiah stretched forth his right hand and gave to Judas a golden sword, saying:
Take this holy sword, a gift from God, with which you will strike down the adversaries of my people Israel.

Thus encouraged by the very good words of Judas, from which valor and the strengthening of the spirits of the young men could arise, they determined to fight bravely and to engage manfully, so that valor might decide the matter, since the holy city and the temple were in danger.
For their concern for wives and children, and likewise for brethren and kindred, was less; but the greatest and first fear was for the holiness of the temple.
And those who were in the city were not in small anxiety for those who were about to engage in battle.
And now, when all were expecting the coming judgment, and the enemies were at hand, and the army was drawn up in order, the beasts and the cavalry placed in a favorable position,

Maccabaeus, observing the arrival of the multitude and the varied equipment of arms and the ferocity of the beasts, stretching out his hands toward heaven, called upon the Lord who works wonders, who gives victory to those who are worthy not according to the power of arms but as it pleases him.
He spoke in his invocation in this manner: You, O Lord, who sent your angel under Hezekiah king of Judah, and killed one hundred and eighty-five thousand from the camp of Sennacherib;
and now, O Ruler of the heavens, send your good angel before us, with fear and trembling of the greatness of your arm,
that those who come with blasphemy against your holy people may be struck with terror.

Nicanor planned to attack with full force on the Sabbath day, because he presumed that the Jews, on account of the religious rest of the Sabbath prescribed by God, would not fight on that day, but would allow themselves to be killed.

Here observe Nicanor's blasphemous act, by which he equates himself with God, as if to say: Let God command in His heaven, but I command on earth, and I order you, O Jews whom I have compelled to follow my camp, to fight on the Sabbath day against Judas and your fellow Jews. This blasphemy inflamed the anger of those Jews who were following him, so that they refused to obey, and provoked God's indignation against him, so that he was slain, mutilated, and hanged by Judas. For he seemed to have challenged God from heaven to do battle with him on earth.

With similar arrogance Mezentius the Etruscan thunders in Virgil, Aeneid 10: 'My right hand is my god, and the weapon I poise to hurl—let these be present now.' And what Statius says of Capaneus in Thebaid 2: 'Valor is my divinity and my sword'; and again of Capaneus in book 10: 'Be with me, right hand only; thou presidest over battles and art an inevitable power; thee I call, thee alone, scorner of the gods above, I worship.' Caeneus ordered his spear to be numbered among the gods and oaths to be sworn by it; hence the proverb 'the spear of Caeneus.' And in Plutarch's Life of Pelopidas, the tyrant Alexander wished the spear with which he had killed Poliphron to be sacred, adorned it, and honored it with sacrifices as if it were a god. So Habakkuk 1:16 says of Nebuchadnezzar: 'Therefore he sacrificed to his net and burned incense to his dragnet,' where by net and dragnet he means his forces, arms, and armies, for these he worshipped as his own divinities, as things which had subjected so many nations to him.
Nevertheless he did not prevail, because the Jews themselves resisted and refused to fight against Judas and their fellow Jews on the Sabbath.
Producing sentences from Holy Scripture by which God promises victory to Jews who worship and invoke Him rightly, as in Deuteronomy 28 and elsewhere.

It is certain that they went out to battle not unarmed but fully armed; he armed each of them not with the protection of shield and spear, with which they were already armed and equipped, but with excellent words and exhortations.

The Roman text reads ὕπαρ, that is, a true vision, which was not so much a dream as reality itself. So Homer in the Iliad says: οὐκ ὄναρ, ἀλλ' ὕπαρ, that is, not a vain and false dream, but a solid and true one. From this one might suppose that Judas truly saw Onias and Jeremiah with his own eyes and really received the golden sword from him. Yet the contrary is more truly the case, namely that all these things were presented to the mind of Judas through an imaginary vision only; for this is what the word ὄνειρον, that is, dream, signifies. Moreover, if he had truly received the sword, he would surely have shown it to his comrades to encourage them; yet we do not read that he did so. So Sanchez.

This Onias, surnamed the Holy, had been known to Judas, and so Judas recognized him by face and appearance. For Onias had been high priest eleven years before, but had been expelled by his brother Jason who purchased the Pontificate from Antiochus Epiphanes, and had then been slain by Andronicus at the instigation of Menelaus in the year of the Greeks 140.
Nicanor and those with him advanced with trumpets and battle songs.
But Judas and those with him, having called upon God through prayers, engaged in battle.
Fighting with their hands but praying to the Lord with their hearts, they laid low no fewer than thirty-five thousand, being magnificently gladdened by the presence of God.
When they ceased and were returning with joy, they learned that Nicanor had fallen with his arms.
Then raising a shout and tumult, they blessed almighty God in the language of their fathers.
Judas, who was in all things ready to die in body and soul for his fellow citizens, commanded the head of Nicanor and the hand cut off with the shoulder to be carried to Jerusalem.
When he arrived there, having called together his fellow tribesmen and the priests to the altar, he also summoned those in the citadel.
He showed the head of Nicanor and the impious hand, which he had stretched out against the holy house of almighty God and magnificently boasted against it.
He also commanded the tongue of the impious Nicanor to be cut out and given piece by piece to birds, and the hand of the madman to be hung up opposite the temple.
All therefore blessed the Lord God of heaven, saying: Blessed is He who has kept His own place undefiled.
He hung the head of Nicanor in the highest part of the citadel, as a clear and manifest sign of the help of God.

All therefore decreed by common counsel that this day should by no means pass without celebration, having the celebration on the thirteenth day of the month Adar, which in the Syrian tongue is called the day before Mordecai's day.
Therefore, these things having been accomplished against Nicanor, and the city being held by the Hebrews from those times, I also will make an end of my account here.
And if it is well done and as befits the history, that is what I myself would wish; but if it is done less worthily, it must be pardoned me.

For as it is contrary always to drink wine or always to drink water, but to use each alternately is pleasant, so if the discourse is always exact, it will not be pleasing to readers. Here therefore it shall be concluded.
Judas sees in a dream the holy and zealous High Priest Onias praying for him and all Israel.

One asks whether, just as the souls of the fathers in Limbo, so also souls in Purgatory pray for us. Richard [of Middleton] in [Sent.] bk. 4, dist. 45, art. 7, q. 8, ad 3, and Gabriel [Biel] in [Exposition of the] Canon [of the Mass], lecture 58, toward the end, and Johannes de Medina, Codex de Poenitentia, treatise on Prayer, in the special question, affirm this. # GEM St. Thomas [Aquinas] in [Sent.] dist. 15, q. 4, art. 5, q. 2, and in [Summa Theologiae] II-II, q. 83, art. 4, ad 3, and art. 9, ad 2, and art. 11, ad 3, [Alexander of] Hales in [Summa Theologiae] pt. 4, q. 92, mem. 1, art. 3, Abulensis on Matthew ch. 6, q. 87, St. Antoninus, [Summa Theologica] pt. 4, tit. 5, ch. 8, §2, Sylvester under the word 'Prayer,' §3, and Navarrus on the chapter 'Quandd.' deny it. The reason is that those souls do not know our prayers, and that they are in a prison for suffering for their own faults, not for praying for others; hence it seems idle and disordered to invoke them.

Wherefore Francisco de Vitoria, Relectio de Magia, n. 17, disapproves of the practice of certain persons who in their difficulties invoke the souls in Purgatory. But Alphonsus Mendoza, Scholastic Questions, q. 6, p. 866, considers it holy and useful to invoke them. For he says: whether or not they are able to pray for us while they are in Purgatory, certainly when they depart thence and arrive in heaven, they then pour forth prayers and pray for those who commended themselves to them and assisted them with suffrages and prayers.

Add to this that the Angels, who visit and console the souls in Purgatory, can report to them the prayers of the living; nor does the suffering of punishment prevent them from prayer, because they bear those punishments with great patience, humility and resignation, and they continually love and praise God.

Yet it is more useful and more certain to invoke the saints reigning with God in heaven; both because they certainly know through God's revelation the prayers addressed to them, for this pertains to their state; and because they see God and are in His love and honor, whence they obtain from Him whatever they wish; and because they are in the state of blessedness and triumph in the heavenly kingdom and glory, whence it pertains to their dignity that they be invoked by us and assist us in our miseries. See Francisco Suarez, bk. 1, de Oratione, in [this place].

[On souls in Purgatory hearing prayers] And guardian angels carry our prayers to the souls in Purgatory. Therefore if anyone invokes them, piously believing they are heard by them, he will sin not at all; because he acts from pious belief and affection, and exposes himself to no danger; because even if perhaps it is not so, and those souls do not perceive prayers of this kind, nonetheless his prayer turns back into his own bosom. And perhaps it is also true that those souls, at least when they become blessed, come to know all prayers poured out to them during that whole time, and are moved by them to intercede in the homeland for their supplicants. Therefore, one who has felt the fruit and devotion of praying in this world does not seem to be one who should be called back from it.

[On the appearance of the glorious man] AFTER THIS, ANOTHER MAN APPEARED, ADMIRABLE IN AGE AND GLORY, AND OF GREAT COMELINESS OF BEARING AROUND HIM — this can also be read as "bearing," because in Greek it is μεγαλοπρεπεστάτην ὑπεροχήν, "adorned on all sides with excellent magnificence," which glory was the sign of the merits of the most holy prophet, and of the glory owed to him in heaven. # GEM

[On Onia's response identifying Jeremiah] BUT ONIA ANSWERING. From this it appears that Judas did not recognize Jeremiah, as one who had died some four hundred years before; and therefore he asked Onia, known to him, who that man might be so august and venerable, and that Onia answered: This is the friend of the brethren (in Greek —

et populi: hic est qui multum ORAT PRO POPULO ET UNIVERSA SANCTA CIVITATE, Jeremias propheta Dei.] Before Isaiah, Amos, Jonah, and the other prophets, Jeremiah appeared to Judas: because he himself with immense zeal, prophesying for 45 years, labored for the salvation of Israel to prevent the destruction of his nation, which nevertheless, on account of the enormity of its crimes, and because it refused to hearken to his oracles and sound counsels, he could not prevent—so that Jerusalem with its temple was taken and burned by the Chaldeans. Wherefore Jeremiah suffered many hard and bitter things at the hands of the Jews, which I have explained in his own prophecy.

Accipit sanctum gladium (from holy God, and blessed by me, that you may fight with holy fortitude for His holy Church and faith and religion); MUNUS A DEO, IN QUO DEJICIES ADVERSARIOS POPULI MEI ISRAEL.] Jeremiah did not truly and really give Judas a sword, as I said. For this was a dream and a symbolic vision, in which it appeared to Judas that he received a sword from Jeremiah, so that through it Judas and his companions might be animated for battle and might conceive a sure hope of victory, because God fought with them and through them against the impious, unbelieving, and blasphemous Nicanor. So too this sword was not one of iron, such as men use in war, but golden, inasmuch as it was heavenly and divine. For the things of God are golden; for gold, being most precious and most splendid, represents to us God and the most august gifts of God.

Note the phrase "holy sword," that is, "blessed." For this custom has prevailed, that commanders and soldiers have their standards and arms blessed by bishops and priests: both so that through the prayers and blessing of the Church they may be more efficacious for victory; and to drive away magical arts, by which enemies often make themselves invulnerable with the help of a demon, as we have now experienced in the German war. Thus St. Louis, about to lead an expedition to the Holy Land, received a standard blessed by the Bishop of Paris. The other leaders of that expedition did the same. Thus an Italian, whose horses and chariot were blessed by St. Hilarion, conquered his adversary in a circus contest who was using the magical arts of his god Marnas. Hear St. Jerome in his Life of Hilarion: "He [St. Hilarion] ordered a clay cup from which he was accustomed to drink to be filled with water by the brethren and given to the man; and when Italicus received it, he sprinkled his stable, his horses, his charioteers, his chariot, and the starting-gate bars. The expectation of the crowd was astonishing; for his adversary had ridiculed this very thing and spread it abroad, while the supporters of Italicus, promising themselves certain victory, exulted. Therefore, when the signal was given, these flew forward, those were impeded; under the chariot of the latter the wheels burned hot, while the former could scarcely see the backs of those flying past; the clamor of the crowd was so great that even the pagans themselves cried out: Marnas is conquered by Christ." # GEM

In the Roman Ceremonial there is a blessing of the sword and of the soldier. The English also formerly had a rite of consecrating a soldier, the form of which Ingulph, describing it in his history, states: "It was the custom of the English that whoever was to be lawfully consecrated to knighthood should, on the evening before his day of consecration, make confession of all his sins, contrite and compunctious, to a bishop, or abbot, or monk, or some priest, and absolved, devoted to prayers and devotions and holy affections, should spend the night in the church. On the following day likewise, being about to hear Mass, he should offer his sword upon the altar, and after the Gospel the priest should place the blessed sword upon the neck of the soldier with a blessing, and having communicated at that same Mass in the sacred mysteries of Christ, the soldier should remain thenceforth a lawful knight." The same is recorded by our Gretser, in his work on Blessings.

Through the golden sword of Holy Scripture, by which Christ prostrated the devil when he was tempted by him in the desert, and even now lays low and routs his heretical followers through the teachers of the Church.

Again, the holy sword is mortification, by which we cut away from ourselves the concupiscences and vices of the flesh and spirit, according to that word of Christ: I came not to send peace, but a sword, Matt. 10:34.

Wherefore Jeremiah, ch. 48, v. 10, says: Cursed is he who withholds his sword from blood. Jeremiah therefore, that is Christ, extends to each faithful person this sword of mortification, golden with the love of God, that with it he may slay all his enemies, that is the passions and appetites of corrupt nature, fighting against God and His law. # GEM

ERIT GRATUS. Human nature delights in variety, according to the saying: "The Muses love alternation." Thus readers delight in variety of style in a writer, so that now it may be elevated, grave and polished, and now brought down, light and less refined and finished. The Greek reads: For just as to drink wine alone, and likewise water alone, is disagreeable, whereas wine mixed with water produces a sweet and pleasing grace (that is, brings a pleasing delight to the one who tastes it), so also the composition of speech (so that it may be mixed with a certain pleasing temperament and moderation, and be harmonious) delights the hearing of those who read, if it is heard read by another, and the eyes, if it is read by themselves. That is to say: just as always to drink undiluted wine, or always pure water, is unpleasant and unhealthful — for wine overheats too much and inflames the head and body, while water too greatly chills it and produces phlegm — but to mix water with wine and drink the mixture is sweet, delightful, healthful and useful, because water tempers and moderates the heat and sharpness of wine, and wine in turn breaks the coldness and rawness of water, and reduces both to a certain pleasing moderation: so likewise speech that is either too grave or too plain is unpleasant to readers and brings tedium; but speech moderated from both and reduced to suitable temperament is pleasing and agreeable. For this same reason I have in this book used a middle style which tempers gravity with lightness; for variety as much as moderation in all things is pleasing, savory and delightful; otherwise satiety and disgust creep over all things in the world, as Cicero says, and as daily experience teaches.

HIC ERGO ERIT CONSUMMATUS. The Greek reads: Here shall be the end or consummation both of this book and of the entire Old Testament, that we may hand on the torch to Christ, who is shortly to succeed, who is the end and goal and perfection and consummation of the Law and the Prophets and of both Testaments, new as well as old. # GEM

DE LIBRO TERTIO MACHABAEORUM. This book is not canonical; yet it possesses authority and credibility equal to, or indeed greater than, that possessed by Livy, Curtius, Florus, Sallust and similar historians. It exists in Greek in the Complutensian and Royal Bibles. It is called by catachresis the Third Book of Maccabees, because it does not properly treat the deeds of the Maccabees — that is, of the brothers Judas, Jonathan and Simon — but rather those things which occurred thirty years before them; and thus in strict chronological order it should be placed before the First Book of Maccabees, and accordingly should be called not the Third but the First.

For it narrates the deeds of Antiochus the Great (who was the father of Antiochus Epiphanes, against whom the Maccabees fought) and of Ptolemy Philopator, who was the fourth king of Egypt after Alexander the Great, and the son of Ptolemy Euergetes, the brother of Ptolemy Philadelphus. He was called Philopator, that is, lover of his father, by antiphrasis; because he himself killed his father, mother, brother, sister and wife, captivated by the allurements of the harlot Agathoclea; and therefore he spent his nights in debauchery and his days in banquets, as Justin says in books 29 and 30. This Philopator was the father of Ptolemy Epiphanes and grandfather of Ptolemy Philometor.

The subject therefore of this Third Book is to narrate the deeds of Ptolemy Philopator with Antiochus the Great and with the Jews. For at the outset it recounts that famous battle of Antiochus with Philopator fought at Raphia in the third year of the 140th Olympiad, namely in the same year in which Hannibal defeated the Romans at Lake Trasimene in a memorable victory. For when Philopator learned that Syria had been taken from him by Antiochus, he gathered all his forces both cavalry and infantry, and taking with him his sister Arsinoe, marched to the regions near Raphia where the forces of Antiochus were. When a fierce battle was joined and Antiochus was fighting more vigorously, Arsinoe walking among the troops, with her hair unbound, besought the soldiers with tears to bring her bold assistance, promising to give each man two gold minas as a victory reward. And so it came about that the enemy were defeated in battle and many were captured.

Thereafter it is narrated how the king came to Jerusalem, and when he attempted to enter the Holy of Holies while the Jews opposed him, he was chastised by a great miracle of divine power and deterred from entering that place. For God, says the author of 3 Maccabees, struck him down in his arrogance, shaking him here and there like a reed in the wind, and thus laid him low, useless even on the ground, his limbs dissolved and unable to speak, afflicted by just judgment; wherefore his friends, fearing lest he should also lose his life, quickly led him out, struck with the greatest terror; but he in no way repented, and departed with bitter threats.

Having arrived in Egypt, not only persisting in boundless lusts but also vomiting blasphemies against the temple, he harassed the Jews in marvelous ways: for first he caused the Jews in Alexandria to be marked with a brand of disgrace; then he had them all registered and reduced to servitude. Shortly thereafter, having issued a dire edict against them, he ordered them to be diligently sought throughout all Egypt, and being vexed in wondrous ways to be brought to Alexandria, to be killed in a single day by elephants, with the king himself and the people looking on. Yet divine protection was present with them, and not only was the people of the Jews miraculously rescued by God from that most atrocious death, but also with the king's fury turned to benevolence, they were thereafter favored by him and restored to their former status by a new decree issued in their favor.

This is the sum of the matters which are narrated at length throughout the whole of 3 Maccabees, and these things are consistent with what Josephus, Pausanias, Justin, Appian and others narrate concerning the same events, and indeed with what Daniel predicted of these same matters three hundred years before.

Lysias, the king's guardian and kinsman, who was in charge of the affairs of state, being greatly displeased at what had happened, gathered about eighty thousand infantry and all his cavalry and advanced against the Jews, intending to make Jerusalem a settlement for Greeks.
He thought to make the temple a source of profit, as he did the other sacred places of the Gentiles, and to put the high priesthood up for sale every year.
He took no account whatever of the power of God, but was elated with his ten thousands of infantry, his thousands of cavalry, and his eighty elephants.
So he invaded Judea, and when he drew near Beth-zur, which was a fortified place about five stadia from Jerusalem, he pressed it hard.
When Maccabeus and his men got word that Lysias was besieging the strongholds, they and all the people, with lamentations and tears, besought the Lord to send a good angel to save Israel.
Maccabeus himself was the first to take up arms, and he urged the others to risk their lives with him to aid their kindred. Then they resolutely set out together.

And suddenly, while they were still near Jerusalem, a horseman appeared at their head, clothed in white and brandishing weapons of gold. # GEM This apparition of the heavenly horseman signifies that God himself goes before his people in battle, as Athanasius and Chrysostom note; the white garments denote the purity and holiness of divine aid, while the golden weapons show that heavenly strength surpasses all earthly might.
And together they all praised the merciful God, and were strengthened in heart, ready to assail not only men but the most savage beasts or even walls of iron.
They advanced in battle order, having their heavenly ally, for the Lord had mercy on them.
They hurled themselves like lions against the enemy, and slew eleven thousand of them and sixteen hundred horsemen, and forced all the rest to flee.
Most of them got away stripped and wounded, and Lysias himself escaped by disgraceful flight.

And as he was not without intelligence, he pondered over the defeat which had befallen him, and realized that the Hebrews were invincible because the mighty God fought on their side. So he sent to them.
And persuaded them to settle everything on just terms, promising that he would persuade the king, constraining him to be their friend.
Maccabeus consented to all that Lysias urged, being mindful of the common good; and whatever Maccabeus wrote to Lysias about the Jews, the king granted.
For there were letters written to the Jews from Lysias to this effect: Lysias to the people of the Jews, greeting.
John and Absalom, who were sent by you, have delivered your signed communication and have asked about the matters indicated therein.
I have informed the king of everything that needed to be brought before him, and he has agreed to what was possible.
If you will maintain your goodwill toward the government, I will endeavor in the future to help promote your welfare.
And concerning these matters and their details, I have ordered these men and my representatives to confer with you.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Dioscorinthius twenty-fourth.
The king's letter ran thus: King Antiochus to his brother Lysias, greeting.
Since our father has gone on to the gods, we desire that the subjects of the kingdom be undisturbed in caring for their own affairs.
We have heard that the Jews do not consent to our father's change to Greek customs but prefer their own way of living and ask that their own customs be allowed them.

Accordingly, since we choose that this nation also be free from disturbance, our decision is that their temple be restored to them and that they shall live according to the customs of their ancestors.
You will do well, therefore, to send word to them and give them pledges of friendship, so that they may know our policy and be of good cheer and go on happily in the conduct of their own affairs.
To the nation the king's letter was as follows: King Antiochus to the senate of the Jews and to the other Jews, greeting.
If you are well, it is as we desire. We also are in good health.
Menelaus has informed us that you wish to return home and look after your own affairs.
Therefore those who go home by the thirtieth of Xanthicus will have our pledge of friendship and full permission.
The Jews shall enjoy their own food and laws, just as before, and none of them shall be molested in any way for what he may have done in ignorance.
And I have also sent Menelaus to encourage you.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth.
The Romans also sent them a letter to this effect: Quintus Memmius and Titus Manius, envoys of the Romans, to the people of the Jews, greeting.
With regard to what Lysias the kinsman of the king has granted you, we also give consent.

But as for the matters which he decided are to be referred to the king, as soon as you have considered them, send some one promptly, so that we may make proposals appropriate for you, for we are going to Antioch.
Therefore make haste and send some men, so that we may have your views.
Farewell. The one hundred and forty-eighth year, Xanthicus fifteenth.
column4: # GEM The inclusion of these Roman letters confirms the historical reliability of this book, as Jerome and Eusebius observe; the Romans here act as mediators of justice for the Jews, showing that even pagan powers are instruments of divine providence for the preservation of God's people.

column3: The second book of Maccabees is not a continuation of the first, but rather a separate work covering some of the same events, written by a different author. It begins with letters sent by the Jews of Jerusalem to those in Egypt.

The brethren the Jews that are in Jerusalem: These are the Jews of Judea writing to their brethren dwelling in Egypt, especially at Alexandria, where a great multitude of Jews had settled since the time of Alexander the Great.
Peace and health: This is the customary greeting of the Hebrews, corresponding to the Greek salutation. The Jews wished their brethren both outward peace and inward health of soul.
God be gracious to you: The writers pray that God would show favor and mercy to their Egyptian brethren, recalling the covenant made with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

Give you all a heart to worship him: They pray that God would give them a heart devoted to His worship and observance of His law, for without this gift of God, the human heart turns away from true religion.
Open your heart in his law and commandments: That is, may God illuminate your minds to understand His law deeply and perfectly, so that you may observe it faithfully. # GEM
Make peace with you: That is, may God be reconciled to you if you have sinned, and grant you His favor and blessing, for without divine reconciliation no true peace is possible.

We pray for you here: The Jerusalem Jews assure their Egyptian brethren that they offer prayers and sacrifices on their behalf in the Temple, thus showing the bond of charity uniting all the dispersed children of Israel.

In the reign of Demetrius: This refers to Demetrius II Nicator, king of Syria. The letter recalls the troubles and persecutions which the Jews had suffered during that reign, particularly under the high priest Jason who apostatized.

Jason...forsook the holy land and the kingdom: Jason, having purchased the high priesthood from Antiochus Epiphanes, introduced Greek customs and abandoned the law of Moses, bringing great ruin upon Jerusalem and the sacred institutions.

Burnt the porch: That is, Jason and his followers set fire to the portico of the Temple and shed innocent blood. This burning of the sacred precincts was a grievous sacrilege lamented by all faithful Jews.

Keep the days of the feast of tabernacles: The writers urge the Egyptian Jews to celebrate the feast of the dedication of the Temple, instituted by Judas Maccabeus, in the manner of the feast of Tabernacles, with similar rejoicing and solemnity.

They of Jerusalem and of Judea: This introduces a second letter, sent to Aristobulus, a learned Jewish philosopher and teacher, and to the Jews in Egypt, containing an account of the miraculous fire recovered from the time of the captivity.

Aristobulus the teacher of king Ptolemy: This Aristobulus was a Peripatetic philosopher of Jewish origin who wrote a commentary on the law of Moses and enjoyed great influence at the Egyptian court. He is mentioned by Clement of Alexandria and Eusebius.

Being delivered from great perils: The writers give thanks to God for their deliverance from the dangers they had faced, particularly the campaign of Antiochus Epiphanes against Persia, which ended in his destruction.
He that drave out them that fought against the holy city: God is praised as the true defender of Jerusalem, who turned back the forces of Antiochus and brought his impious campaign to ruin.

For when the leader was come into Persia: This refers to Antiochus Epiphanes, who entered Persia to plunder the temple of Nanea, and was there destroyed along with his army, as divine punishment for his impiety.

Under pretence of marrying her: Antiochus pretended to wish to marry the goddess Nanea in order to obtain her great treasures under the guise of a dowry. This deceit was turned back upon him by divine justice.

They of the temple of Nanea: The priests of Nanea, seeing through the stratagem of Antiochus, shut the doors of the temple upon him and his companions and slew them, so that the deceiver was deceived.

Throwing stones: The priests of Nanea cast stones down upon Antiochus and his men from above, cut off their heads, and threw them out to those waiting outside, a fitting end for the persecutor of God's people.

Blessed be our God in all things: The sacred writers break forth into a doxology of praise, blessing God who delivered the wicked into the hands of the wicked, and who punished the impious king with a death fitting his crimes.

We intend to keep the purification of the temple: The writers explain the reason for their letter, namely to invite the Egyptian Jews to celebrate the purification and rededication of the Temple by Judas Maccabeus, which took place on the twenty-fifth of Chislev.

The fire...when Nehemiah offered sacrifice: The letter introduces the miraculous account of the sacred fire which had been hidden at the time of the Babylonian captivity and miraculously recovered in the time of Nehemiah.

Our fathers were led into Persia: At the time of the Babylonian captivity, the priests took the fire from the altar and hid it secretly in a hollow valley, where it remained concealed throughout the years of exile.

After many years...Nehemiah...sent: When God was pleased to restore His people, Nehemiah sent the descendants of the priests who had hidden the fire to retrieve it, but they found not fire but a thick liquid, which is interpreted as naphtha or petroleum. # GEM

When the sacrifice was laid on: Nehemiah commanded that the thick liquid be poured over the wood and the sacrifice upon the altar, and when this was done and the sun came out from behind clouds, a great fire was kindled miraculously.

A great fire was kindled: This miraculous kindling of fire from the liquid was a sign of divine favor, confirming that God had accepted the sacrifices of His restored people and would once again dwell among them.

The priests made a prayer: While the sacrifice burned, the priests and all the people offered prayer, Jonathan leading the prayer and the rest responding, as was the custom in solemn liturgical prayer.

O Lord, Lord God: The prayer is addressed to God as the creator and king of all, the sole holy and almighty one, who governs all things and whose mercy endures forever. This is a beautiful liturgical prayer. # GEM

Who only art bountiful: God alone is the source of all goodness and bounty, the only just and almighty one, the eternal one who saves Israel and who chose the fathers and sanctified them as His own people.

Receive the sacrifice for all thy people Israel: The prayer asks God to accept the sacrifice offered on behalf of all Israel, to preserve His own portion and inheritance, and to sanctify those who are His own.

Gather together our scattered people: The prayer asks for the ingathering of the dispersed exiles of Israel, a theme recurring throughout Scripture and Jewish prayer, looking toward the full restoration of God's people. # GEM

Bring low them that oppress us: The prayer asks God to humble the persecutors of Israel and to punish those who treat His people with contempt and arrogance, so that God's justice may be manifest to all nations.

Plant thy people again in thy holy place: The prayer concludes with a petition for the permanent restoration of Israel in the Holy Land and the rebuilding of Jerusalem, so that God's people may serve Him in peace and security.

Then the priests sang hymns: After the sacrifice, the priests sang hymns of praise while the sacrifice was consumed. Jonathan led the prayer and the rest responded, showing the antiphonal character of Jewish liturgical worship.

The remainder of the liquid: When the sacrifice was consumed, Nehemiah commanded that the remaining liquid be poured over large stones, a detail emphasizing the abundance of this miraculous substance and God's generosity.

When this was known: When the report of this miracle reached the king of Persia, he enclosed the place where the liquid had been found, making it sacred, and distributed gifts from the treasuries, showing that even pagan kings recognized the wonder.

The king...enquired about the matter: The king of Persia, upon hearing of this miracle, made careful inquiry about it, and when the matter was explained to him, he ordered that the place be enclosed and reverenced.

The king called the place Nephthar: The name given to the place means purification or cleansing in the Chaldean tongue, though some derive it differently. The sacred author preserves this etymology to explain the name by which the place became known.

The liquid was called Nephthai: The liquid itself also received this name, meaning purification, because of its connection with the restoration of sacred fire and the purification of Israel's worship. Thus the miracle was commemorated in the very name of the substance.

Antiochus, returning from Persia having been repulsed, turns toward Jerusalem with a mind full of rage and fury, intending to make it a tomb for the Jews; but divine vengeance strikes him down along the way.
He had entered the city Persepolis and attempted to plunder the temple and subjugate the city, but was repulsed by the inhabitants who rose up in arms, and thus departed in disgrace.
While still in Ecbatana, news reached him of what had happened to Nicanor and the forces of Timotheus.

Swollen with anger, he thought to inflict upon the Jews the injury done to him by the Persians, and so he commanded his charioteer to drive without ceasing until he had finished his journey, though the judgment of God was already upon him.

But the Lord God of Israel, who sees all things, struck him with an incurable and invisible wound; for as soon as he had spoken these words, a pain of the bowels that was remediless came upon him, and sore torments of the inner parts.
This was just, since he had tormented other men's bowels with many and strange afflictions; yet he did not cease from his insolence.

Moreover he was filled with pride, breathing out fire in his rage against the Jews, and commanding to haste the journey; but it came to pass that he fell down from his chariot as it was going, so that having a sore fall, every member of his body was much pained.

He who a little before thought he might command the waves of the sea, and weigh the high mountains in a balance, was now brought to the ground, and carried in a litter, shewing forth unto all the manifest power of God.
