So that worms rose up out of the body of this wicked man, and whilst he lived in sorrow and pain, his flesh fell away, and the filthiness of his smell was noisome to all his army.
And the man that thought a little before he could reach the stars of heaven, no man could endure to carry for his intolerable stench.
Here therefore, being plagued, he began to leave off his great pride, and to come to the knowledge of himself by the scourge of God, his pain increasing every moment.
And when he himself could not abide his own smell, he said these words: It is meet to be subject unto God, and that a man that is mortal should not proudly think of himself as if he were God.
This wicked person vowed also unto the Lord, who now no more would have mercy upon him.
He promised to make free the holy city of Jerusalem, which he was in haste to make level with the ground, and to make it a common burying place.

And touching the Jews, whom he had judged not worthy to be so much as buried, but to be cast out with their children to be devoured of fowls and wild beasts, he promised he would make them all equal to citizens of Athens.

And the holy temple, which before he had spoiled, he promised to garnish with goodly gifts, and to restore all the holy vessels with many more, and out of his own revenue to defray the charges belonging to the sacrifices.
Yea and that he would become a Jew himself, and should go through all the world that was inhabited, and declare the power of God.

But for all this his pains would not cease; for the just judgment of God was come upon him; therefore despairing of his health, he wrote unto the Jews the letter underwritten, containing the form of a supplication, after this manner.
Antiochus, king and governor, to the good Jews his citizens, wisheth much joy, health, and prosperity.
If ye and your children fare well, and your affairs be to your contentment, I give very great thanks to God, having my hope in heaven.

As for me, I was weak, or else I would have remembered kindly your honour and good will; returning out of Persia, and being taken with a grievous disease, I thought it necessary to care for the common safety of all.
Not distrusting mine health, but having great hope to escape this sickness.
But considering that even my father, at what time he led an army into the high countries, appointed a successor.
To the end that, if any thing fell out contrary to expectation, or if any tidings were brought that were grievous, they of the land knowing to whom the state was left, might not be troubled.

Again, considering how that the princes that are borderers and neighbours unto my kingdom wait for opportunities, and expect what shall be the event, I have appointed my son Antiochus king, whom I often committed and commended unto many of you, when I went up into the high provinces; and I have written to him the things that are underwritten.
I pray you therefore and request you, that remembering the benefits done publicly and privately, you will every man of you continue to be faithful to me and my son.
For I am persuaded that he understanding my mind, will favourably and graciously yield to your desires.
Thus the murderer and blasphemer having suffered most grievously, as he entreated other men, so died he a miserable death in a strange country in the mountains.
And Philip, that was brought up with him, carried away his body, who also fearing the son of Antiochus went into Egypt to Ptolemeus Philometor.
Nicanor, hearing that Judas and his men were in the region of Samaria, resolved to attack them on the day of rest, completely without danger.
But those who were compelled to follow him said: Do not destroy them so savagely and barbarously, but give honor to the day which He who sees all things has honored and hallowed before other days.
The thrice-wicked man asked if there were a Sovereign in heaven who had commanded the Sabbath day to be kept.
And when they declared: There is the living Lord himself in heaven, the Mighty One, who commanded the seventh day to be kept.
He said: And I am a sovereign upon the earth, who command to take arms and to do the king's business. Nevertheless he prevailed not to accomplish his design.
So Nicanor in his exceeding pride had determined to set up a public monument of victory over Judas and his company.
But Machabeus ever trusted with all confidence that God would help him.
And he exhorted his people not to fear the assault of the nations, but to remember the help they had before received from heaven, and now also to hope for victory from the Almighty.
And speaking to them out of the law and the prophets, and withal putting them in mind of the conflicts they had maintained, he made them more cheerful.
And when he had animated their courages, he gave his orders, and at the same time shewed the perfidiousness of the Gentiles and their breach of oaths.
So he armed every one of them, not with defence of shield and spear, but with very good speeches and exhortations, and told them a dream worthy to be believed, whereby he rejoiced them all.

Now the vision was in this manner: Onias, who had been high priest, a good and virtuous man, modest in his looks, gentle in his manners, and graceful in speech, and who from a child had learned all things belonging to virtue, holding up his hands, prayed for all the people of the Jews.
After this there appeared also another man, admirable for age and glory, and environed with great beauty and majesty.
Then Onias answering, said: This is he that prayeth much for the people and for all the holy city, Jeremias, the prophet of God.
And Jeremias stretched forth his right hand and gave to Judas a sword of gold, saying:
Take this holy sword, a gift from God, wherewith thou shalt overthrow the adversaries of my people Israel.

Thus, being exhorted with very good words by Judas, whereby the young men's hearts were lifted up with valour, they resolved to fight and to set upon them manfully, that valour might decide the matter, because the holy city and the temple were in danger.
For their concern was less for their wives, and children, and brethren, and kinsfolks, than for the sanctuary that was supreme in their minds.
And they also who were in the city were in no small agony, being troubled for the encounter in the open field.
And now when all expected what judgment would be given, and the enemies were at hand, and the army was set in order, the beasts and the horsemen ranged on the right flank,

Machabeus beholding the coming of the multitude, and the divers preparations of armour, and the fierceness of the beasts, stretching out his hands to heaven, called upon the Lord that worketh wonders, knowing that victory is not in arms, but that the Lord giveth it to whom he will.

And calling upon God he said after this manner: Thou, O Lord, who didst send thy angel in the time of Ezechias, king of Juda, and didst kill a hundred and eighty-five thousand of the army of Sennacherib:
Send now also, O Lord of heaven, thy good angel before us, for the fear and dread of the greatness of thy arm,
That they who come with blasphemy to thy holy people, may be afraid. And he ended this way.
Then Nicanor and they that were with him came forward with trumpets and songs.
But Judas and they that were with him encountered the enemy with invocation of God by prayers.
So fighting with their hands, but praying to the Lord with their hearts, they slew no fewer than five and thirty thousand, being greatly cheered with the presence of God.
And when the battle was over, and they were returning with joy, they understood that Nicanor was slain in his armour.
Then making a shout and a great noise, they blessed the Almighty Lord in their own language.

And Judas, who was every way the chief defender of the citizens both in body and mind, and who had kept through his whole life the love of his countrymen, commanded that Nicanor's head and his hand with the shoulder should be cut off and carried to Jerusalem.
And when he was come thither, having called together his countrymen, and the priests to the altar, he sent also for them that were in the castle,
And shewing them the head of Nicanor, and the wicked hand which he had stretched out with proud boasting against the holy house of the Almighty God,
He commanded also that the tongue of the wicked Nicanor should be cut out and given by pieces to the birds, and the hand of the furious man to be hanged up over against the temple.
Then all, blessing the Lord of heaven, said: Blessed be he that hath kept his own place undefiled.
And he hung up Nicanor's head in the top of the castle, that it might be an evident and manifest sign of the help of God.

And they all ordained by a common decree, by no means to let this day pass without solemnity, but to celebrate the thirteenth day of the month of Adar, called in the Syrian language the day before Mardochias' day.
So these things being done in regard to Nicanor, and from that time the city being possessed by the Hebrews, I also will here end my narration.
And if I have done well and as it becometh the history, it is what I desired; but if not so perfectly, it must be pardoned me.

For as it is hurtful to drink always wine, or always water, but pleasant to use sometimes the one and sometimes the other, so if the speech be always nicely framed, it will not be grateful to the readers. But here it shall end.

Lapide notes: Onias the high priest appearing in this vision to Judas is a remarkable testimony to the intercession of saints in heaven for those on earth. For Onias had already died, yet he is seen praying with outstretched hands for the Jewish people. This vision, divinely granted to Judas, confirms that the souls of the just, even after death, exercise a ministry of intercession before God on behalf of the living. # GEM

Lapide notes: Here we have the most explicit scriptural testimony to the intercession of departed saints, for Jeremiah, long dead, is shown interceding for the holy city. This passage was a favorite of the Fathers against those who denied invocation of the saints. Bellarmine and others cite it as definitive proof that the saints in glory pray for us. # GEM

Lapide notes: The golden sword given by Jeremiah to Judas signifies the divine assistance conferred through the intercession of the saints. The sword is described as a gift from God, showing that what the saints give, they give not from themselves but as instruments and ministers of divine grace and power.

Lapide notes: Judas stretching out his hands to heaven before battle represents the posture of prayer common to the ancients, and especially the gesture of the Cross, which many Fathers discern in the outstretched arms of those who pray. So Moses prayed with extended hands against Amalek, and so the Church prays with arms extended in the form of the cross.

Lapide notes: The cutting out of Nicanor's tongue and its being given to the birds is a fitting punishment for his blasphemy against the temple of God. As he had used his tongue to blaspheme, so his tongue was cut out and scattered, that even in punishment the divine justice might be manifest to all.

Lapide notes: The Day of Nicanor, the thirteenth of Adar, was instituted as a perpetual festival, the day before the feast of Mordecai, that is, before Purim. This shows the piety of the Jewish people in commemorating divine deliverances with annual celebrations, a practice continued in the Church with feast days of saints and days of thanksgiving for divine benefits.

Lapide notes: The author humbly excuses any deficiency in his work, showing that sacred historians were not unmindful of their own weakness, and committing the judgment of their labors to the benevolence of their readers. This Christian humility in the epilogue contrasts with the pride of pagan authors and commends the work to charitable reception.

In the same time Antiochus prepared another expedition, and Maccabeus gathered together those that were left, and besought the Lord that he would look upon the people that was oppressed by all; and that he would have pity on the temple that was profaned by wicked men.
That he would have pity also upon the city that was ready to be brought low, and be made even with the ground, and would hear the voice of the blood that cried to him.
That he would remember also the most unjust slaughter of harmless infants, and to render vengeance to the blasphemers, and injury done to his name.
Now Maccabeus, when he had gathered a multitude, was irresistible to the heathens, for the wrath of the Lord was now turned into mercy.
Coming therefore unawares upon towns and cities, he burnt them, and recovering the most commodious places, he put to flight no small number of the enemies.
He took advantage chiefly of the nights for such kind of enterprises, and his fame was spread abroad everywhere.
So that Timotheus, who had been overcome by the Jews before, having called together a foreign army with a very great multitude of horsemen, came as if he would take Judea by storm.
But Maccabeus and they that were with him, perceiving his coming, made supplication to the Lord, sprinkling earth upon their heads, and girding their loins with haircloth.
And lying prorate at the foot of the altar, besought him to be merciful to them, and to be an enemy to their enemies, and an adversary to their adversaries, as the law saith.
So rising up from their prayer they took their weapons, and went somewhat far from the city; and when they were come near to the enemies, they kept by themselves.

Now the sun being newly risen, they joined battle both sides: the one part having, together with their virtue, their refuge in the Lord for a pledge of success and victory; the other side making their rage their captain.

But when the battle was strongly fought on both sides, the enemies' army was at length put to flight, and many of them being wounded fled away naked; and Timotheus himself fled into a strong hold called Gazara, where Chereas was governor.
But Maccabeus and his men were joyful, and they besieged the fortress four days.
And they that were within, trusting in the strength of the place, blasphemed exceedingly, and cast forth abominable words.

But when the fifth day appeared, twenty young men of them that were with Maccabeus, burning with indignation because of the blasphemies, assaulted the wall manfully, and with a fierce courage killed all they met withal.

And others running in like manner upon others and turning upon the quarters that were behind, burnt the towers; and kindling fires, they burnt the blasphemers alive; while others breaking open the gates, receiving in the rest of the army, and seized the city.
And Timotheus had hid himself in a well; but they killed him and his brother Chereas and Apollophanes.
When this was done, they blessed the Lord with hymns and thanksgiving, who had done great things for Israel, and given them the victory.
A little while after this, Lysias the king's lieutenant and governor, and who was of the chief account next to the king, being greatly displeased with what had happened,
Gathered together fourscore thousand men, and all the horsemen, and came against the Jews, thinking to take the city, and make it a habitation of the Gentiles.
And to make a gain of the temple, as of other temples of the Gentiles, and to set the high priesthood to sale every year.
He made no account at all of the power of God, but was puffed up with his ten thousands of foot soldiers, and his thousands of horsemen, and his fourscore elephants.
So he came into Judea, and approaching to Bethsura, which was a strong place, and distant from Jerusalem about five furlongs, he pressed it hard.
But when Maccabeus and his company heard that the fortresses were besieged, they and all the people besought the Lord with lamentations and tears, that he would send a good angel to save Israel.
Then Maccabeus himself first taking his weapons, exhorted the others to expose themselves together with him to the danger, and to succour their brethren.

And when they were going out together with a willing mind, one of the Jews that had set fire to the sacred gates, and was not to be trusted, told Lysias that a great power was coming against him, and that those who were with Maccabeus were fighting to overthrow the camp.
Lysias, fearing lest this should happen, lost his courage; because things had not succeeded with him in Judea as he had expected, and as the holy writer says, he was not without misgiving of mind.
He sent to make peace with them, and promised to perform all things that were just, and to persuade the king that he should be a friend to them.
Then Maccabeus gave consent to the request of Lysias, providing for the common good in all things; and whatsoever Maccabeus wrote to Lysias, concerning the Jews, the king granted.
For there were letters written to the Jews from Lysias, to this effect: Lysias to the people of the Jews, greeting.
John and Absalom who were sent from you, delivering your writings, requested that I would accomplish the things signified by them.
Therefore whatsoever things were needful to be reported to the king, I have represented to him; and he hath granted what was possible to be done.
If therefore you will keep yourselves loyal in affairs, henceforth also I will endeavour to be a means of your good.
But as concerning other particulars, I have given orders by word both to these, and to them that are sent from me, to commune with you.
Fare ye well. In the year one hundred and forty eight, the four and twentieth day of the month Dioscorus.
But the king's letter contained these things: King Antiochus to Lysias his brother, greeting.
Our father being translated amongst the gods, we are desirous that they that are in our realm should live quietly, and apply themselves diligently to their own concerns.

We have heard that the Jews would not consent to my father to turn to the rites of the Greeks, but that they would keep to their own manner of living, for which cause they desire of us that we permit them to live after their own laws.

Wherefore being desirous that this nation also should be at rest, we have ordained and decreed that the temple shall be restored to them, and that they shall live according to the custom of their ancestors.

Thou shalt do well therefore to send to them and give them the right hand of friendship; that their minds being informed of our pleasure, they may be of good comfort, and look after their own affairs.
But to the Jews the king's letter was to this effect: King Antiochus to the senate of the Jews, and to the rest of the Jews, greeting.
If you are well, you are as we desire. We ourselves also are in good health.
Menelaus came to us and said that you desired to come down to us, who were of your nation.
We give therefore right hand of assurance to them that come up, that they may use their own proper meats, and keep their own laws, as before.
And none of them shall be any ways troubled for things done through ignorance. And we have sent also Menelaus to speak to you.
Fare ye well. In the year one hundred and forty eight, the fifteenth day of the month Xanthicus.
The Romans also sent them a letter, to this effect: Quintus Memmius and Titus Manlius, ambassadors of the Romans, to the people of the Jews, greeting.
Whatsoever Lysias the king's cousin hath granted to you, we also give our consent to it.

But as touching such things as he thought should be referred to the king, after you have diligently conferred together, send some one forthwith, that we may decree such things as are meet for you: for we are going to Antioch.
And therefore make haste to write back, that we may know what is your mind.
Farewell. In the year one hundred and forty eight, the fifteenth day of the month Xanthicus.
The second book of Maccabees is not a continuation of the first, but rather a separate work, treating partly of the same matters, partly of different ones, and written by a different author.
The first book was written in Hebrew, as Jerome attests; this second book was written in Greek, as is clear from the epilogue, where the author says he abridged it from five books of Jason of Cyrene.
The author of this abridgment is unknown; some think it was Judas Maccabeus himself, others think it was some other learned Jew writing in Greek.

The brethren who are the Jews in Jerusalem and in Judea send greetings to the brethren who are the Jews dwelling in Egypt — particularly those in Alexandria, where a great multitude of Jews lived under the Ptolemies.

Note that this letter is prefixed to the book as a kind of introduction, sent by the Jews of Jerusalem to their brethren in Egypt, exhorting them to celebrate the feast of the dedication of the Temple, which had been restored by Judas Maccabeus.
May God be good to you — this is the form of greeting and blessing, wishing them all good things from God.

And may he remember his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, his faithful servants — that is, may he fulfill to you the promises made to the patriarchs, protecting and blessing you as their descendants. # GEM

May he give you a heart to worship him and to do his will with a great heart and a willing soul — that is, may he give you not only external observance of the Law but an interior devotion and willing spirit.
May he open your heart in his law and commandments — that is, may he illuminate your minds to understand the deep meaning of the Law, and inflame your wills to keep it.
And may he make peace — peace with God through grace and forgiveness of sins, and peace among yourselves through charity and concord.
May he hear your prayers and be reconciled to you — implying that the Jews in Egypt were partly estranged from God by their sins and needed reconciliation, which comes through prayer and repentance.
We are praying for you here — showing the charity of the Jerusalem Jews toward their brethren in Egypt, interceding for them before God.

In the reign of Demetrius, in the year one hundred and sixty-nine — this dates the earlier letter sent to Egypt, referring to Demetrius I Soter, king of Syria, under whose persecution the Jews suffered greatly.

We, the Jews, wrote to you in the distress and violence that came upon us in those years after Jason and his company revolted from the holy land and the kingdom — Jason the high priest having wickedly purchased his office from Antiochus Epiphanes and then been expelled.
And burned the gate and shed innocent blood — referring to the profanations and violence that accompanied Jason's attempt to retake Jerusalem, as described more fully in chapter 5.
We prayed to the Lord and were heard — showing that prayer in tribulation is efficacious and that God delivers those who cry to him in truth.

And now see that you keep the days of the feast of Tabernacles in the month of Casleu — that is, the feast of the Dedication of the Temple, celebrated in the month of Casleu (our November-December), which Judas Maccabeus instituted to recall the purification and rededication of the Temple after its desecration by Antiochus.

This feast is called here the feast of Tabernacles in the month of Casleu to indicate that it was celebrated with similar joy and solemnity to the ancient feast of Tabernacles in the seventh month, lasting eight days.
In the year one hundred and eighty-eight — this dates the present letter, written some years after the earlier one mentioned in verse 7.
The people of Jerusalem and of Judea, and the senate and Judas — Judas Maccabeus is here mentioned as still living, which helps to date the letter to around 164 BC.

To Aristobulus, the teacher of King Ptolemy — this Aristobulus was a learned Alexandrian Jew, a Peripatetic philosopher, who wrote a commentary on the Law of Moses and dedicated it to Ptolemy VI Philometor. # GEM
Who is of the stock of the anointed priests — showing that Aristobulus was of priestly descent, which gave his teaching special authority among the Jews.
And to the Jews who are in Egypt — the letter is addressed both to the distinguished Aristobulus and to the whole Jewish community in Egypt.
Being saved by God out of great dangers, we give him great thanks — Judas and the Jerusalem Jews acknowledge God as their deliverer and give him public thanks, setting an example of gratitude.
For he drove out them that fought against the holy city — referring to the defeat of Antiochus Epiphanes and his generals, who had profaned Jerusalem and the Temple.
For when the leader came into Persia — referring to Antiochus Epiphanes, who went to Persia to collect tribute and plunder temples.

And the army that seemed invincible was scattered in the temple of Nanea by the deceit of the priests of Nanea — Nanea was a Persian goddess identified with Artemis or Venus; her priests treacherously killed Antiochus when he came to plunder her temple. # GEM

For Antiochus, as if he would marry her, came into the place with his friends that he might receive great sums of money under the title of a dowry — that is, Antiochus pretended to wish to take the goddess as his bride, in order to gain access to her treasury.

And when the priests of Nanea had set it forth and he had entered with a small company within the wall of the temple — showing how the trap was laid by limiting the number who could enter with the king.
They shut the temple when Antiochus had come in — thus trapping him inside with only a few attendants.

And opening a secret entrance of the ceiling, they threw stones and struck down the leader and them that were with him, and hewed them in pieces and cut off their heads and threw them to those that were without — a detailed account of the violent end of the great persecutor. # GEM
Blessed be God in all things, who has delivered up the wicked — the sacred writer breaks into a doxology upon narrating the death of the tyrant, seeing in it the hand of divine justice.

Therefore whereas we are now purposed to keep the purification of the temple in the month of Casleu, on the five and twentieth day — the feast of Dedication (Hanukkah) began on the 25th of Casleu, the same day on which three years earlier Antiochus had profaned the altar.

We thought it necessary to signify it to you, that you also may keep the day of Tabernacles and the day of the fire — the letter's primary purpose is now stated: to invite the Egyptian Jews to celebrate this feast along with their brethren in Judea.

Which was given when Nehemiah offered sacrifice, after the temple and the altar were built — the author now introduces the story of the miraculous fire to encourage and instruct the Egyptian Jews, connecting the new dedication to ancient precedents.

For when our fathers were led into Persia, the priests that then were worshippers of God took privately the fire from the altar and hid it in a valley where there was a deep pit without water — this is the story of the hiding of the sacred fire at the time of the Babylonian captivity. # GEM
And there it was kept secret for a long time, till in the time of Nehemiah — showing that God preserved the holy fire through the years of captivity, as a sign that he would restore his people.
When Nehemiah was sent by the king of Persia — Artaxerxes I (or according to some, Artaxerxes II) sent Nehemiah to Jerusalem to rebuild the city and its walls.
He sent the descendants of the priests who had hidden it to look for the fire — showing the care taken to recover what had been divinely preserved.

And as they told us, they found no fire but thick water — that is, a thick, oily liquid, which the Fathers interpret as a kind of naphtha or petroleum, which has the property of catching fire easily. # GEM
Then he commanded them to draw it up and bring it to him — Nehemiah's faith led him to use even this unexpected substance in the service of God.

And after the sacrifices were offered, Nehemiah commanded the priests to pour the water over the wood and the things laid upon it — this detail shows that the miracle was preceded by the proper ritual actions.

And when it was done and some time had passed, and the sun which had been clouded over shone out — the shining of the sun is part of the miraculous sign, the cloud giving way to reveal the sun's light.
A great fire blazed up, so that all marveled — the sudden ignition of the wet wood and sacrifice by the sunlight shining on the naphtha was a clear divine sign. # GEM
And while the sacrifice was being consumed, the priests and everyone prayed — showing that miracles are accompanied by prayer and worship, and that the two go together.
The prayer of Jonathan leading, and the rest responding — a beautiful picture of liturgical prayer, with a leader and the congregation responding together.
The prayer itself is a sublime act of adoration, confessing God as the Lord of all creation, the one God, the Father and God of Israel. # GEM
Who alone art generous, who alone art good, who alone art just and almighty and eternal — this accumulation of divine attributes is a magnificent doxology, worthy of meditation. # GEM
Receive the sacrifice for all your people Israel — the prayer asks that the sacrifice be accepted on behalf of the whole nation, not just those present.

Gather together our scattered people, deliver those who are slaves among the Gentiles — a prayer for the ingathering of the exiles, which has both a literal and a spiritual meaning: the return from Babylon literally, and the gathering of all God's people spiritually. # GEM
Punish those who oppress and insult us — a prayer for divine justice against the persecutors of Israel, which is not mere vengeance but a cry for God to vindicate his name and his people.

Plant your people in your holy place, as Moses said — the reference is to the song of Moses in Exodus 15:17, where he prophesies that God will plant his people in the mountain of his inheritance. # GEM
Then the priests sang hymns — after the prayer and sacrifice, the liturgy continues with hymns, showing the ordered nature of the sacred worship.
And when the materials of the sacrifice were consumed, Nehemiah commanded that the remaining water be poured upon great stones — the disposal of the miraculous liquid is done with reverence.
When this was done, a flame blazed up, but the light from the altar shone back and went out — a further miraculous sign confirming the divine approval of the action.

When this matter became known and it was told to the king of the Persians — the miracle became widely known and reached even the Persian court, showing that God's power was recognized even by the Gentiles.
The king examined the matter and enclosed the place and made it sacred — the Persian king honored the site of the miracle, showing that even pagan rulers could recognize divine power. # GEM
And the king gave many presents to those whom he thought worthy — a further sign of the royal recognition of the miracle and the people of God.

And Nehemiah and his associates called this thing Nephthar, which means purification — the naming of the miraculous liquid with a Hebrew/Aramaic word meaning cleansing or purification connects it symbolically with the purification of the Temple being celebrated in the feast of Dedication.

To the brethren, the Jews throughout Egypt, greeting and good health — He writes to the Jews of Egypt, that is, to those dwelling in Alexandria and throughout all Egypt, where there were very many Jews after the deportation under the Ptolemies.
The Jews of Jerusalem and those in Judea send greeting — The author of this letter is Judas Maccabaeus with the elders and the whole people, as appears from verse 10.
God be gracious to you — That is, may He show Himself favorable and kind to you in all things.

And remember His covenant with Abraham, Isaac and Jacob — That is, the covenant of circumcision and the promises made to the patriarchs, by which He promised to be their God and the God of their seed forever.
Give you all a heart to worship Him and to do His will — That is, may He give you a devout and willing heart, prompt and ready for divine worship and for fulfilling His commandments.
Open your hearts in His law and commandments — That is, may He illuminate your minds to understand the law and the prophets more deeply and perfectly. # GEM
Hear your prayers and be reconciled to you — That is, may He be appeased and turn away His wrath from you, and grant you His grace and favor.
We are now praying for you here — That is, we who are in Jerusalem and Judea pray earnestly for you who are in Egypt, that God may grant you all these things.
In the reign of Demetrius, in the one hundred and sixty-ninth year — This was the year of the Seleucid era, which corresponds approximately to 143 BC.

We the Jews wrote to you in the tribulation and in the violence that came upon us in those years — That is, we sent you a letter during those persecutions and troubles, when Jason and his followers revolted from the sacred covenant and defiled the sanctuary.

After Jason and his company revolted from the holy land and the kingdom — Jason, the high priest, a man most wicked and impious, abandoned the law and introduced Greek customs, as is related more fully in chapter 4.
They burned the gate and shed innocent blood — That is, Jason and his followers, when they attacked the city by force, killed many innocents and set fire to the gates of the temple or of the city.

We therefore have written to you that ye keep the feast of tabernacles in the month Casleu — The Jews are here exhorted to celebrate the feast of the dedication of the temple, which fell in the month of Casleu, that is, November-December, instituted by Judas Maccabaeus, as narrated in 1 Maccabees 4.
In the one hundred and eighty-eighth year — This second letter begins here, written in the year 188 of the Seleucid era, approximately 124 BC.

The people of Jerusalem and Judea, the senate, and Judas — Here are named the authors of this second letter: the people, the senate or Sanhedrin, and Judas Maccabaeus, who was still living at this time.
To Aristobulus, the teacher of King Ptolemy — Aristobulus was a learned Jew, a Peripatetic philosopher, who dedicated a commentary on the law of Moses to Ptolemy Philometor, as Eusebius attests.
Being delivered by God out of great perils — Judas gives thanks to God for his victories over Antiochus and his generals.
For He drove out those who fought against the holy city — That is, God Himself put to flight and destroyed the forces of Antiochus, not our valor alone.
Their leader himself perishing in Persia — This refers to the death of Antiochus Epiphanes, who died miserably in Persia, as is narrated at length in chapter 9.
For the leader had come into Persia with a great army — Antiochus entered Persia with a mighty host intending to plunder the temple of Nanaea, that is, Diana or Venus, and there he perished.
Under pretense of marrying her — That is, Antiochus feigned that he wished to contract a sacred marriage with the goddess Nanaea, so that under this pretext he might more easily seize her treasures.
And when the priests of Nanaea had set out the treasure — The priests exposed the treasure in order to entice Antiochus to enter the temple, and then they slew him.

Taking the stones they slew the leader and his men — They cast down heavy stones from above through a hidden entrance, killing Antiochus and his companions. Thus God punished the sacrilegious robber with a fitting death.
Blessed be our God in all things, who delivered up the ungodly — Judas breaks forth in thanksgiving to God for the punishment of Antiochus, the persecutor of the Jews.

Whereas we are now purposed to keep the purification of the temple — This is the main purpose of the letter: to urge the Egyptian Jews to celebrate with them the feast of the dedication or purification of the temple, restored by Judas Maccabaeus.

Upon the five and twentieth day of the month Casleu — This date, the 25th of Casleu, is the feast of dedication, called Hanukkah, instituted by Judas after he purified the temple from the profanations of Antiochus.

For when our fathers were led into Persia, the priests that were then devout took the fire of the altar privily — Here begins the account of the miraculous fire hidden by the priests at the time of the Babylonian captivity.
And hid it in a hollow place of a pit without water — They concealed the sacred fire in a dry cistern or pit, so that it would be preserved and not extinguished.
Now after many years, when it pleased God — After seventy years of captivity, when the Jews returned from Babylon under Zerubbabel.
Nehemiah, being sent from the king of Persia — Nehemiah was sent by Artaxerxes king of Persia to rebuild Jerusalem and restore divine worship.
Sent of the posterity of those priests that had hid it to the fire — Nehemiah sent the descendants of the priests who had hidden the fire to find it and bring it for the sacrifice.
But when they told us they found no fire, but thick water — They found not fire but a thick, viscous liquid resembling mud or naphtha, which had been transformed from fire over the long years.
And when they offered the sacrifice on it, they were commanded to pour that water on the logs — By Nehemiah's command this thick liquid was poured over the wood of the sacrifice.
The fire blazed up — When the sun shone upon it, the naphtha ignited and the sacrifice was consumed, which was a manifest miracle confirming the divine approval of the restored worship. # GEM

And Nehemiah made a long prayer — Nehemiah prayed a long and devout prayer as the sacrifice was being offered, giving thanks to God for the return from captivity and for the restoration of divine worship.

O Lord, Lord God, Creator of all things — The prayer of Nehemiah here recorded is solemn and beautiful, ascribing to God His principal attributes: omnipotence, justice, mercy, and fidelity to His covenant. # GEM
Thou only art Lord, thou art mighty — God alone is truly Lord and omnipotent; all creatures depend entirely upon Him and have their power only from Him.
The only Savior — God alone is the proper and principal Savior of men, working salvation by His own power; men are only instruments.
Receive the sacrifice for all thy people Israel — Nehemiah asks God to accept the sacrifice offered for the whole people, forgiving their sins and restoring His favor.

Gather those together who are dispersed from us — A prayer for the gathering of the scattered Jews from all the nations where they had been dispersed, which was fulfilled under Zerubbabel and Nehemiah, and more perfectly under the Messiah. # GEM
Deliver them that serve among the heathen — That is, those Jews still remaining in captivity and servitude among the Gentiles.
Look upon them that are despised and abhorred — God is asked to turn His merciful eyes upon the afflicted and contemned Jewish people.
Plant thy people again in thy holy place — That is, restore the people to the Holy Land and to Jerusalem, as a gardener replants a tree that has been uprooted.
And the priests sang hymns — After the sacrifice was consumed by the miraculous fire, the priests broke forth in hymns and canticles of praise and thanksgiving.

And as soon as the sacrifice was consumed, Nehemiah commanded the water that was left to be poured upon the great stones — Whatever remained of the naphtha or thick water was poured upon the stones, perhaps to show that the miracle was complete and the matter consumed.
When this was done, a flame was kindled — When the sun shone upon the remaining naphtha poured on the stones, it was again kindled into flame, confirming the miracle.
This thing was not kept secret — The report of this miracle spread everywhere and came to the ears of the king of Persia himself.
And the king, inclosing the place, made it holy — The king of Persia, moved by the miracle, ordered the place to be enclosed and consecrated, granting special privileges to those who kept it.
And Nehemiah called this thing Nephthar — The thick liquid or naphtha was given this name, which means cleansing or purification in Hebrew, because it purified and kindled the sacrifice.
But many men call it Nephi — That is, the common people gave it a slightly different name, derived from the Hebrew or Aramaic word for fire or kindling.

Simon, who had informed against the temple treasury, now slandered Onias to the king, saying that it was Heliodorus who had plotted against the king, and that Onias had been the instigator of these evils.
He dared to call the benefactor of the city, the guardian of his countrymen, and the zealot of the laws, an enemy of the state.
When the enmity had grown to such a degree that even murders were committed by some of Simon's approved men,
Onias considered that the rivalry was dangerous, and that Apollonius, the son of Menestheus, the governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, was provoking Simon's malice,
he went to the king, not to be an accuser of his fellow citizens, but looking to the public and private good of all the people.
For he saw that without the king's providence it was impossible for the state to have peace, and that Simon would not cease from his folly.
But after Seleucus departed this life, Antiochus, called Epiphanes, succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corruption,
promising the king three hundred and sixty talents of silver, and from another revenue eighty talents.

Beyond this he promised to pay a hundred and fifty more, if power were given him to set up a place for exercise and a company of youths for it, and to enroll the men of Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch.
When the king had assented, and he had gotten possession of the office, he immediately began to bring his own nation over to the Greek fashion.

He set aside the royal concessions granted to the Jews through the mediation of John the father of Eupolemus, who went on the embassy to Rome for friendship and alliance, and doing away with the lawful ways of living, he introduced new customs against the law.
For he eagerly established a place of exercise under the tower itself, and he brought the chief young men under his power, and made them wear the broad-brimmed hat.
There was such an extreme of Hellenism and increase of foreign customs through the exceeding wickedness of Jason, that ungodly wretch and no high priest,

that the priests were no longer earnest about the services of the altar; but despising the sanctuary, and neglecting the sacrifices, they hastened to enjoy what was unlawfully provided in the palaestra after the signal for the discus-throwing,
not setting by the honors of their fathers, but liking the glory of the Greeks best of all.
By reason of this, heavy calamity beset them; and they whose customs they followed and whom they desired to imitate in everything became their enemies and punished them.
For it is not a light thing to do impiously against the divine laws, as the time following will declare.
Now when the game that was held every fifth year was kept at Tyre, and the king was present,

the vile Jason sent sacred envoys, as being Antiochians of Jerusalem, bearing three hundred drachmas of silver to the sacrifice of Hercules, which even the bearers thought it not right to use for any sacrifice, because it was not fitting, but to spend on another charge.

So the money intended by the sender for the sacrifice to Hercules was applied, by the decision of those who carried it, to the making of triremes. # GEM Here Lapide notes the scruple of conscience shown even by pagan messengers, who refused to apply sacred money to idolatrous sacrifice, showing that natural law writes reverence for sacred things on all hearts.

Now when Apollonius the son of Menestheus was sent into Egypt for the first admission of Philometor as king, Antiochus, understanding that Philometor had become hostile to his government, took measures for his own security; so he went to Joppa, and from there to Jerusalem.
He was magnificently received by Jason and the city, and ushered in with torchlight and with shoutings, and from there he marched his army into Phoenicia.
Now after a space of three years Jason sent Menelaus, the brother of the Simon mentioned before, to convey the money to the king, and to make reports concerning matters of business.
But Menelaus, having been commended to the king, flattered him with an air of authority, and outbid Jason by three hundred talents of silver, and so brought the high priesthood to himself.
He came back with the royal mandate, possessing nothing worthy of the high priesthood, but having the passion of a cruel tyrant and the rage of a savage beast.
So Jason, who had undermined his own brother, was himself undermined and driven as a refugee into the country of the Ammonites.
And Menelaus held the office, but paid nothing of the money he had promised to the king.
And Sostratus the captain of the citadel required payment; for to him belonged the gathering of the revenues. So they were both called by the king to account for this matter.
And Menelaus left his own brother Lysimachus as deputy in the high priesthood; and Sostratus left Crates, who was over the Cyprians.
Now while these things happened, it came to pass that they of Tarsus and Mallus made an insurrection, because they were to be given as a gift to Antiochis the king's concubine.
The king therefore came to Tarsus in haste to appease them; and he left Andronicus, one of his nobles, to be his deputy.

But Menelaus, thinking he had obtained a convenient opportunity, presented to Andronicus certain golden vessels belonging to the temple, some of which had been given, and others he had himself taken, from the temple; and this he had already done with some having been sold to Tyre and the neighboring cities.
When Onias had certain knowledge of these things, he rebuked Menelaus, having withdrawn himself into a sanctuary at Daphne, near Antioch.

Whereupon Menelaus, privately gaining Andronicus, urged him to kill Onias. So Andronicus came to Onias, and giving him pledges with oaths, and reaching to him his right hand, persuaded him to come out of the sanctuary, and immediately, with no regard for justice, he slew him.

For which cause not only the Jews, but many also of the other nations, were indignant, and bore it ill for the unjust slaying of the man. # GEM Lapide notes this as remarkable testimony that even pagans recognized the sanctity of Onias and the inviolability of sanctuary, proving the natural law inscribed in all mankind concerning sacred asylum.

And when the king had come back from the places in Cilicia, the Jews that were in the city, and certain of the Greeks also that abhorred the wicked deed, came to him to complain about the murder of Onias.
Therefore Antiochus was grieved in his soul, and was moved to pity, and wept, because of the sober and modest life of him who was dead.

And being inflamed with anger, he immediately stripped Andronicus of his purple, and tore off his garments, and when he had led him through the whole city to that very place where he had committed the impiety against Onias, there he slew the murderer; the Lord rendering to him the punishment he deserved.

Now when many sacrileges had been committed in the city by Lysimachus with the knowledge of Menelaus, and when the report of them had spread abroad, the people assembled against Lysimachus, because many of the golden vessels had already been dispersed.

When the mob had gathered in their anger, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men, and with unjust violence began the conflict; a certain Auranus leading the onset, a man already old and no less advanced in folly.

But when the Jews perceived the assault of Lysimachus, some caught up the stones, some blocks of wood, some took handfuls of the ashes that lay there, and threw them all confusedly upon Lysimachus and his company;
by which means having wounded many of them, they brought some to the ground, and put all the rest to flight; and as for the temple robber himself, they slew him beside the treasury.
Now touching these matters, a charge was laid against Menelaus.
When the king came to Tyre, three men were sent from the senate to plead their cause before him.
But Menelaus, being already as good as beaten, promised a substantial sum of money to Ptolemy the son of Dorymenes, to the end that he might win over the king.
Ptolemy therefore, taking the king aside into a certain portico as if to take the air, brought him to change his mind.

And Menelaus, who was the cause of all the mischief, he acquitted of the charges against him; while those poor men who, if they had spoken their cause even before Scythians, would have been judged innocent, these he sentenced to death. # GEM Lapide observes here the bitter irony that even barbarian Scythians, proverbially the most savage of peoples, would have acquitted these innocent men, yet they perished by corrupt royal justice — a testimony to how far tyranny exceeds even barbarism in wickedness.
So they immediately suffered unjust punishment who had pleaded for the city, for the people, and for the sacred vessels.
Wherefore even certain Tyrians, being moved with hatred of the wickedness, provided magnificently for their burial.
But Menelaus, through the covetousness of those who had power, remained still in his office, growing in malice, and having become the chief plotter against his fellow citizens.
What you do, do quickly (John 13:27) — cited at p.590, from the Second Epistle to Timothy.
From the Epistle to the Hebrews, ch. 9, v. 4: 'In which was a golden urn containing manna.' [Reference to Heb 9:4 regarding the golden urn of manna]
From the Epistle to the First Letter of St. Peter [cross-reference noted]
Hebrews 11:30: 'By faith the walls of Jericho fell after being encircled for seven days.' [Cross-reference to Heb 11:30 regarding the walls of Jericho falling after seven days of encirclement]
This chapter treats of the second expedition of Antiochus against Jerusalem, the slaughter of the Jews, the profanation of the Temple, and the installation of Philip as governor of Judaea.

Lapide notes that "at that time" refers to the period when Antiochus was preparing his second campaign against Egypt, having been repulsed the first time, and that this setting serves to show how the affliction of Jerusalem coincided with the ambitions of a tyrant abroad.

The apparitions of horsemen seen in the air over Jerusalem for forty days were a divine warning of the calamities about to fall upon the city, similar to the prodigies described by Josephus before the destruction of Jerusalem by Titus.
Lapide observes that God in His mercy always forewarns a people before punishing them, so that these celestial visions were signs of divine clemency as much as of impending wrath. # GEM

The vision of armed troops rushing together in the sky, with clashing of shields and a forest of spears, and gold-armored horsemen riding in array, signified the armies that would shortly devastate Judaea.

All who saw these portents prayed that they might be turned to good; Lapide notes that such heavenly signs are ambiguous in themselves and that the devout instinct to pray for a favorable interpretation reflects sound theology.

When Antiochus was reported dead, Jason, who had been deposed from the high priesthood, attacked Jerusalem with a thousand soldiers, seized the city, and drove Menelaus onto the citadel; but he failed to maintain his position.

Lapide notes that Jason's assault illustrates how ambition for sacred office, obtained by simony and fraud, inevitably ends in greater ruin; he had purchased the priesthood wickedly and now attempted to recover it by arms.

Jason slaughtered his own fellow citizens without pity, not realizing that success against one's own kindred is the greatest of disasters; Lapide comments that civil war is the cruelest of all wars, being a sin against nature and charity alike.
Jason did not secure power but only shame; he was compelled to flee again into the land of the Ammonites, defeated in his treacherous enterprise.

Lapide recounts how Jason, driven from place to place, was hated by all as an apostate and enemy of his nation; he fled to the Lacedaemonians hoping to find protection on account of a claimed kinship, but found none.
Jason, who had driven many into exile, himself died in exile, having gone to Egypt and then sailing to the Lacedaemonians; he perished miserably, unlamented and unburied.
He who had cast out many to lie unburied had no one to mourn for him and received no burial of any kind, no funeral rites, and no place in the tomb of his fathers.
Lapide draws the moral that divine justice is perfectly poetic: Jason had denied others burial and was himself denied it; the same measure he used was measured back to him. # GEM
When Antiochus heard of the disturbances in Jerusalem caused by Jason, he interpreted these events as a revolt of the whole Jewish people and marched against Jerusalem in a fury of rage.
He commanded his soldiers to cut down without mercy all whom they met; he himself led the slaughter of citizens in the streets of the holy city, a most savage and impious act.

There was massacre of young and old, destruction of women and children, slaughter of virgins and infants; within three days, eighty thousand perished, forty thousand taken prisoner, and as many sold into slavery.

Lapide laments that these numbers, drawn from Josephus and confirmed by the sacred text, demonstrate how terrible are the chastisements God permits tyrants to inflict upon an apostate and sinful people.
Not content with his slaughter, Antiochus dared to enter the Temple itself, guided by Menelaus the traitor to his own nation and priest, who was more impious than Antiochus himself.

Lapide observes that Menelaus is here called a traitor to the laws and to his own people, because he who should have been the guardian of the holy things became the instrument of their profanation; simony and apostasy brought him to this depth of wickedness. # GEM
Antiochus seized with his polluted hands the sacred vessels which had been dedicated by other kings for the glory and honor of the Temple, and handled them with sacrilegious insolence.

Lapide notes that the author of Second Maccabees pauses to reflect theologically: Antiochus was puffed up in his mind and did not see that God permitted this outrage only because of the sins of the inhabitants of the city.
Had the Jews not sinned so gravely, Antiochus would have been scourged and turned back from his presumption as soon as he approached the Temple, just as Heliodorus had been in the time of Onias.

Lapide comments that this theological parenthesis is among the most important in the whole book: sin opens the sanctuary to the profaner, while holiness is the only true defense of sacred things. # GEM

God did not choose the place for the sake of the people, but the people for the sake of the place; that is, the Temple and the holy city were given as a gift to a worthy people, and when the people became unworthy, the gift was withdrawn.

Lapide here develops the principle at length: the external means of grace—temples, sacraments, sacred ministers—are given for the sake of souls; when souls despise them, God permits those means to be taken away or violated. # GEM
Therefore the holy place itself shared in the calamities of the nation when it had been abandoned by the Almighty; but when the great Lord was reconciled, it was restored with full glory.

Antiochus departed from Jerusalem carrying off eighteen hundred talents from the Temple, imagining in his pride that he could make the land navigable by foot and the sea passable on foot—a proverbial expression for supreme arrogance.
He left governors to afflict the people: Philip, a Phrygian by birth, more barbarous in character than the man who set him there, was placed over Jerusalem.
At Gerizim he installed Andronicus; and in addition Menelaus was set over the citizens as a tool of oppression; for all these men harbored an ill will toward their Jewish subjects.

Antiochus sent Apollonius with an army of twenty-two thousand men with orders to slay all the adult males and to sell the women and younger people into slavery; a command of extraordinary and diabolical cruelty.
Apollonius arrived in Jerusalem with a show of peace and waited until the holy Sabbath day to make his move, knowing that the Jews would not fight on that day.

Lapide notes that this stratagem of attacking on the Sabbath was characteristic of the enemies of Israel; it illustrates how the enemies of God exploit the very piety of the faithful as a weapon against them.
Then Apollonius ordered his troops out and slew all who came out to the spectacle; running through the city with his armed soldiers he cut down a great multitude of people.

But Judas Maccabeus, with about nine others, withdrew into the wilderness to live like wild animals in the mountains, sustaining themselves on herbs so as not to be defiled by the forbidden foods of the Gentiles.

Lapide praises Judas for this triple virtue: zeal for the law of God, fortitude in enduring the harshness of desert life, and prudence in preserving himself and his companions for the future liberation of his people; seeing in him a figure of those who flee into solitude rather than apostatize. # GEM
Jason obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means, bribing Antiochus with silver, thus introducing the Hellenistic customs which would corrupt Jewish life and worship.
He who had been a guardian of his people and a zealot for the laws was now called a subverter of the laws by his enemies, though this charge was false and malicious.
When Antiochus Epiphanes succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corrupt negotiation, promising the king three hundred and sixty talents of silver.

He furthermore promised to pay a hundred and fifty more, if he might have the authority to establish a gymnasium and a body of youth for it, and to register the inhabitants of Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch.
There was such an extreme of Hellenism and advance of an alien religion, because of the exceeding wickedness of Jason, that ungodly man and no true high priest.
Jason sent envoys as if they were Antiochians from Jerusalem, bearing three hundred drachmas of silver for the sacrifice to Hercules. # GEM
After a period of three years, Jason sent Menelaus, the brother of the previously mentioned Simon, to convey the money to the king and to supply reports on important matters.
Menelaus stole certain golden vessels from the temple and gave them to Andronicus.
Andronicus treacherously murdered Onias the legitimate high priest, drawing him out from the place of sanctuary with false oaths.
The king returned from Cilicia and punished Andronicus with death for the murder of the righteous Onias. # GEM
A charge was brought against Menelaus before Antiochus concerning the stolen golden vessels.
Menelaus, owing to the greed of those in power, remained in office, growing in wickedness, having become the chief plotter against his fellow citizens.
The seven brothers with their mother are arrested and compelled by the king to eat pork contrary to the law, and when they refuse, they are tortured.
Note that this chapter contains one of the most celebrated examples of martyrdom in the Old Testament, indeed a prefiguration of Christian martyrdom.
The first brother speaks boldly, saying they are ready to die rather than transgress the laws of their fathers.
The king in his fury commands that pans and caldrons be heated, a punishment of extraordinary cruelty.
The first brother is tortured with scalping, cutting off of hands and feet, and frying while still alive, yet he and his brothers encourage one another.
The mother especially was admirable, encouraging her sons with a manly spirit, inflamed with feminine emotion yet reinforced by masculine reason.
The first brother, dying, says: the Lord God will look upon us and will comfort us, as Moses declared in his canticle: He will have mercy on his servants.

When the first was dead in this manner, they brought the second to the mockery, and they tore off the skin of his head with the hair and asked if he would eat before being tormented through all his members.
The second brother answers in the language of his fathers, that he will not do so; therefore he also received the next tortures.

The second brother at the last breath says: the King of the world will raise us up, who die for his laws, in the resurrection of eternal life. # GEM Here is explicit Old Testament testimony to bodily resurrection and eternal life as the reward of martyrdom.
After him the third was made a mockery, and when they demanded he put out his tongue he did so quickly, and stretched forth his hands courageously.

The third brother says nobly: these I have from heaven, and for the laws of God I now despise them, and from him I hope to receive them again. # GEM A remarkable confession of bodily resurrection: the very members lost for God shall be restored by God.
So that the king and his company were astonished at the young man's courage, for he regarded the pains as nothing.
After he was dead, they tormented the fourth in like manner.

The fourth brother, when he was now ready to die, said: it is better, being put to death by men, to look for hope from God to be raised up again by him; as for you, you shall have no resurrection unto life. # GEM A clear distinction between the resurrection unto life of the martyrs and the damnation of the persecutor.
After him they brought the fifth and tormented him.
The fifth brother looked at the king and said: you have power among men, though you are corruptible; you do what you will; but think not that God has forsaken our nation.
The fifth warns Antiochus to wait and see the great power of God, how he will torment him and his seed.

After him they brought the sixth, who dying said: do not deceive yourself in vain, for we suffer these things for ourselves, having sinned against our God; therefore things worthy of admiration are done unto us.
The sixth adds: but think not that you who have attempted to fight against God shall escape unpunished.

But the mother was wonderful above all, and worthy of honorable memory, for when she watched seven sons perish within the space of one day, she bore it with a good courage because of the hope she had in God.
She exhorted every one of them in the language of their fathers, filled with a courageous spirit, stirring up her womanly thoughts with manly courage.
She said to them: I cannot tell how you came into my womb, for I neither gave you breath nor life, neither was it I that formed the members of every one of you.

The mother professes faith in the Creator, saying: the Creator of the world who formed the generation of man and found out the beginning of all things, will also of his own mercy give you breath and life again, as you now regard not your own selves for his laws' sake. # GEM A magnificent confession of creation ex nihilo as the basis for belief in resurrection.

Antiochus, thinking himself despised and suspecting reproachful speech, when the youngest was yet alive, did not only make appeals to him in words but promised with oaths that he would make him rich and happy, and would make him his friend and trust him with affairs.
But when the young man would in no case hearken to him, the king called his mother and exhorted her to counsel the young man to save himself.
After he had exhorted her with many words, she promised she would counsel her son.

But she bowing herself toward the youngest son, laughing the cruel tyrant to scorn, spoke in the language of her fathers: O my son, have pity upon me who carried you nine months in my womb, and gave you suck three years, and nourished you, and brought you up to this age.

The mother urges: I beseech you, my son, look upon the heaven and the earth, and all that is therein, and consider that God made them of things that were not, and so was mankind made likewise. # GEM Here is the clearest scriptural affirmation of creation ex nihilo in the Old Testament, a foundational text for the doctrine.
Fear not this tormentor, but, being worthy of your brethren, take your death that I may receive you again in mercy with your brethren.

While the mother was yet speaking these words, the young man said: whom wait you for? I will not obey the commandment of the king, but I will obey the commandment of the law that was given unto our fathers by Moses.
The youngest son says to Antiochus: you that have been the author of all mischief against the Hebrews shall not escape the hands of God.
The youngest acknowledges: for we suffer because of our sins.
He continues: and though the living Lord be angry with us a little while for our chastening and correction, yet shall he be at one again with his servants.
But you, O godless man, and of all other most wicked, be not lifted up without cause, nor puffed up with uncertain hopes, lifting up your hand against the servants of God.
For you have not yet escaped the judgment of Almighty God, who sees all things.

The youngest says: for our brethren, who have now suffered a short pain, are dead under God's covenant of everlasting life; but you, through the judgment of God, shall receive just punishment for your pride. # GEM The martyrs' brief suffering is contrasted with eternal life received under God's covenant, a precious testimony to martyrdom's eschatological reward.
The youngest offers himself to die for his country and people, praying that God would speedily be gracious unto the nation, and that the king might be brought to confess that God alone is God.
He prays also that the wrath of the Almighty, which is justly brought upon all the nation, may cease with his own death and his brethren's.
Then the king, being in a rage, handled him worse than all the rest, taking it grievously that he was mocked.
So the youngest brother died undefiled, and put his whole trust in the Lord.
Last of all after the sons, the mother died.
Let this be enough now to have spoken concerning the idolatrous feasts and the extreme tortures.
The Jews in Jerusalem and in the land of Judea send greetings to their brethren the Jews in Egypt, and wish them peace and good health.
May God be good to you, and remember His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, His faithful servants.
May He give you all a heart to worship Him and to do His will with a great heart and a willing soul.
May He open your heart in His law and in His commandments, and send you peace.
May He hear your prayers, and be reconciled to you, and not forsake you in the evil time.
And now here we are praying for you.

In the reign of Demetrius, in the year one hundred and sixty-nine, we Jews wrote to you in the tribulation and violence that came upon us in those years, after Jason and his company revolted from the holy land and from the kingdom.

And they burned the gate, and shed innocent blood; then we prayed to the Lord, and were heard; and we offered sacrifices and fine flour, and lighted the lamps, and set forth the loaves of proposition.
And now celebrate ye the days of Scenopegia in the month of Casleu.

In the year one hundred and eighty-eight, the people that are at Jerusalem and in Judea, and the senate, and Judas, to Aristobulus the preceptor of king Ptolemy, who is of the stock of the anointed priests, and to the Jews that are in Egypt, health and welfare.
Being delivered by God from great dangers, we give Him great thanks, forasmuch as we had such a one as Antiochus to fight against.
For He drove out them that fought against the holy city.
For when the leader was come into Persia, and the army with him that seemed invincible, they were cut to pieces in the temple of Nanea, by the deceit of the priests of Nanea.
For Antiochus, as though he would marry Nanea, came into the place, and his friends that were with him, to receive the money, as in the name of a dowry.
And when the priests of Nanea had set it forth, and he had entered with a small company into the compass of the temple, they shut the temple when Antiochus was come in.
And opening a secret entrance of the roof, they threw stones and slew the leader, and them that were with him, and hewed them in pieces, and cutting off their heads they threw them forth.
Blessed be God in all things, who hath delivered up the ungodly.

We are about therefore to celebrate the purification of the temple on the five and twentieth day of the month of Casleu; we thought it necessary to signify it to you, that you also may keep the day of Scenopegia, and the day of the fire, which was given when Nehemias offered sacrifice, after the temple and the altar were built.

For when our fathers were led into Persia, the priests that then were worshippers of God took privately the fire from the altar and hid it in a valley where there was a deep pit without water, and there they kept it safe, so that the place was unknown to all men.

But when many years had passed, and it pleased God that Nehemias should be sent by the king of Persia, he sent some of the posterity of those priests that had hid it, to seek for the fire; and as they told us, they found no fire, but thick water.

Then he bade them draw it up and bring it to him; and the priest Nehemias commanded the sacrifices that were laid on to be sprinkled with the same water, both the wood and the things that were laid upon it.
And when this was done, and the time came that the sun shone out, which before was in a cloud, there was a great fire kindled, so that all men marvelled.
And all the priests made prayer while the sacrifice was consuming, Jonathan beginning and the rest answering.
And the prayer of Nehemias was after this manner: O Lord God, Creator of all things, dreadful and strong, just and merciful, who alone art the good King,
Who alone art gracious, who alone art just and almighty and eternal, who deliverest Israel from all evil, who didst choose the fathers and didst sanctify them:
Receive the sacrifice for all thy people Israel, and preserve thy own portion and sanctify it.
Gather together our scattered people, deliver them that are in bondage among the Gentiles, and look upon them that are despised and abhorred: that the Gentiles may know that thou art our God.
Punish them that oppress us and that treat us injuriously with pride.
Plant thy people in thy holy place, as Moses hath spoken.
And the priests sung hymns till the sacrifice was consumed.
And when the sacrifice was consumed, Nehemias commanded the water that remained to be poured upon the great stones.
Which being done, there was kindled a flame from them; but it was consumed by the light that shone from the altar.

And the matter became known, and it was told to the king of Persia that in the place where the priests that were led away had hid the fire, there appeared water, and that Nehemias and his company had purified the sacrifices with it.
And the king considering and diligently examining the matter, made a temple to it to prove the wonder.
And when he had proved it, he gave the priests many goods and divers presents, and he took them with his own hand and gave them to those that deserved them.
And Nehemias called this place Nephthar, which is interpreted purification; but many call it Nephi. # GEM
Then Antiochus sent an old man of Antioch to compel the Jews to forsake the laws of their fathers, and not to live after the laws of God.

Lapide: Antiochus Epiphanes, inflamed with hatred against the Jewish religion, sent Geron the Athenian (as some read) or an old man from Athens, to force the Jews to abandon their ancestral laws and to live no longer according to the laws of God.
And to pollute also the temple in Jerusalem, and to call it the temple of Jupiter Olympius; and that in Garizim, of Jupiter the Defender of Strangers, as they were that dwelt in the place.

Lapide: The temple of Jerusalem was to be consecrated to Jupiter Olympius, the greatest of the pagan gods, and that on Mount Gerizim to Jupiter Xenius, i.e., the protector of strangers, because the Samaritans who dwelt there were themselves foreigners and colonists brought from Babylon and other places.
And very grievous was the coming in of this mischief, and hard to be endured.
Lapide: This was a most bitter and intolerable calamity, that the temple of the true God should be defiled with idols and given over to the worship of demons.

For the temple was full of riot and revelling by the Gentiles, who dallied with harlots, and had to do with women within the circuit of the holy places, and besides that brought in things that were not lawful.

Lapide: The Gentiles filled the temple with wantonness and revelry, bringing in harlots and committing fornication within the sacred precincts, and introducing other things that were unlawful and profane.
The altar also was filled with profane things, which the law forbiddeth.
Lapide: The altar of God was polluted with abominable and unlawful sacrifices, such as swine and other unclean animals forbidden by the Law of Moses.
Neither was it lawful to keep sabbath days or ancient feasts, nor to profess himself to be a Jew.
Lapide: The observance of the Sabbath and of the ancient feasts was forbidden under penalty of death, nor was it permitted to anyone to confess publicly that he was a Jew.

And in the day of the king's birth every month they were brought by bitter constraint to eat of the sacrifices; and when the feast of Bacchus was kept, the Jews were compelled to go in procession to Bacchus, carrying ivy.

Lapide: On the monthly birthday feast of Antiochus, Jews were compelled by force to eat of the sacrificial meats offered to idols; and at the feast of Dionysus (Bacchus) they were driven to walk in the procession crowned with ivy, which was sacred to Bacchus.

Moreover there went out a decree to the neighbour cities of the heathen, by the suggestion of Ptolemy, against the Jews, that they should observe the same fashions, and be partakers of their sacrifices.

Lapide: At the instigation of Ptolemy the son of Dorymenes, a decree went forth to the neighboring Greek cities commanding that they likewise compel the Jews dwelling among them to observe the same heathen rites and to partake of their sacrifices.
And whoso would not conform themselves to the manners of the Gentiles should be put to death: therefore many were the distresses they suffered.
Lapide: Those who refused to conform to the customs of the Gentiles were to be killed; and thus great misery and tribulation came upon the Jewish people.

For there were two women brought, who had circumcised their children; whom when they had led about through the city, with the babes hanging at their breasts, they cast them down headlong from the wall.

Lapide: Two women were apprehended who had circumcised their sons; they were led through the city publicly as a spectacle, with the infants hanging from their necks, and then were cast headlong from the top of the city wall. # GEM

And others, that had run together into caves near by, to keep the sabbath day secretly, being discovered by Philip, were all burnt together, because they made a scruple to defend themselves, for the honour of the most sacred day.

Lapide: Others who had hidden themselves in nearby caves to keep the Sabbath in secret were discovered by Philip, the governor appointed over Jerusalem, and were burned alive together, for they refused to resist and fight on the Sabbath day, out of reverence for the sacred day.
Now I beseech those that read this book, that they be not discouraged at these calamities, but that they judge those punishments not to be for destruction, but for a chastening of our nation.

Lapide: The author here interrupts his narrative to address his readers, begging them not to be disheartened by these terrible sufferings, but to understand that these punishments were not meant for the destruction of the people, but for their chastisement and correction.
For it is a token of his great goodness, when wicked men are not suffered for a long time, but forthwith punished.

Lapide: It is a sign of God's great goodness and mercy that He does not allow sinners to go unpunished for a long time, but chastises them quickly, so that they may repent and not accumulate sins unto a greater condemnation.
For not as with other nations, whom the Lord patiently forbeareth to punish, till they be come to the full of their sins, so dealeth he with us.

Lapide: God deals with the Jews differently than with the Gentile nations: with the pagans He waits patiently until the measure of their iniquities is full before He punishes them; but with His own people Israel He chastises them sooner and more quickly, as a father chastises beloved children.
Lest that, being come to the height of sin, he might afterwards take vengeance of us.

Lapide: This is so that, if God waited until the Jews had reached the fullness of their sins, He might then have to punish them with utter destruction, as He did the Canaanites and other pagan nations.
And therefore he never withdraweth his mercy from us: and though he punish with adversity, yet doth he never forsake his people.

Lapide: God never withdraws His mercy entirely from His people Israel; He chastises them with tribulation, but He does not abandon them altogether, for He has made an eternal covenant with them. # GEM
But let this that we have spoken be for a warning unto us. And now let us come to the declaring of the matter in a more brief manner.
Lapide: Let what has been said serve as an admonition and consolation to readers; the author now returns to the narrative and promises to be more concise in the account that follows.
Eleazar, one of the principal scribes, an aged man, and of a well favoured countenance, was constrained to open his mouth, and to eat swine's flesh.

Lapide: Eleazar, one of the chief scribes, a man of advanced age and noble appearance, was compelled by the agents of Antiochus to open his mouth and eat pork, which was strictly forbidden by the Mosaic Law.
But he, choosing rather to die gloriously, than to live stained with such an abomination, spit it forth, and came of his own accord to torment.

Lapide: Eleazar, preferring a glorious death to a life defiled by such an abomination, spat out the flesh and voluntarily went forward to endure the torture prepared for those who refused, showing great courage and nobility of soul.
As it behoved them to come, that are resolute to stand against such things, as cannot be lawfully tasted for love of life.

Lapide: This is how men ought to behave who are resolved to refuse, even at the cost of life, to taste what the law of God forbids; they should advance freely and courageously to death rather than purchase life by sin.

But they that had the charge of that wicked feast, for the old acquaintance they had with the man, taking him aside, prayed him to bring flesh of his own provision, such as was lawful for him to use, and make as if he did eat of the flesh of the sacrifice commanded by the king.

Lapide: Those who were in charge of the impious sacrificial banquet, out of their old friendship and regard for Eleazar, took him aside privately and urged him to bring his own lawful food and merely pretend to eat the sacrificial meat, so as to avoid death.
That by doing so he might be delivered from death, and for the old friendship with them find favour.

Lapide: By this stratagem of merely pretending to eat the forbidden meat, Eleazar could save his life, and in return for their old friendship they would show him this kindness and look after his safety.
