But he began to consider discreetly, and as became his age, and the excellency of his ancient years, and the honour of his gray head, whereunto he was come, and his most honest education from a child, or rather the holy law made and given by God himself, he answered accordingly, and willed them straight ways to send him to the grave.

Lapide: Eleazar reflected carefully, as befitted a man of his age, his venerable gray hair, his upright upbringing from childhood, and above all the holy Law of God; and having thus deliberated, he answered them plainly that they should send him at once to his death.
For it became not our age, saith he, in any case to dissemble, whereby many young persons might think that Eleazar, being fourscore years and ten, were now gone to a strange religion.

Lapide: Eleazar argues that it is unbecoming for a man of ninety years of age to practice deception or dissimulation; for many young men, seeing him appear to apostatize, might be led astray into thinking that Eleazar, a teacher and elder, had really gone over to the heathen religion. # GEM
And so they through mine hypocrisy, and desire to live a little time and a moment longer, should be deceived by me, and I get a stain to mine old age, and make it abominable.

Lapide: If he were to simulate apostasy, young people would be scandalized and deceived by his hypocrisy; and he himself, for the sake of a brief and wretched extension of life, would bring shame and disgrace upon his old age, leaving an infamous memory.
For though for the present time I should be delivered from the punishment of men: yet should I not escape the hand of the Almighty, neither alive, nor dead.

Lapide: Even if he escaped the punishment of men by this deception, he could not escape the hand of the Almighty God, who judges both the living and the dead; the punishment of God is far more to be feared than that of men. # GEM
Wherefore now, manfully changing this life, I will shew myself such an one as mine age requireth.
Lapide: Therefore, departing this present life with manly courage, he will show himself worthy of his age, giving an example of virtue and constancy to those who come after him.
And leaving a notable example of virtue, to instruct the young, I will die willingly and courageously for the venerable and holy laws.
Lapide: He will die willingly and bravely for the sake of the holy and venerable laws of God, leaving to the young a glorious and memorable example of fidelity and fortitude. # GEM
And when he had said these things, he went immediately to the rack.
Lapide: Having spoken these words, Eleazar went immediately and voluntarily to the instruments of torture, showing by his actions the courage that his words had expressed.
And they that led him changing the good will they bare him a little before, into hatred, because the foresaid words were, as they thought, mere madness.

Lapide: Those who had previously treated him with friendship and kindness now turned against him with hatred, because they regarded the words he had spoken as sheer madness and folly, not recognizing the true virtue and wisdom that inspired them.

But when he was ready to die with stripes, he groaned, and said, It is manifest unto the Lord, who hath the holy knowledge, that whereas I might have been delivered from death, I suffer sharp pains in my body by being beaten: but in my soul I am well content to suffer these things, because I fear him.

Lapide: As he was about to die under the blows, Eleazar groaned and said that the Lord who has holy knowledge knows well that he could have escaped death, but he endures these sharp bodily pains willingly, because in his soul he suffers them gladly out of fear and reverence for God. # GEM
Thus this man died, leaving his death for an example of a noble courage, and a memorial of virtue, not only unto young men, but unto all his nation.

Lapide: So Eleazar died, leaving by his death a most noble example of courage and virtue, not only to young men but to the entire Jewish nation, showing that it is better to die for the Law of God than to live by transgressing it. # GEM
The prophet Jeremiah commanded those who were carried captive to take fire, as has been noted, and how he gave the law to those going into captivity.

And how he charged them that were carried away captive, that they should not forget the commandments of the Lord, and that they should not err in their minds, seeing the idols of gold and silver, and the ornaments of them.
And with other such speeches he exhorted them that they should not remove the law from their heart.

It was also contained in the same writing, that the prophet being warned by God, commanded that the tabernacle and the ark should follow with him, till he came forth to the mountain where Moses went up and saw the heritage of God.
And when Jeremiah came thither he found a hollow cave and he carried in thither the tabernacle, and the ark, and the altar of incense, and so stopped the door.
Then some of them that followed him came to mark the place and could not find it.

And when Jeremiah perceived it, he blamed them saying: The place shall be unknown till God gather together the congregation of the people and receive them to mercy. # GEM Lapide notes here that Jeremiah's hiding of the ark signifies that divine grace and the sacraments, once withdrawn from a sinful people, remain hidden until God in His mercy restores the congregation; the Fathers see in this a type of the Church's treasures preserved through persecution.

And then the Lord will shew these things, and the majesty of the Lord shall appear, and there shall be a cloud as it was also shewed to Moses, and he shewed it when Solomon prayed that the place might be sanctified to the great God.
For Solomon also celebrated the dedication with the same manner of prayer, and wisdom was given him to offer the sacrifice of the dedication and the finishing of the temple.
And as Moses prayed to the Lord, and fire came down from heaven and consumed the holocaust, so Solomon also prayed and fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offering.
And Moses said: Because the sin offering was not eaten, it was consumed so.
And Solomon kept the eight days in like manner.

Now these same things were set down in the memoirs and commentaries of Nehemias, and how he made a library and gathered together out of the countries the books concerning the kings and the prophets and the psalms of David, and the letters of the kings concerning the holy gifts.
In like manner Judas also gathered together all those things that had been scattered by the war that was made against us, and they are in our possession.
If therefore ye need these things, send some that may bring them to you.
As we are then about to celebrate the purification, we wrote to you; ye shall therefore do well if ye keep the same days.
For God who hath delivered his people and hath rendered to all the inheritance, and the kingdom, and the priesthood, and the sanctuary,

As he promised in the law, we hope that he will shortly have mercy on us and gather us together from every place under heaven into the holy place: for he hath delivered us from great perils and hath cleansed the place.
Now as concerning Judas Maccabeus and his brethren, and the purification of the great temple, and the dedication of the altar,
And the battles that appertained to Antiochus Epiphanes and his son Eupator,

And the manifestations that came from heaven to them that behaved themselves manfully to their honor for Judaism, so that, being but a few, they made themselves masters of the whole country, and put to flight the barbarian multitudes,
And recovered again the most renowned temple in all the world, and delivered the city, and restored the laws that were abolished, the Lord being propitious to them with all clemency:
And all such things as were comprised in five books by Jason of Cyrene, we have attempted to abridge in one book.
For considering the multitude of books and the difficulty that they find that desire to undertake the narrations of histories, because of the multitude of the matter,

We have taken care for those indeed that are willing to read, that it might be a pleasure of mind; and for the studious, that they may the more easily commit to memory; and for all those that take the books into their hands, that they may receive profit.
And for us who have taken upon us this painful labor of abridging, it is not easy but a matter of sweat and watching.
Even as it is no easy thing for one that prepareth a feast, and seeketh the benefit of others: yet for the sake of the thankfulness of many we will willingly undergo the labor.
Leaving to the authors the exact handling of every particular, and as for ourselves, according to the plan proposed, studying to be brief.

For as the master builder of a new house must have care of the whole building; but he that undertaketh to paint it and adorn it, must seek out fit things for the adorning of it: so must it be judged of us.
For to collect all that is to be known, to put in order the disposition of the whole history, and curiously to discuss every particular point, is the duty of the author of a history.
But to pursue brevity of speech, and to avoid nice declarations of things, is to be granted to him that maketh an abridgment.

Here then shall we begin the narration: let thus much suffice for a preface: for it is a foolish thing to make a long preamble before the history, and to abridge the history itself. # GEM Lapide observes that the epitomist's humility in confessing the toilsomeness of his work, and his distinction between the architect and the decorator, is praised by St. Jerome as a model for all who condense sacred learning, showing that brevity in service of piety is itself a virtue pleasing to God.

Simon, who had informed Heliodorus about the treasury, now falsely accused Onias to Apollonius, governor of Coele-Syria, saying that Onias had driven Heliodorus away and was plotting against the king. Thus the wicked man, having failed in his wicked design, turned his tongue to calumny against the innocent.

He called Onias a traitor against the state — Onias, who was in fact a benefactor of the city, a protector of his fellow countrymen, and zealous for the laws of God. Note that zeal for religion and for the laws of God was counted as sedition by the impious.
Simon's malice grew to such a degree that murders were committed by certain of his associates.
Onias, seeing the danger of the contention and that Apollonius, the governor of Coele-Syria and Phoenicia, was inflaming Simon's wickedness, appealed to the king.
He went to the king not to be an accuser of his fellow citizens, but for the common good of all the people, both publicly and privately considered.
For he saw that without the king's providence it was impossible that the state could obtain peace, and that Simon would not cease from his folly.
But after Seleucus died and Antiochus, called Epiphanes, succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means.
He promised the king three hundred and sixty talents of silver and eighty talents from other revenues.

He promised a hundred and fifty more if permission were given to set up a place for exercise and a body of youth to be trained therein, and to enroll the people of Jerusalem as citizens of Antioch. Note here the ambition and avarice of Jason, who purchased the sacred office with money — a manifest example of simony, condemned by all theologians. # GEM Chrysostom notes that simony is a sin against the Holy Spirit inasmuch as it traffics in sacred things which belong to the Spirit.
When the king had assented, and Jason had seized the office, he immediately brought over his fellow countrymen to the Greek way of life.

He set aside the privileges which the kings had granted to the Jews through the mediation of John the father of Eupolemus, who went on the mission to establish friendship and alliance with the Romans; he overthrew the lawful government and introduced customs contrary to the law.

For he eagerly established a gymnasium right under the citadel, and brought the finest of the young men under the trainer's hat. Note that Jason's corruption began with the introduction of pagan customs and pleasures, which drew youth away from the law and from piety. This is the usual method of tyrants and heretics: first corrupt morals, then corrupt doctrine.
There was such an extreme of Hellenization and increase in the adoption of foreign ways because of the surpassing wickedness of Jason, who was ungodly and not a true high priest.

That the priests were no longer devoted to the services of the altar; but despising the sanctuary and neglecting the sacrifices, they hastened to take part in the unlawful exercises in the wrestling arena after the signal for the discus-throwing.

They disdained the honors prized by their ancestors and put the highest value upon Greek forms of prestige. Note the terrible disorder: those who should have been ministers of God became servants of worldly vanity and pagan custom. # GEM Bernard observes that when prelates neglect divine things for human pleasures, the whole people falls into ruin.
For this reason heavy disaster overtook them, and those whose ways of living they admired and wished to imitate completely became their enemies and punished them.

It is no light thing to show irreverence to the divine laws — a fact which later events will make clear. Note this moral: those who abandon God's law and imitate the impious will be punished by those very people whom they imitated. God's justice is shown in this irony.
When the games that were held every five years at Tyre were in progress, with the king present.

The abominable Jason sent as spectators those who bore the title of Antiochians from Jerusalem, carrying three hundred silver drachmas for the sacrifice to Hercules. But even the bearers thought it not right to use it for a sacrifice, since that was not fitting, and they contributed it to the cost of the triremes. Note here a glimmer of conscience even in corrupted men: even those sent by the apostate Jason refused to contribute the money directly to a pagan sacrifice.

So the money was used for the building of triremes instead of the sacrifice to Hercules, because those who carried it deemed it wrong to use it for sacrifices. This divine providence overturned the plan of the impious Jason.

When Apollonius the son of Menestheus was sent to Egypt for the coronation of Philometor as king, Antiochus learned that Philometor had become hostile to his government, and so he departed from there to Joppa, and from there went to Jerusalem.
He was welcomed magnificently by Jason and the city, and was escorted in with torches and shouts; and so he marched his army into Phoenicia.
After a period of three years Jason sent Menelaus, the brother of the Simon mentioned before, to carry the money to the king and to complete the records of essential business.

But Menelaus, when presented to the king, extolled him with an air of authority, and secured the high priesthood for himself, outbidding Jason by three hundred talents of silver. Note again the evil of simony: Menelaus surpassed Jason in wickedness by offering a higher bribe, having no right to the high priesthood, not even being of the priestly tribe.
After receiving the king's orders he returned, possessing no qualification for the high priesthood, but having the hot temper of a cruel tyrant and the rage of a savage wild beast.

And Jason, who had supplanted his own brother, was supplanted by another man, was driven as a fugitive into the land of Ammon. Note the just judgment of God: Jason who had wrongfully displaced Onias was himself displaced by Menelaus. He who lives by the sword of ambition perishes by it. # GEM Gregory: the ambitious man who climbs by wrong means falls by the same means.
And Menelaus continued to hold the office, but he paid nothing of the money he had promised to the king.
Though Sostratus the captain of the citadel kept demanding payment — for the collection of the revenue was his responsibility — the two of them were summoned by the king on account of this matter.
Menelaus left his own brother Lysimachus as his deputy in the high priesthood, while Sostratus left Crates, who was in command of the Cyprians.
Now while such was the state of affairs, it happened that the people of Tarsus and Mallus revolted, because they had been given as a present to Antiochis the king's concubine.
The king went hastily to settle the trouble, leaving Andronicus, one of his courtiers, to act as his deputy.

But Menelaus, thinking he had obtained a suitable opportunity, stole some of the gold vessels from the temple and gave them to Andronicus; other vessels, as it happened, he had sold to Tyre and neighboring cities. Note the sacrilege of Menelaus, who added theft of sacred vessels to simony. Thus one crime leads to another when a man has abandoned conscience.
When Onias became fully aware of these acts he publicly reproached Menelaus, having first withdrawn to a place of sanctuary at Daphne near Antioch.

Therefore Menelaus, taking Andronicus aside, urged him to kill Onias. Andronicus came to Onias, and resorting to treachery offered him sworn pledges and persuaded him to come out from the place of sanctuary; then, with no regard for justice, he immediately put him out of the way. Note this execrable crime: Onias, the holy high priest, was murdered through treachery after being lured from sanctuary by false oaths. This is a type of the persecution of the innocent by the wicked.
For this reason not only Jews, but many also of other nations, were grieved and displeased at the unjust murder of the man.
When the king returned from the region of Cilicia, the Jews in the city appealed to him with regard to the unreasonable murder of Onias, and the Greeks shared their feelings.
Antiochus was grieved at heart and filled with pity, and wept because of the moderation and good conduct of the deceased.

Inflamed with anger, he immediately stripped off the purple robe of Andronicus, tore off his garments, and led him about the whole city to that very place where he had committed the outrage against Onias, and there he slew the bloodthirsty fellow. The Lord thus repaid him with the punishment he deserved. Note: even the pagan tyrant Antiochus showed some justice in punishing the murderer of Onias. God uses even wicked instruments to execute justice. # GEM Augustine: God is so omnipotent that He can bring good even out of evil, and justice even through the unjust.

Now many acts of sacrilege had been committed in the city by Lysimachus, with the connivance of Menelaus, and when word of them got about, the populace gathered against Lysimachus, because many of the gold vessels had already been disposed of.

Since the crowds were becoming aroused and filled with anger, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men and launched an unjust attack, under the leadership of a certain Auranus, a man advanced in years and no less advanced in folly.

When the Jews became aware of Lysimachus's attack, some picked up stones, some blocks of wood, and others took handfuls of the ashes that were lying about, and threw them in wild confusion at Lysimachus and his men.

As a result, they wounded many of them, and killed some, and put all the rest to flight; and the temple robber himself they killed close by the treasury. Note: God protected His temple and the people defending it even by seemingly inadequate means — ashes and stones sufficed to defeat an army. This shows divine assistance to those who fight for sacred things.
Charges were brought against Menelaus about this incident.
When the king came to Tyre, three men sent by the senate of Jerusalem presented the just cause before him.
But Menelaus, already as good as beaten, promised a substantial bribe to Ptolemy son of Dorymenes to win over the king.
Therefore Ptolemy, taking the king aside into a colonnade as if to get some fresh air, induced the king to change his mind.

Menelaus, the cause of all this evil, he acquitted of the charges against him, while he sentenced to death those poor men who, if they had pleaded even before Scythians, would have been discharged innocent. Note the terrible perversion of justice: the guilty man Menelaus went free through bribery while innocent men were condemned. This is what corruption of judges produces. # GEM Chrysostom: when money speaks, justice is silent; the court becomes a market and the judge a merchant.
So those who had spoken for the city and the villages and the holy vessels quickly suffered the unjust penalty.
Therefore even some Tyrians, vexed at the outrage, provided magnificently for their burial.

But Menelaus, because of the greediness of those in power, remained in office, growing in wickedness, having become the chief plotter against his fellow citizens. Note this moral conclusion to the chapter: wickedness protected by powerful patrons grows ever bolder. The corrupt official, shielded by bribes, becomes ever more dangerous to the innocent. This is the fruit of simony and ambition: the destruction of both the sacred office and the common good.
Antiochus sent an Athenian senator to compel the Jews to forsake the laws of their fathers and to cease living by the laws of God.

He was also commissioned to pollute the temple in Jerusalem and to call it the temple of Jupiter Olympius, and to call the one in Gerizim the temple of Jupiter the Hospitable, as the inhabitants of the place requested.
The onset of this evil was harsh and utterly grievous.
For the Gentiles filled the temple with debauchery and revelry; they amused themselves with prostitutes and lay with women in the sacred precincts, and besides brought in things that were not lawful.
The altar was covered with abominable offerings that were forbidden by the laws.
A man could neither keep the sabbath nor observe the feasts of his fathers, nor so much as confess himself to be a Jew.

On the monthly celebration of the king's birthday, the Jews were taken, under bitter constraint, to partake of the sacrifices; and when a festival of Dionysus was celebrated, they were compelled to march in the procession in honor of Dionysus, wearing wreaths of ivy.
At the suggestion of Ptolemy a decree was issued to the neighboring Greek cities, that they should adopt the same policy toward the Jews and make them partake of the sacrifices.
Those who refused to change to Greek customs were to be put to death.

Two women were brought in for having circumcised their children; these women they paraded publicly around the city, with their babies hung at their breasts, then hurled them down headlong from the wall.

Others who had assembled in the caves nearby, in order to observe the seventh day secretly, were betrayed to Philip and were all burned together, because their piety kept them from defending themselves, in view of their regard for that most holy day.
Now I urge those who read this book not to be depressed by such calamities, but to recognize that these punishments were designed not to destroy but to discipline our people.
In fact, not to let the impious alone for long, but to punish them immediately, is a sign of great kindness.
For in the case of the other nations the Lord waits patiently to punish them until they have reached the full measure of their sins; but he does not deal in this way with us.
In order that he may not take vengeance on us afterward when our sins have reached their height, he never withdraws his mercy from us.
Although he disciplines us with calamities, he does not forsake his own people.
Let what we have said serve as a reminder; we must go on briefly with the story.
Eleazar, one of the scribes in high position, a man now advanced in age and of noble presence, was being forced to open his mouth to eat swine's flesh.
But he, welcoming death with honor rather than life with pollution, went up to the rack of his own accord, spitting out the flesh.
As men ought to do who have the courage to refuse things that it is not right to taste, even for the natural love of life.

Those who were in charge of that unlawful sacrifice took the man aside, because of their long acquaintance with him, and privately urged him to bring meat of his own providing, proper for him to use, and pretend that he was eating the flesh of the sacrificial meal which had been commanded by the king.
By doing this he would be saved from death, and be treated kindly on account of his old friendship with them.

But making a high resolve, worthy of his years and the dignity of his old age and the gray hairs which he had reached with such distinction and his excellent life even from childhood, and moreover according to the holy God-given law, he declared himself quickly, telling them to send him to Hades.
Such pretense is not worthy of our time of life, he said, lest many of the young should suppose that Eleazar in his ninetieth year has gone over to an alien religion.
And so by my pretense, for the sake of living a brief moment longer, they would be led astray because of me, while I defile and disgrace my old age.
For even if for the present I should avoid the punishment of men, yet whether I live or die I shall not escape the hands of the Almighty.
Therefore, by manfully giving up my life now, I will show myself worthy of my old age.
And I will leave to the young a noble example of how to die a good death willingly and nobly for the revered and holy laws.
When he had said this, he went at once to the rack.
And those who a little before had acted toward him with good will now changed to ill will, because the words he had uttered were in their opinion sheer madness.

When he was about to die under the blows, he groaned aloud and said: It is clear to the Lord in his holy knowledge that, though I might have been saved from death, I am enduring terrible sufferings in my body under this beating, but in my soul I am glad to suffer these things because I fear him. # GEM Eleazar's interior witness before God—distinguishing bodily pain from spiritual gladness—is the mark of true martyrdom: the soul consents freely to what the body endures against its will, and this free consent is itself the act of worship.
So in this way he died, leaving in his death an example of nobility and a memorial of courage, not only to the young but to the great body of his nation.
Simon, who had informed about the money against his own country, slandered Onias as if he had been the one who stirred up Heliodorus against the treasury and was the instigator of evils.
But Onias, who had both defended his country and shown zeal for the law, he accused of plotting against the king's affairs.
But the enmity went so far that murders were committed by one of Simon's approved men.
Onias, seeing the danger of the contention and that Apollonius the son of Menestheus, the governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, was furious and increasing Simon's malice, went to the king.
Not to be an accuser of citizens, but looking to the common and private good of all the people.
For he saw that without the king's providence, it was impossible for the state to have peace, and for Simon to cease from his folly.
But when Seleucus departed this life, and Antiochus who was called Epiphanes succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means.
Promising the king three hundred and sixty talents of silver and eighty talents from other revenues.
Besides this he promised a hundred and fifty more, if power were given him to set him up a place for exercise and a body of youth for it and to entitle them Antiochians in Jerusalem.
The king agreed to this; and as soon as he had gotten the authority into his hands, Jason immediately made his countrymen change over to the Greek fashion.

And abolishing those royal grants to the Jews, obtained through John the father of Eupolemus, who went on the embassy to the Romans for friendship and alliance, he set aside the lawful ways of living and introduced customs contrary to the law.
For he very readily established a gymnasium right under the citadel itself, and he caused the noblest of the young men to wear the Greek hat.
Now such was the height of Greek fashion and increase of heathenish manners through the exceeding profaneness of Jason, that ungodly wretch and no high priest.

That the priests were no longer eager about the services of the altar, but despising the sanctuary and neglecting the sacrifices, they hastened to partake of the unlawful allowance in the place of exercise after the discus throwing was called.
And thinking nothing of the honors of their fathers, they esteemed the Hellenic glories best.
For which cause heavy affliction came upon them, and they whose manner of life they so earnestly followed and whom they desired to be like in all things became their enemies and punished them.
For it is not a light thing to do wickedly against the laws of God, but the time following shall declare these things.
Now when the games that are held every fifth year were kept at Tyre, and the king was present.

The wicked Jason sent special messengers from Jerusalem, who were Antiochians, carrying three hundred drachms of silver for the sacrifice of Hercules; which money even they that brought it thought fit not to bestow on the sacrifice, because that was not right, but to bestow it on other charges.
So this money was appointed by him that sent it for the sacrifice of Hercules, but by the bearers of it was transferred to the building of galleys.

Now when Apollonius the son of Menestheus was sent into Egypt for the first access of Philometor the king, Antiochus understanding him not to be well affected to his affairs, provided for his own security; whereupon he came to Joppa and from thence to Jerusalem.
Where he was honorably received of Jason and of the city, and was brought in with torch lights and with great shoutings, and so afterward went with his host into Phoenicia.
Now after the space of three years Jason sent Menelaus, the aforesaid Simon's brother, to bear the money unto the king, and to put him in mind of certain necessary matters.

But he being brought to the presence of the king, when he had magnified him for the glorious appearance of his power, got the priesthood for himself, outbidding Jason by three hundred talents of silver.
And having received the king's mandate he came to Jerusalem, bringing nothing worthy the high priesthood, but having the fury of a cruel tyrant and the rage of a savage beast.
Then Jason who had undermined his own brother was himself undermined by another and driven as a fugitive into the country of the Ammonites.
So Menelaus got the principality, but as for the money that he had promised to the king, he took no good order for it, albeit Sostratus the ruler of the castle required it.
For unto him appertained the gathering of the customs. For which cause they were both called before the king.
Now Menelaus left his brother Lysimachus in his stead in the priesthood, and Sostratus left Crates, who was governor of the Cyprians.
While those things were in doing, they of Tarsus and Mallos made insurrection, because they were given to the king's concubine, called Antiochis.
Then came the king in all haste to appease matters, leaving Andronicus, a man in authority, for his deputy.
Now Menelaus, supposing that he had gotten a convenient time, stole certain vessels of gold out of the temple and gave some of them to Andronicus and sold some to Tyre and the cities round about.
Which when Onias knew of a surety, he reproved him, having withdrawn himself into a sanctuary at Daphne, that lieth by Antioch.

Wherefore Menelaus, taking Andronicus apart, prayed him to get Onias into his hands; who being persuaded thereunto and coming to Onias in deceit, gave him his right hand with oaths of fidelity, and though he was suspected by him, yet persuaded him to come forth from the sanctuary; whom forthwith he shut up without any regard for justice.
For the which cause not only the Jews, but many also of other nations took great indignation and displeasure at the unjust murder of the man.
And when the king was come back again from the places about Cilicia, the Jews that were in the city and certain of the Greeks that abhorred the fact also informed him of Onias's murder.
Wherefore Antiochus was heartily sorry and moved to pity, and wept, because of the sober and modest behavior of him that was dead.

And being inflamed with anger, he took away Andronicus his purple and tore off his garments, and leading him through the whole city unto that very place where he had committed impiety against Onias, there slew he the murderer of the murderer. Thus the Lord rewarded him his punishment, as he had deserved.

Now when many sacrileges had been committed in the city by Lysimachus with the consent of Menelaus, and the report thereof was spread abroad, the multitude gathered themselves together against Lysimachus, many vessels of gold being already dispersed.

Whereupon the common people rose up and, being filled with rage, Lysimachus armed about three thousand men and began first to offer violence; one Auranus being the leader, a man far gone in years and no less in folly.

But when they perceived the attempt of Lysimachus, some of them caught up stones, others pieces of wood, some again took handfuls of the dust that lay at hand and threw them all together pell-mell upon Lysimachus and those that set upon them.
By this means many of them were wounded and some slain, and Lysimachus himself was put to flight and died hard by the treasury.
Of this matter therefore there was an accusation laid against Menelaus.
Now when the king came to Tyre, three men were sent from the senate to plead the cause before him.
But Menelaus, being now convicted, promised Ptolemy the son of Dorymenes to give him much money if he would pacify the king.
Whereupon Ptolemy taking the king aside into a certain gallery as though he would take the air, brought him to be of another mind.

Insomuch that he discharged Menelaus from the accusations, who notwithstanding was cause of all the mischief; and those poor men, who, if they had told their cause even before the Scythians, should have been judged innocent, them he condemned to death.
Thus they that followed the matter for the city and for the people and for the holy vessels did soon suffer unjust punishment.
Wherefore even the Tyrians, moved with hatred of that wicked deed, caused them to be honorably buried.
And so through the covetousness of them that were in power, Menelaus remained still in authority, increasing in malice and being a great traitor to the citizens.

When Antiochus made his second expedition into Egypt, it came to pass that throughout the city of Jerusalem for almost forty days there appeared horsemen running through the air in golden raiment, and armed troops, and squadrons of cavalry in array, and encounters and pursuits of both sides, and movements of shields, and a multitude of spears, and casting of arrows, and a glittering of golden ornaments, and armor of all sorts.

Wherefore every man prayed that the apparition might turn out for good. # GEM — The ancients held that divine powers had taken up arms against enemies; vexilla and arms of commanders were customarily blessed; standard-bearers in war were formerly called Draconarii.

Now when there was gone out a false rumor, as though Antiochus had been dead, Jason took at the least a thousand men, and suddenly made an assault upon the city; and they that were upon the walls being put to flight, and the city being now at length taken, Menelaus fled into the castle.

But Jason slew his own citizens without mercy, not considering that to get the day of them of his own nation would be a most unhappy day for him; but thinking them to be his enemies, and not his countrymen, whom he conquered.
Howbeit he got not the principality, but at the last received shame for the reward of his treachery, and fled again into the country of the Ammonites.

In the end therefore he had an unhappy return, being accused before Aretas the king of the Arabians, fleeing from city to city, pursued of all men, hated as a forsaker of the laws, and being had in abomination as an open enemy of his country and countrymen, he was cast out into Egypt.
Thus he that had driven many out of their country perished in a strange land, retiring to the Lacedaemonians, and thinking to find succor by reason of his kinship with them.

And he that had cast out many, unburied, had none to mourn for him, nor any solemn funerals at all, nor sepulchre with his fathers. # GEM — The vengeance of God is swift upon the treacherous, as shown by the example of Jason the pseudo-pontiff; vengeance recoils upon its own author.
Now when this that was done came to the king's ear, he thought that Judea had revolted: whereupon removing out of Egypt in a furious mind, he took the city by force of arms.
And commanded his men of war not to spare such as they met, and to slay such as went up upon the houses.
Thus there was killing of young and old, making away of men, women, and children, slaying of virgins and infants.
And there were destroyed within the space of three whole days fourscore thousand, whereof forty thousand were slain in the conflict; and no fewer sold than slain.
Yet was he not satisfied herewith, but presumed to go into the most holy temple of all the world, Menelaus, that traitor to the laws and to his own country, being his guide.

And taking the holy vessels with polluted hands, and with profane hands pulling down the things that were dedicated by other kings to the augmentation and glory and honor of the place, he gave them away.
And Antiochus was lifted up in mind, not considering that the Lord was angry for a little while for the sins of them that dwelt in the city, and therefore his eye was not upon the place.

For had they not been formerly wrapped in many sins, this man, as soon as he had come, had forthwith been scourged and put back from his presumption, as Heliodorus was, who was sent under king Seleucus to view the treasury. # GEM — God permits the holy place to be defiled on account of the sins of the people; the Lord withdraws His protecting eye when sin abounds.
But God did not choose the people for the place's sake, but the place for the people's sake.

And therefore the place itself that was made partaker with them in the adversities that happened unto the people, did afterward communicate in the benefits sent from the Lord: and as it was forsaken in the wrath of the Almighty, so again, the great Lord being reconciled, it was set up with all glory.

So Antiochus, having carried out of the temple a thousand and eight hundred talents, departed in all haste unto Antioch, thinking in his arrogancy to make the land navigable, and the sea passable by foot: such was the haughtiness of his mind.
And he left governors to vex the nation: at Jerusalem, Philip, for his country a Phrygian, and for his manners more barbarous than he that set him there;
And at Garizim, Andronicus; and besides, Menelaus, who worse than all the rest bare an heavy hand over the citizens, having a malicious mind against his countrymen the Jews.
He sent also that detestable ringleader Apollonius with an army of two and twenty thousand, commanding him to slay all those that were in their best age, and to sell the women and the younger sort.
Who coming to Jerusalem, and pretending peace, did forbear till the holy day of the sabbath, when taking the Jews keeping holy day, he commanded his men to arm themselves.
And so he slew all them that were gone to the celebrating of the sabbath, and running through the city with weapons, slew great multitudes.

But Judas Maccabeus with nine others, or thereabout, withdrew himself into the wilderness, and lived in the mountains after the manner of beasts, with his company, who fed on herbs continually, lest they should be partakers of the pollution. # GEM — Judas Maccabeus prefigures Christ withdrawing into the desert; the faithful must flee defilement even at great personal cost rather than share in impiety.

[On Antiochus's second expedition against Egypt] Antiochus, returning from Egypt, attacked Jerusalem with great ferocity, as one inflamed with rage. # GEM Chrysostom notes that tyrants rage most fiercely when they feel their power threatened, as a wounded beast is most dangerous.

The heavenly visions of cavalry troops and armed men appearing in the sky over Jerusalem were a divine warning of the coming tribulation, as God sometimes reveals future calamities through prodigies to call sinners to repentance.
These aerial battles and cavalry charges seen in the sky prefigured the actual battles that were about to occur on earth, God thus mercifully forewarning His people.
The prayer of the people that these prodigies might turn to good omen shows that even amid tribulation the faithful should turn to prayer rather than despair.
Jason's treacherous attack on Jerusalem, slaughtering his own citizens, shows how ambition and lust for power destroy all bonds of nature, piety, and humanity.
Jason sinned gravely in thinking his fellow citizens enemies rather than friends; thus do ambition and faction blind men so that they slay their own brethren.
Jason, having obtained nothing by his rashness, was driven back into exile; thus does God confound the counsels of the wicked and turn their plots back upon themselves.
Jason died miserably in exile, unwept and unburied, a just punishment for his treachery; for as he had cast others into exile and death, so he himself perished by the same measure.
He who had cast many from their homeland into exile was himself cast out unburied, showing the divine justice that repays men in the same coin in which they have dealt with others.
Jason received no burial rites — he who had deprived so many of burial; thus divine Providence arranges that punishments correspond to crimes in a kind of retributive symmetry. # GEM
Antiochus, hearing of Jason's revolt, supposed all Judea had rebelled and marched against Jerusalem in fury; thus the sins of the few bring calamity upon the many, a lesson for rulers of communities.
Antiochus commanded his soldiers to cut down all they met and to spare none; such is the cruelty that ambition and rage produce in tyrants who have no fear of God.

The slaughter of eighty thousand souls — men, women, and children — shows what the wrath of a tyrant, unchecked by law or piety, can accomplish; and how grievously God sometimes punishes sinful peoples through foreign enemies.
As many were sold into slavery as were slain; thus war brings a double misery — death for some, servitude for others, both evils being divine punishments for the sins of the people.
Antiochus daring to enter the most holy Temple was the height of sacrilege and impiety; yet God permitted it to punish the sins of Israel and to test the faith of the just.

Antiochus laid his impious hands upon the sacred vessels; God permitted this as He had permitted Nebuchadnezzar, to punish His people, but He would afterwards exact most severe vengeance from the sacrilegious plunderer.
The author notes that God allowed this profanation because of the sins of Jerusalem's inhabitants; divine chastisements always have their cause in the sins of those who suffer them. # GEM
Had the people not sinned, Antiochus would have been scourged and turned back as was Heliodorus; thus holiness of the people is the best defense of sacred places, and sin exposes them to profanation.

God chose the Temple for the sake of the people, not the people for the sake of the Temple; therefore when the people sin, God withdraws His protection from the Temple also, showing that He values souls above buildings. # GEM

The Temple shared in the calamities of the people when they sinned, and shared in their glory when they flourished; thus sacred places reflect the spiritual condition of the faithful who worship there.

Antiochus departed from Jerusalem carrying away eighteen hundred talents from the Temple treasury, elated in spirit and arrogant of heart; but such pride goes before destruction, as subsequent events proved.

Antiochus left governors to oppress the Jewish people — Philip, a Phrygian by race, more barbarous in character than his master; Andronicus over Gerizim. Thus tyrants appoint ministers worse than themselves.
These governors afflicted the Jews with continual harassments, showing that when God withdraws His protection from a sinful people, enemies multiply and pile oppression upon oppression.

Apollonius was sent with twenty-two thousand soldiers against Jerusalem, commanded to slay all men of age and to sell women and youth into slavery — a command of singular cruelty surpassing even Antiochus himself.

Apollonius came to Jerusalem with peaceful pretense, then on the Sabbath day attacked, knowing the Jews would not fight on the Sabbath; thus the wicked use feigned peace to accomplish what open force cannot. # GEM
Apollonius slew those who came forth to observe the Sabbath; this martyrdom of those who died rather than profane the Sabbath was most pleasing to God, who repaid their fidelity with eternal glory.

Judas Maccabeus with nine companions fled to the desert and lived on herbs rather than be defiled; thus the courageous soul prefers poverty and hardship to sin and apostasy. This flight of Judas prefigures the flight of Christ into Egypt and the withdrawal of the saints from the corruption of the world. # GEM
Courtiers should withdraw from court in good time before a fall, ibid. c. 7. v. 10.
Soft and lax upbringing makes children insolent, 2 Mach. c. 12. v. 14.

ON THE NATIVITY OF THE LORD. "And behold an Angel of the Lord stood by them." The wondrous condescension of Angels toward the human race, Tob. c. 3. v. 25. They are creatures and as it were sons of God, ibid. c. 5. v. 7. and 18. They are our fellow-citizens and kinsmen, ibid. c. 7. v. 3. They stand before God in sevenfold order, and are distinguished into those who assist and those who minister, c. 12. v. 15. # GEM

FEAR NOT; FOR BEHOLD I BRING YOU TIDINGS OF GREAT JOY. A sign of a good Angel appearing is that first horror is struck into one, then consolation and joy are brought; of an evil angel, if the contrary occurs, Tob. c. 12. v. 17. See the article on Angels.
He was the true light. Christ, through whom light and truth were made, prefigured by the High Priest in his pontifical vestments, in which were the Urim and Thummim, clothed, Esdras c. 2. v. 63.

The Word was made flesh. Christ was sent by the Father into the flesh, that he might comfort those who mourn in Sion. Nehemiah, p. 802. 2. The Incarnation of the Word was the greatest work of divine power and strength, Judith c. 13. v. 20.

ON THE FEAST OF THE CIRCUMCISION OF THE LORD. "His name was called." Names are as it were omens signifying some present or future nature, virtue, or property of a thing, Esdras c. 1. v. 8. God, in giving a name to someone, at the same time gives the reality signified by that same name, as in the name.
Jesus: Jesus is honey in the mouth, melody in the ear, jubilation in the heart. See Esther 14:18. See notes in the Index under "Jesus Christ."

FOR THE FEAST OF ST. STEPHEN: "Behold, your house shall be left unto you desolate" — Tobit the elder prophesied concerning the destruction of Jerusalem to be carried out by Nebuchadnezzar, Tobit 13:11.

FOR THE FEAST OF ST. JOHN THE APOSTLE: "Who also reclined at supper upon His breast" — How St. John at the Last Supper of Christ reclined upon His breast as if in His bosom is clear from the form of reclining at table, by which guests of old...
The Jews in Jerusalem and Judea send greetings to their brethren in Egypt, wishing them peace and good things.
May God be gracious to you and remember his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, his faithful servants.
May he give you all a heart to worship him and to do his will with a strong heart and a willing spirit.
May he open your heart to his law and his commandments and bring you peace.
May he hear your prayers and be reconciled to you, and not forsake you in time of evil.
We are now praying for you here.

In the reign of Demetrius, in the one hundred and sixty-ninth year, we Jews wrote to you in the critical distress that came upon us in those years after Jason and his company revolted from the holy land and the kingdom.
They burned the gate and shed innocent blood; then we prayed to the Lord and were heard, and we offered sacrifice and cereal offering, and we lit the lamps and set out the loaves.
And now see that you keep the feast of booths in the month of Chislev, in the one hundred and eighty-eighth year.

Those in Jerusalem and those in Judea and the senate and Judas, to Aristobulus, who is of the family of the anointed priests, teacher of King Ptolemy, and to the Jews in Egypt, greeting and good health.
Having been saved by God out of grave dangers we thank him greatly for taking our side against the king.
For he drove out those who fought against the holy city.
For when the leader reached Persia with a force that seemed irresistible, they were cut to pieces in the temple of Nanea by a deception employed by the priests of the goddess Nanea.
For under pretext of intending to marry her, Antiochus came to the place together with his friends, to secure most of its treasures as a dowry.
When the priests of the temple of Nanea had set out the treasures and Antiochus had come with a few men inside the wall of the sacred precinct, they closed the temple as soon as he entered it.
Opening a secret door in the ceiling, they threw stones and struck down the leader and his men, and dismembered them and cut off their heads and threw them to the people outside.
Blessed in every way be our God, who has delivered up the ungodly.

Since we are about to celebrate the purification of the temple on the twenty-fifth day of the month Chislev, we thought it necessary to notify you, in order that you also may celebrate the feast of booths and the feast of the fire given when Nehemiah offered sacrifices after rebuilding the temple and the altar.

For when our fathers were being led captive to Persia, the pious priests of that time took some of the fire of the altar and secretly hid it in the hollow of a dry cistern, where they kept it safe so that the place was unknown to anyone.

But after many years had passed, when it pleased God, Nehemiah, having been commissioned by the king of Persia, sent the descendants of the priests who had hidden the fire to get it. And when they reported to us that they had not found fire but only thick liquid, he ordered them to draw it out and bring it.
When the materials for the sacrifices were presented, Nehemiah ordered the priests to sprinkle the liquid on the wood and on the things laid upon it.
When this was done and some time had passed, and when the sun, which had been clouded over, shone out, a great fire blazed up, so that all marveled.
And while the sacrifice was being consumed, the priests offered prayer — the priests and everyone. Jonathan led, and the rest responded, as did Nehemiah.
The prayer was to this effect: O Lord, Lord God, Creator of all things, who art awe-inspiring and strong and just and merciful, who alone art King and art kind.
Who alone art bountiful, who alone art just and almighty and eternal, who dost rescue Israel from every evil, who didst choose the fathers and consecrate them.
Accept this sacrifice on behalf of all thy people Israel and preserve thy portion and make it holy.
Gather together our scattered people, set free those who are slaves among the Gentiles, look upon those who are rejected and despised, and let the Gentiles know that thou art our God.
Punish those who oppress and are insolent with pride.
Plant thy people in thy holy place, as Moses said.
Then the priests sang the hymns.
And when the materials of the sacrifice were consumed, Nehemiah ordered that the liquid that was left should be poured upon large stones.
When this was done, a flame blazed up; but when the light from the altar shone back, it went out.

When this matter became known, and it was reported to the king of the Persians that, in the place where the exiled priests had hidden the fire, the liquid had appeared with which Nehemiah and his associates had burned the materials of the sacrifice.
The king investigated the matter, and enclosed the place and made it sacred.
And with those persons whom the king favored he exchanged many excellent gifts.
Nehemiah and his associates called this naphtha, which means purification, but by most people it is called naphtha.

The high priesthood of Onias and the peace of Jerusalem: under his governance the holy city enjoyed complete peace and the laws were observed in the best possible manner, owing to the piety and hatred of evil of Onias the high priest.
Kings held the temple in the highest honor and glorified it with the finest gifts, so that even Seleucus king of Asia paid all the expenses of the sacrificial ministry from his own revenues.
But a certain Simon of the tribe of Benjamin, who had been appointed overseer of the temple, fell into a dispute with the high priest about the management of the market in the city.

When Simon could not prevail over Onias, he went to Apollonius son of Thraseas, who was then governor of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, and reported to him that the treasury in Jerusalem was full of untold sums of money, so that the amount of the funds could not be counted.

Apollonius met with the king and told him about the money that had been reported to him. The king chose Heliodorus, who was in charge of his affairs, and sent him with orders to effect the removal of the aforesaid money.
Heliodorus at once set out on his journey, ostensibly to make a tour of inspection of the cities of Coelesyria and Phoenicia, but in fact to carry out the king's purpose.

When he arrived at Jerusalem and had been courteously welcomed by the high priest of the city, he told him about the disclosure that had been made, and stated what the purpose of his visit was, and inquired whether this was really the situation.

The high priest explained that there were deposits belonging to widows and orphans, and also some property of Hyrcanus son of Tobias, a man of very prominent position, and that the total amounted to four hundred talents of silver and two hundred of gold. He declared that it was utterly impossible to wrong those who had trusted in the sanctity of the place and in the solemnity and inviolability of a temple venerated throughout the whole world.
But Heliodorus, because of the king's orders that he had, said that this money must in any case be confiscated for the royal treasury.
So on the day he had set, Heliodorus entered to make an inventory of the funds. There was no little distress throughout the whole city.

The priests prostrated themselves before the altar in their priestly vestments and called toward heaven upon him who had given the law about deposits, that he should keep them safe for those who had deposited them.
To see the appearance of the high priest was to be wounded at heart, for his face and the change in his color disclosed the anguish of his soul.
For terror and bodily trembling had come over the man, which plainly showed to those who looked at him the pain lodged in his heart.
People also hurried out of their houses in crowds to make a general supplication because the holy place was about to be brought into contempt.

Women, girded with sackcloth under their breasts, thronged the streets. Some of the young women who were kept indoors ran together to the gates, and some to the walls, while others peered out of the windows.
And holding up their hands to heaven, they all made supplication.
There was something pitiable in the prostration of the whole populace and the anxiety of the high priest in his great anguish.
While they were calling upon the Almighty Lord that he would keep what had been entrusted safe and secure for those who had entrusted it, Heliodorus went on with what had been decided.

But just as Heliodorus arrived at the treasury with his bodyguard, the Sovereign of spirits and of all authority caused so great a manifestation that all who had been so bold as to accompany him were astounded by the power of God, and became faint with terror.

For there appeared to them a magnificently caparisoned horse, with a rider of frightening mien; it rushed furiously at Heliodorus and struck at him with its front hoofs. Its rider was seen to have armor and weapons of gold.

Two young men also appeared to him, remarkably strong, gloriously beautiful and splendidly dressed, who stood on either side of him and flogged him continuously, inflicting many blows on him. # GEM The apparition of the heavenly horse and its rider signifies that God himself fights for his temple and for those who trust in him; the two angels represent the twofold assistance of divine providence, both restraining evil and actively punishing the wicked.

When he suddenly fell to the ground and deep darkness came over him, his men took him up, put him on a stretcher, and carried him away—this man who had just entered the aforesaid treasury with a great retinue and his whole bodyguard but was now unable to help himself. They recognized clearly the sovereign power of God.

While he lay prostrate, speechless because of the divine intervention and deprived of any hope of recovery, they praised the Lord who had acted marvelously for his own place. And the temple, which a little while before was full of fear and disturbance, was filled with joy and gladness, now that the Almighty Lord had appeared.
Some of Heliodorus's friends quickly begged Onias to call upon the Most High to grant life to one who was lying quite at his last breath.
So the high priest, fearing that the king might get the notion that some foul play had been perpetrated by the Jews with regard to Heliodorus, offered sacrifice for the man's recovery.

While the high priest was making an atonement, the same young men appeared again to Heliodorus dressed in the same clothing, and they stood and said, "Be very grateful to the high priest Onias, since for his sake the Lord has granted you your life."
And see that you, who have been flogged, report to all people the majestic power of God. Having said this they vanished.
Then Heliodorus offered sacrifice to the Lord and made very great vows to the savior of his life, and having bidden Onias farewell, he marched off with his forces to the king.
He bore testimony to all concerning the deeds of the supreme God, which he had seen with his own eyes.
When Heliodorus appeared before the king, he was asked by him what sort of person would be suitable to send on another mission to Jerusalem. He replied,

If you have any enemy or plotter against your government, send him there, for you will get him back thoroughly flogged, if he survives at all; for there is certainly some power of God about that place. # GEM Lapide notes here a remarkable pagan confession: even this worldly official, sent to plunder the sanctuary, was compelled by the evidence of his own body to become a witness to divine omnipotence—thus God turns his enemies into heralds of his glory.
For he who has his dwelling in heaven watches over that place and brings it aid, and he strikes and destroys those who come to do it injury.
This was the outcome of the episode of Heliodorus and the protection of the treasury.
